blocks:
Ayahs

سُورَةٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهَا وَفَرَضْنَـٰهَا وَأَنزَلْنَا فِيهَآ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (١)
[This is] a surah which We have sent down and prescribed, and We have sent down in it manifest signs so that you may take admonition. (1)
ٱلزَّانِيَةُ وَٱلزَّانِى فَٱجْلِدُوا۟ كُلَّ وَٰحِدٍ مِّنْهُمَا مِا۟ئَةَ جَلْدَةٍ ۖ وَلَا تَأْخُذْكُم بِهِمَا رَأْفَةٌ فِى دِينِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۖ وَلْيَشْهَدْ عَذَابَهُمَا طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢)
As for the fornicatress and the fornicator, strike each of them a hundred lashes, and let not pity for them overcome you in Allah’s law, if you believe in Allah and the Last Day, and let their punishment be witnessed by a group of the faithful. (2)
ٱلزَّانِى لَا يَنكِحُ إِلَّا زَانِيَةً أَوْ مُشْرِكَةً وَٱلزَّانِيَةُ لَا يَنكِحُهَآ إِلَّا زَانٍ أَوْ مُشْرِكٌ ۚ وَحُرِّمَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٣)
The fornicator will not marry anyone but a fornicatress or an idolatress, and the fornicatress will be married by none except a fornicator or an idolater, and that is forbidden to the faithful. (3)

The Virtue of Surah "An-Nur"

In a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Whoever recites Surah An-Nur, Allah will grant him ten good deeds as a reward for every believing man and woman in the past and the future." (This refers to one who recites it and implements its contents in their life).

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Protect your wealth and your private parts by reciting Surah An-Nur, and protect your women with it; for whoever continues its recitation every day or every night, no one from his household will ever fall into an act of indecency until he dies." [^1] Paying attention to the content of the Surah—which rises to combat the factors of deviation from the path of chastity through various effective means—clarifies the main point of the above hadith as well as its practical meaning.

Content of Surah An-Nur

This Surah can truly be considered the Surah of purity, chastity, and the struggle against sexual impurities, as the major part of its instructions revolves around cleansing society from sexual pollutions through various methods. This objective is implemented in several stages:

The first stage is the statement of the severe punishment for the adulterous man and woman, which is presented with total decisiveness in the second verse of this Surah.

The second stage addresses the fact that executing this severe legal punishment (Hadd) is not a simple matter; according to Islamic judicial standards, it has heavy conditions. Regarding someone other than a husband and wife, "four witnesses" are required, and in the case of a husband and wife, the program of "Li'an" (mutual imprecation)—the details of which will follow—must be executed. Furthermore, if someone accuses another but cannot prove their claim in an Islamic court of law, they themselves will face a severe punishment (four-fifths of the punishment for adultery), so that no one imagines they can easily drag others to Islamic punishment by accusing them; rather, they will conversely be caught in punishment themselves.

Then, on this occasion, the "famous Hadith of Ifk" and the slander directed at one of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله wives is discussed. The Quran pursues this matter intensely to make it completely clear what a heavy sin it is to spread rumors about pure individuals.

In the third stage, so it is not imagined that Islam contents itself only with the punishment of the sinner, it addresses one of the most important ways to prevent sexual impurity. It brings up the issue of prohibiting men from casting lustful glances at women and women at men, and the subject of the Hijab for Muslim women, discussing this in detail. This is because two major factors of sexual deviation are lustful glancing and the lack of Hijab, and until these are uprooted, impurities will not be eliminated.

In the fourth stage, again as an important prevention against falling into acts contrary to chastity, it issues the command for easy and simple marriage, so that it may combat illicit satisfaction of the sexual instinct through legitimate satisfaction.

In the fifth stage, it explains part of the etiquette of social interaction and the principles of child-rearing in relation to parents regarding this matter: that at specific times when it is likely that a husband and wife are in private, children should not enter their room without permission, so that grounds for the deviation of their thoughts are not provided through this path.

On this occasion, it mentions some other etiquettes of family life, even if they are not directly related to sexual issues.

In the sixth stage, which is interspersed within these discussions, it mentions part of the issues related to Monotheism (Tawhid), the Origin and the Resurrection, and submission to the command of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. This is because the backbone of all practical and moral programs is those very matters of belief and faith in the Origin, the Resurrection, and the truth of Prophethood; until this root exists, those branches, leaves, flowers, and fruits will not bloom.

Additionally, on the occasion of discussions related to faith and righteous deeds, mention is made of the global government of the righteous believers, and some other Islamic instructions are also pointed out, which altogether form a complete and comprehensive unit.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ سُورَةٌ أَنْزَلْناها وَ فَرَضْناها وَ أَنْزَلْنا فِيها آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (1) الزَّانِيَةُ وَ الزَّانِي فَاجْلِدُوا كُلَّ واحِدٍ مِنْهُما مِائَةَ جَلْدَةٍ وَ لا تَأْخُذْكُمْ بِهِما رَأْفَةٌ فِي دِينِ اللَّهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَ لْيَشْهَدْ عَذابَهُما طائِفَةٌ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (2) الزَّانِي لا يَنْكِحُ إِلاَّ زانِيَةً أَوْ مُشْرِكَةً وَ الزَّانِيَةُ لا يَنْكِحُها إِلاَّ زانٍ أَوْ مُشْرِكٌ وَ حُرِّمَ ذلِكَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (3)

Commentary

The Punishment for the Adulterer and Adulteress

We know the name of this Surah is Surah An-Nur because of the "Verse of Light" (Ayat al-Nur), which is one of the most striking verses of the Surah. However, beyond this, the content of the Surah also possesses a special radiance; it grants the light of chastity and purity to humans, families, men, and women. It gives the radiance of piety and truth to tongues and speech, and the light of Monotheism, the worship of God, faith in the Resurrection, and submission to the call of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to hearts and souls.

The first verse of this Surah is, in fact, a brief reference to the sum of the discussions in the Surah, saying: "[This is] a Surah which We have sent down and made mandatory, and in it We have sent down clear signs that perhaps you may remember." (سُورَةٌ أَنْزَلْناها وَ فَرَضْناها وَ أَنْزَلْنا فِيها آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ).

"Surah" comes from the root "Sur," meaning the height and loftiness of a building. Subsequently, the high walls that were built around cities in the past to protect against enemy attacks were called "Sur." Since these walls separated the city from the outside area, this word was gradually applied to a piece or section of something—including a section of the Quran that is separated from the rest.

Some linguists have also said that "Surah" refers to beautiful, high, and exalted buildings, and the different sections of a large building are also called "Surah." By this analogy, the different sections of the Quran that are separated from one another are called Surahs. [^1] In any case, this expression is an indication of the fact that all the laws and subjects of this Surah, whether beliefs, etiquettes, or instructions, are of extraordinary importance because they have all been sent down by Allah.

Specifically, the phrase "فَرَضْناها" (We made it mandatory), considering the meaning of "Fard" which implies "cutting" or "fixing," also emphasizes this meaning.

The expression "آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ" (clear signs) may refer to the truths of Monotheism, the Origin, the Resurrection, and Prophethood discussed therein, in contrast to "Faradnaha," which refers to the laws and instructions stated in this Surah. In other words, one refers to "beliefs" and the other to "laws."

There is also the possibility that "clear signs" refers to the proofs provided for the mandatory laws in this Surah.

The phrase "لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ" (that perhaps you may remember) once again visualizes the reality that the roots of all true beliefs and practical programs of Islam are embedded within human nature (Fitrah); based on this, explaining them is considered a type of "reminder."

[^1]: Lisan al-Arab, Vol. 4, under the root "Sur."

After this general statement, it proceeds to the first decisive and firm instruction regarding the adulterous woman and man, saying: "The woman and the man guilty of adultery or fornication—flog each of them with a hundred stripes" (الزَّانِيَةُ وَ الزَّانِي فَاجْلِدُوا كُلَّ واحِدٍ مِنْهُما مِائَةَ جَلْدَةٍ).

And for further emphasis, it adds: "And let not pity for them withhold you from [executing] the religion of Allah, if you believe in Allah and the Last Day" (وَ لا تَأْخُذْكُمْ بِهِما رَأْفَةٌ فِي دِينِ اللَّهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ).

Finally, at the end of this verse, it points to another note to complete the conclusion of this divine punishment, saying: "And let a group of the believers witness their punishment" (وَ لْيَشْهَدْ عَذابَهُما طائِفَةٌ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

In fact, this verse contains three instructions:

1- The ruling for the punishment of women and men tainted by indecency (Zina refers to sexual intercourse between a man and a woman who are not spouses and without Sharia authorization).

2- Emphasis that in executing this divine punishment, you should not be caught in misplaced affection and feelings—feelings and affection that result in nothing but the corruption and pollution of society. To neutralize the motives for such feelings, it brings up the issue of faith in Allah and the Last Day, because the sign of faith in the Origin and the Resurrection is absolute submission to His command. Faith in the All-Knowing, All-Wise Allah causes a person to know that every ruling has a philosophy and wisdom and was not legislated without reason, and faith in the Resurrection causes a person to feel responsible for violations.

Here, an interesting hadith (from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله) is narrated that is necessary to note: "On the Day of Resurrection, a ruler who reduced the divine punishment by one lash will be brought to the scene of gathering and asked: 'Why did you do that?' He will say: 'Out of mercy for Your servants!' The Lord will say to him: 'Were you more merciful to them than Me?!' And it will be ordered that he be cast into the Fire. Another will be brought who added one lash to the divine punishment; he will be asked: 'Why did you do that?' He will answer: 'So they would refrain from Your disobedience!' Allah will say: 'Were you more knowledgeable and wise than Me?!' Then it will be ordered that he too be taken to the fire of Hell!" [^1]

3- The instruction for a group of believers to be present at the scene of punishment. This is because the goal is not only for the sinner to take heed, but also for their punishment to serve as a lesson for others. In other words, given the fabric of human social life, moral pollutions do not remain fixed in one individual but spread to society. For cleansing, just as the sin was committed openly, the punishment must also be public.

In this way, the basis for the answer to the question of why Islam allows a person's reputation to be tarnished in public becomes clear. As long as the sin has not become public and has not been brought to the Islamic court, "Allah, the Veiler of Faults" is not pleased with tearing the veil. However, after the crime is proven, the secret is out, society is polluted, and the gravity of the sin is diminished, the punishment must be carried out in a way that neutralizes the negative effects of the sin and restores the gravity of the sin to its original state.

Fundamentally, in a healthy society, "violation of the law" must be regarded as significant. Certainly, if the violation is repeated, that significance is broken, and its restoration is only through the publicizing of the punishment of the violators.

One should also not lose sight of the fact that many people value their dignity and reputation more than the issue of physical punishment, and this publicizing of the penalty is a powerful brake on their rebellious whims.

Since the verse under discussion speaks of the punishment of the adulterous man and woman, a question arises: what is the ruling on legitimate marriage with such women? The third verse answers this question as such: "The man who commits adultery/fornication does not marry except an adulteress or a polytheist, just as the woman who commits adultery/fornication—none marries her except an adulterer or a polytheist" (الزَّانِي لا يَنْكِحُ إِلَّا زانِيَةً أَوْ مُشْرِكَةً وَ الزَّانِيَةُ لا يَنْكِحُها إِلَّا زانٍ أَوْ مُشْرِكٌ).

"And that has been forbidden to the believers" (وَ حُرِّمَ ذلِكَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

There is a discussion among commentators as to whether this verse is the statement of a divine ruling or a report of an external and natural occurrence.

Some believe the verse only states an objective reality: that the polluted always seek the polluted, and "birds of a feather flock together." Pure and faithful individuals never submit to such pollutions or choose polluted spouses, and they forbid it upon themselves. The evidence for this interpretation is the literal appearance of the verse, which is stated as a "declarative sentence."

However, another group believes that this sentence is the statement of a Sharia and divine ruling; specifically, it wants to prevent Muslims from marrying adulterous individuals, because moral diseases, like physical diseases, are often contagious. Furthermore, this act is considered a type of disgrace and shame for pure individuals. Additionally, children raised in such tainted or suspicious laps have an ambiguous destiny. For these reasons, Islam has prohibited this act. The evidence for this interpretation is the phrase "وَ حُرِّمَ ذلِكَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ" (and that has been forbidden to the believers), where the term "prohibition" (Tahrim) is used. Another piece of evidence is the numerous narrations that have reached us from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the other Infallible Imams عليهم السلام in this regard, interpreting it as a ruling.

Some great commentators have even written regarding the occasion of the verse's revelation: "A man from the Muslims asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله for permission to marry 'Umm Mahzul'—a woman who was famous for pollution in the Age of Ignorance and had even placed a flag at her door for identification! The above verse was revealed and answered them." [^1]

In another hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "This verse is about men and women who were tainted with adultery in the time of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله; Allah forbade the Muslims from marrying them. Even now, people are subject to this ruling: whoever becomes famous for this act and the divine punishment is carried out upon them, do not marry them until their repentance is proven." [^2]

It is also necessary to remind that many rulings are stated in the form of a "declarative sentence," and it is not necessary for divine rulings to always be in the form of "command" and "prohibition."

Additionally, it should be noted that the conjunction of "polytheists" with "adulterers" is actually to state the importance of the matter; that is, the sin of "adultery" is on par with the sin of "polytheism." This is because it has also been mentioned in some narrations that "the adulterer," at the moment he commits this act, is stripped of faith. (The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said: "...The adulterer does not commit adultery while he is a believer, and the thief does not steal while he is a believer; for when he does that, faith is stripped from him just as a shirt is stripped off.") [^1]

Key Points

1- Cases where the punishment for adultery is execution

What is mentioned in the above verse regarding the punishment for adultery is a general ruling that has exceptional cases. Among them is "adultery by a married person" (Muhsan and Muhsanah), the punishment for which, upon fulfillment of conditions, is execution. "Muhsan" refers to a man who has a wife and his wife is available to him, and "Muhsanah" refers to a woman who has a husband and her husband is with her. Whenever someone, despite having such a legitimate path, still commits adultery, their punishment is execution. The conditions and quality of executing this ruling are detailed in books of jurisprudence. Also, adultery with close relatives (incest) carries the punishment of execution. Similarly, rape (adultery by force and compulsion) carries the same punishment. Of course, in some cases, in addition to the issue of flogging, there is exile and certain other punishments, the details of which should be read in books of jurisprudence.

2- Why is the adulteress mentioned first?

Undoubtedly, this act contrary to chastity is ugly from anyone, but it is uglier and more repulsive from women, because they possess more modesty and shame, and breaking it is evidence of a more severe rebellion. Beyond this, although the grim consequences of this matter affect both, in the case of women, the grim consequences are greater. There is also the possibility that the source of temptation for this act often comes from their side, and in many cases, they are considered the primary factor. The sum of these aspects has caused the polluted woman to be mentioned before the polluted man in the above verse. However, chaste and faithful women and men are far removed from all these issues.

3- Why punishment in the presence of a group?

The above verse, which is in the form of a "command," conveys the obligation of the presence of a group of believers during the execution of the punishment for adultery. However, it goes without saying that the Quran did not stipulate that this ruling must be executed in public; rather, it varies according to conditions and interests. The presence of three people or more is sufficient; what matters is that the judge determines how many people's presence is necessary. [^1]

The philosophy of this ruling is also clear, because as we said, firstly, it is a lesson for everyone and a cause for cleansing society. Secondly, the shame of the criminal will prevent the commission of the crime in the future. Thirdly, whenever the execution of the punishment is done in the presence of a group, the judge and the executors of the punishment will not be accused of compromise, taking bribes, discrimination, or torture and the like. Fourthly, the presence of a crowd prevents autocracy and excess in the execution of the punishment. Fifthly, the criminal might, after the execution of the punishment, create rumors and accusations against the judge and the executor; the presence of the crowd clarifies his position and prevents his destructive activities in the future, along with other benefits.

4- What was the punishment for the adulterer previously?

From verses 15 and 16 of Surah An-Nisa, it appears that before the revelation of the ruling of Surah An-Nur regarding adulterers and immoral women, if they were married, their punishment was set as life imprisonment (فأمسكوهن في البيوت حتى يتوفاهن الموت) and if they were unmarried, their punishment was harm and annoyance (فآذوهما). However, the amount of this harm was not specified. But the verse under discussion limited and specified it to one hundred lashes. Therefore, the ruling of execution for the married person replaced life imprisonment, and the ruling of one hundred lashes became a limit and boundary for the ruling of harm. (For more explanation, refer to Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 306 onwards, under verses 15 and 16 of Surah An-Nisa).

5- Excess and negligence in executing the punishment are forbidden!

Undoubtedly, human and emotional issues require that maximum effort be made so that no innocent person is caught in punishment, and also that there be forgiveness and pardon as far as divine laws allow. However, after the crime is proven and the punishment is certain, decisiveness must be shown, and one must avoid false feelings and fake emotions that are harmful to the social order. Specifically, in the verse under discussion, the expression "فِي دِينِ اللَّهِ" (in the religion of Allah) is used, meaning when the ruling is the ruling of God, no one can precede the Compassionate and Merciful God. Here, the dominance of affectionate feelings is prohibited, because the majority of people have such a state and the probability of affectionate feelings dominating them is higher. However, it cannot be denied that a minority exists who favor more violence; this group also—as we previously indicated—deviates from the path of the divine ruling and must control their feelings and not precede God, which also carries a severe punishment.

6- Conditions for the prohibition of marriage with an adulterer and adulteress

We said the literal appearance of the above verses is the prohibition of marriage with an adulterer and adulteress. Of course, this ruling in Islamic narrations has been restricted to men and women who are famous for this act and have not repented. Therefore, if they are not famous for this act, or have withdrawn from their past actions and decided on purity and chastity, and have also practically shown the effect of their repentance, there is no Sharia obstacle to marrying them. In the second case, it is because the title "adulterer" and "adulteress" no longer applies to them; it was a state that has vanished. But in the first case, this condition is derived from Islamic narrations, and the occasion of the verse's revelation also confirms it.

In a reliable hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that the famous jurist "Zurarah" asked him: "What is the interpretation of the verse 'The adulterer does not marry except an adulteress...'?" The Imam said: "These are women famous for adultery and men famous for adultery, who have become notorious for adultery and are known by it. People today are in that position; whoever has the punishment for adultery carried out upon them, or becomes famous for adultery, it is not appropriate for anyone to marry them until their repentance is known from them." [^1] This theme has also appeared in other narrations.

7- The philosophy of prohibiting adultery

We do not think the grim consequences that affect the individual and society because of this act are hidden from anyone, but a brief explanation in this regard is necessary: The emergence of this ugly act and its spread undoubtedly breaks down the family system. It makes the relationship between child and father ambiguous and dark. It increases the number of children lacking identity in society, who, according to experience, turn into dangerous criminals. This disgraceful act is the cause of various conflicts and struggles among the lustful. Furthermore, the mental and venereal diseases that are among its grim effects are not hidden from anyone. Killing children, abortion, and crimes like them are among the grim effects of this act. (Read more in this regard in Volume 12 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 32 of Surah Al-Isra).

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan under the verse discussed and the book "Thawab al-A'mal" by Saduq (as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 568). [^1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Vol. 23, p. 148. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verse discussed; Qurtubi also narrated this hadith in his commentary under the same verse. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verse discussed. [^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 26 (Islamiyah Press, 1388 AH) as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 571. [^1]: A group of jurists have doubted the obligation of the presence of a group of believers during the execution of the punishment, while the literal appearance of the command is obligation, not recommendation. [^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 14, p. 335.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَأْتُوا۟ بِأَرْبَعَةِ شُهَدَآءَ فَٱجْلِدُوهُمْ ثَمَـٰنِينَ جَلْدَةً وَلَا تَقْبَلُوا۟ لَهُمْ شَهَـٰدَةً أَبَدًا ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٤)
As for those who accuse chaste women and do not bring four witnesses, strike them eighty lashes, and never accept any testimony from them after that, and they are transgressors, (4)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٥)
excepting those who repent after that and reform, for Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (5)

Commentary

The Punishment for Slander

Since the previous verses detailed severe punishments for adulterous men and women, there was a possibility that malicious and impious individuals might use this as a pretext to accuse chaste people. Therefore, immediately after stating the severe punishment for adulterers, the Quran describes the heavy penalty for slanderers who seek to abuse this law. This ensures that the dignity and sanctity of chaste families remain immune from the danger of such individuals, and that no one dares to assault the honor of the people.

First, it states: "Those who accuse chaste women of an act contrary to modesty must bring four (just) witnesses to prove this claim; and if they do not bring them, flog each of them with eighty lashes!" (وَ الَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ الْمُحْصَناتِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَأْتُوا بِأَرْبَعَةِ شُهَداءَ فَاجْلِدُوهُمْ ثَمانِينَ جَلْدَةً).

Following this severe physical punishment, it adds two other rulings:

"And never accept their testimony" (وَ لا تَقْبَلُوا لَهُمْ شَهادَةً أَبَداً).

"And they are the transgressors (Fasiqun)" (وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ).

In this way, not only are such individuals subjected to severe punishment, but in the long term, their word and testimony are stripped of value and credibility so they cannot further stain the reputation of the pure. Additionally, the brand of "fisq" (transgression) is placed upon their foreheads, disgracing them within society.

This strictness regarding the preservation of the dignity of chaste people is not limited to this instance; it is reflected in many Islamic teachings, all of which signify the extraordinary value Islam places on the honor of believing and chaste men and women.

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

اذا اتهم المؤمن اخاه انماث الايمان من قلبه كما ينماث الملح فى الماء:

"When a Muslim accuses his Muslim brother of something that is not in him, faith melts in his heart just as salt melts in water!" [^1]

However, since Islam never closes the door of return to anyone, but rather encourages the defiled at every opportunity to purify themselves and compensate for past mistakes, the next verse says: "Except those who repent thereafter and make amends and compensate, for Allah grants them His pardon and forgiveness; Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Merciful" (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ وَ أَصْلَحُوا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

There is a discussion among commentators and scholars as to whether this exception applies only to the phrase "أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ" (they are the transgressors) or if it also refers back to the phrase "وَ لا تَقْبَلُوا لَهُمْ شَهادَةً أَبَداً" (never accept their testimony). If it refers to both, the result is that through repentance, their testimony will be acceptable in the future, and the ruling of fisq (transgression) will be removed from them in all Islamic contexts and rulings.

However, if it refers only to the final phrase, the ruling of fisq will be removed regarding other matters, but their testimony will remain invalid until the end of their lives.

Of course, according to the accepted rules in "Usul al-Fiqh" (Principles of Jurisprudence), exceptions following two or more sentences apply to the last sentence unless there are indications that the preceding sentences are also included in the exception. Incidentally, such an indication exists in this context; for when the ruling of fisq is removed through repentance, there is no reason for testimony not to be accepted, as the rejection of testimony is due to fisq. One who has repented and re-acquired the quality of justice ('adalah) is no longer subject to that restriction.

Numerous narrations from the sources of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام also emphasize this meaning. To the extent that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, after explicitly stating the acceptance of the testimony of such individuals who have repented, asked the questioner: "What do the jurists among you say?"

The person replied: "They say: his repentance is accepted between him and Allah, but his testimony will never be accepted!" The Imam replied:

بئس ما قالوا كان ابى يقول اذا تاب و لم يعلم منه الاخير جازت شهادته:

"How evil is what they said! My father used to say: When he repents and nothing but good is seen from him, his testimony shall be accepted." [^1] Several other hadiths have been narrated on this subject; only one hadith opposes it, which can be interpreted as being issued under Taqiyyah (precautionary dissimulation).

It is also necessary to mention that the word "ابدا" (forever/never) in the phrase "لا تَقْبَلُوا لَهُمْ شَهادَةً أَبَداً" indicates the generality of the ruling, and we know that every general rule is subject to exception (especially a connected exception). Therefore, the notion that the expression "forever" prevents the effect of repentance is a pure mistake.

Key Points

1- What is the meaning of "Ramy" in the verse?

"Ramy" originally means throwing an arrow, a stone, or the like, which naturally causes injury in many cases. Subsequently, this word has been used metaphorically for accusing individuals, insulting them, and making false attributions, as if these words strike the person's body like an arrow and wound them.

Perhaps this is why in the verses under discussion, as well as future verses, this word is used in an absolute sense. For example, it does not say "those who accuse chaste women of adultery" (yarmoona al-muhsanati bi-zina), because the concept of adultery is understood within the meaning of "yarmoon," especially given the context. Furthermore, not explicitly mentioning it—especially when speaking of chaste women—is considered a form of respect, etiquette, and modesty in speech.

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 269, Chapter on Accusation and Ill-Suspicion. [^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 18, Book of Testimonies, Chapter 36, p. 282.

2- Why four witnesses?

We know that usually, to prove rights and crimes in Islam, two just witnesses are sufficient. Even in the case of homicide, the crime is proven with two just witnesses. However, in the case of an accusation of adultery, four witnesses are specifically mandatory. The weight of the witnesses here may be because many people's tongues are loose regarding such accusations, and they constantly wound the honor and dignity of individuals with or without suspicion. Islam has been strict in this regard to protect people's honor, whereas in other matters, even homicide, tongues are not as defiled to this extent.

Furthermore, homicide actually involves one side—meaning there is one criminal—whereas in the matter of adultery, the crime is proven for two people. If we required two witnesses for each, it would total four witnesses.

This is the essence of a hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, where Abu Hanifa, the famous Sunni jurist, says: I asked Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Is adultery more severe or murder?" He replied: "Murder." I said: "If so, why are two witnesses sufficient for murder, but four are required for adultery?"

He asked: "What do you say about this matter?" Abu Hanifa had no clear answer. The Imam said: "This is because in adultery there are two hudud (prescribed punishments): a punishment carried out on the man and a punishment on the woman; therefore, four witnesses are required. But in murder, only one punishment is carried out against the killer." [^1] Of course, there are cases where the punishment is carried out on only one party in adultery (such as rape and the like), but these are exceptional cases. What is common and customary is that which occurs with the consent of both parties, and we know that the philosophy of rulings always follows the majority of cases.

3- An important condition for the acceptance of repentance

We have stated many times that repentance is not merely seeking forgiveness or regret for the past, or even a decision to quit in the future; rather, in addition to all this, the sinner must seek to make amends.

If one has truly stained the reputation of a chaste man or woman, for their repentance to be accepted, they must retract their words in front of all those who heard the accusation from them and, so to speak, restore their reputation.

The phrase "وَ أَصْلَحُوا" (and make amends) after the mention of "تابُوا" (they repented) refers to this very fact: that such persons must repent of their sin and seek to rectify the corruption they have committed.

It is not right for a person to falsely accuse another in public (or through the press and media) and then seek forgiveness from God in the privacy of their home; Allah will never accept such a repentance.

Therefore, it is narrated in several hadiths from the leaders of Islam, in response to the question: "Will the testimony of those who make accusations against honor be accepted after the execution of the legal punishment and after repentance?" they replied: "Yes." And when asked how their repentance should be, they said: "He comes before the Imam (or judge) and says: 'I accused so-and-forth and I repent for what I said.'" [^1]

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 574. [^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 18, p. 283 (Chapters on Testimonies, Chapter 36, Hadith 4).

4- The rulings of Qadhf (Slander)

In the book of "Hudud," we have a chapter titled "The Punishment for Qadhf."

"Qadhf" literally means throwing toward a distant point, but in these contexts—like the word ramy—it is a metaphor for accusing someone of a sexual offense; in other words, it consists of insults and slurs related to these matters.

Whenever qadhf is performed with explicit terms, in any language and in any form, its punishment is eighty lashes as mentioned above. If it is not explicit, it falls under the ruling of "Ta'zir" (discretionary punishment). (Ta'zir refers to sins for which no specific punishment is mentioned in Sharia, but is left to the discretion of the judge, who decides the amount within a certain range based on the characteristics of the criminal, the nature of the crime, and other conditions).

Even if someone accuses a group with such charges and insults them, repeating this attribution for each individual, he is subject to the punishment of qadhf for each attribution. However, if he accuses them all at once in one instance, if they all demand his punishment together, he receives one punishment. But if they file lawsuits separately, he receives a separate punishment for each! This matter is so important that if someone is accused and then passes away, his heirs can file a lawsuit and demand the execution of the punishment. Of course, since this ruling pertains to a personal right, if the right-holder forgives the "criminal," the punishment is dropped, unless the crime is repeated so often that it endangers the dignity and honor of society, in which case it is treated differently.

Whenever two people exchange sexual insults with each other, the hadd (prescribed punishment) is dropped for both sides, but both are punished with ta'zir by the ruling of the Sharia judge. Therefore, no Muslim has the right to respond to an insult with a similar insult; rather, he can only seek justice through the Sharia judge and demand the punishment of the insulter.

In any case, the goal of this Islamic ruling is, firstly, to preserve the honor and dignity of human beings, and secondly, to prevent the numerous social and moral corruptions that afflict society through this path. For if corrupt individuals were free to hurl any insult or false attribution at anyone and remain immune from punishment, the dignity and honor of the people would constantly be at risk. It could even cause a husband to become suspicious of his wife due to these false accusations, or a father to doubt the legitimacy of his child! In short, the existence of the family would be endangered, and an environment of suspicion and pessimism would prevail over society; the market for rumor-mongers would flourish, and all chaste people would be stained in the minds of others.

This is where one must act with decisiveness—the same decisiveness that Islam has shown toward these foul-mouthed and dirty-tongued individuals.

Yes, they must taste eighty lashes as the penalty for a foul insult and accusation, so they do not treat the dignity and honor of the people as a plaything!

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ أَزْوَٰجَهُمْ وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُمْ شُهَدَآءُ إِلَّآ أَنفُسُهُمْ فَشَهَـٰدَةُ أَحَدِهِمْ أَرْبَعُ شَهَـٰدَٰتٍۭ بِٱللَّهِ ۙ إِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٦)
As for those who accuse their wives [of adultery], but have no witnesses except themselves, the testimony of such a man shall be a fourfold testimony [sworn] by Allah that he is indeed stating the truth, (6)
وَٱلْخَـٰمِسَةُ أَنَّ لَعْنَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ إِن كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٧)
and a fifth [oath] that Allah’s wrath shall be upon him if he were lying. (7)
وَيَدْرَؤُا۟ عَنْهَا ٱلْعَذَابَ أَن تَشْهَدَ أَرْبَعَ شَهَـٰدَٰتٍۭ بِٱللَّهِ ۙ إِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٨)
The punishment shall be averted from her by her testifying with four oaths [sworn] by Allah that he is indeed lying, (8)
وَٱلْخَـٰمِسَةَ أَنَّ غَضَبَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهَآ إِن كَانَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٩)
and a fifth [oath] that Allah’s wrath shall be upon her if he were stating the truth. (9)
وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ تَوَّابٌ حَكِيمٌ (١٠)
Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, and that Allah is all-clement, all-wise.... (10)

Circumstances of Revelation

Regarding the circumstances of the revelation of these verses, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that Sa'd ibn Ubadah (the leader of the Ansar) addressed the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the presence of a group of companions, saying: "O Prophet of Allah! If accusing someone of an unchaste act carries this punishment—that if one cannot prove it, he must receive eighty lashes—then what should I do if I enter my own house and see with my own eyes a wicked man committing a lewd act with my wife? If I leave to bring four witnesses to see and testify, he will have finished his act; if I intend to kill him, no one will accept my word without witnesses and I will be executed as a murderer in retaliation (Qisas); and if I come and report what I saw as a complaint, eighty lashes will be placed upon my back!"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله seemingly sensed a form of objection to this divine decree in these words. He turned toward the assembly of the Ansar and spoke in a tone of grievance: "Did you not hear what your leader said?"

They came forward to apologize and said: "O Messenger of Allah! Do not reproach him; he is a man of great honor (ghayrah), and what he says is due to the intensity of his protective honor."

Sa'd ibn Ubadah spoke up and said: "O Messenger of Allah! May my father and mother be sacrificed for you. By Allah, I know that this is a divine decree and it is the truth, but nevertheless, I am astonished by the nature of this situation (and I could not resolve this problem in my mind)." The Prophet said: "This is the decree of Allah." Sa'd then replied: "Allah and His Messenger have spoken the truth (صَدَقَ اللَّهُ وَ رَسُولُهُ‌)."

Not long after, his cousin named Hilal ibn Umayyah entered through the door, having seen a wicked man with his wife during the night. He came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to file a complaint and said explicitly: "I saw this matter with my own eyes and heard their voices with my own ears!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله became so distressed that the signs of discomfort were visible on his blessed face.

Hilal said: "I see the signs of distress on your face, but by Allah, I am telling the truth and there is no lie in my words. I hope that Allah Himself will open a way out of this problem."

In any case, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله decided to carry out the punishment for slander (Qadhf) upon Hilal because he had no witnesses for his claim.

At that moment, the Ansar were saying to one another: "See, the very scenario Sa'd ibn Ubadah mentioned has occurred. Will the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله truly lash Hilal and reject his testimony?"

At this point, revelation descended upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and its signs appeared on his face. Everyone fell silent to see what new message had come from Allah.

"The above verses were revealed" [1] and provided a precise solution to the Muslims, the details of which you will read below.

[1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Fi Zilal, Nur al-Thaqalayn, and al-Mizan under the verses in question (with minor variations).

Commentary

The Punishment for Accusing a Spouse!

As is evident from the circumstances of revelation, these verses serve as an exception and a proviso to the ruling on the punishment for slander (Qadhf). This means that if a husband accuses his wife of an unchaste act and says, "I saw her performing this illicit act with a stranger," the punishment for slander (eighty lashes) is not carried out against him. On the other hand, his claim without evidence or witnesses will not be accepted against the woman either, for he might be telling the truth or he might be lying.

Here, the Quran proposes a solution that resolves the issue in the best and most just manner.

That is, first, the husband must testify four times that he is truthful in this claim. As the Quran states: "And those who accuse their wives and have no witnesses except themselves, then the testimony of one of them shall be four testimonies by Allah that he is of the truthful" (وَ الَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ أَزْواجَهُمْ وَ لَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُمْ شُهَداءُ إِلَّا أَنْفُسُهُمْ فَشَهادَةُ أَحَدِهِمْ أَرْبَعُ شَهاداتٍ بِاللَّهِ إِنَّهُ لَمِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ‌).

"And the fifth time [he shall say] that the curse of Allah be upon him if he is of the liars" (وَ الْخامِسَةُ أَنَّ لَعْنَتَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ‌).

In this way, the husband, to prove his claim on one hand and to avert the punishment of slander on the other, repeats this sentence four times: "I bear witness by Allah that I am among the truthful in the accusation of adultery I have leveled against this woman" (اشهد باللَّه انى لمن الصادقين فيما رميتها به من الزنا).

And on the fifth time, he says: "The curse of Allah be upon me if I am among the liars" (لعنت اللَّه على ان كنت من الكاذبين).

At this point, the woman stands at a crossroads. If she confirms the man's words or refuses to negate this accusation in the manner described in the following verses, the punishment for adultery (Zina) is established against her.

However, "she can avert the punishment (of adultery) from herself by testifying four times by Allah that he is of the liars" (وَ يَدْرَؤُا عَنْهَا الْعَذابَ أَنْ تَشْهَدَ أَرْبَعَ شَهاداتٍ بِاللَّهِ إِنَّهُ لَمِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ‌).

"And the fifth time [she shall say] that the wrath of Allah be upon her if he is of the truthful" (وَ الْخامِسَةَ أَنَّ غَضَبَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْها إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ‌).

Thus, in response to the man's five testimonies regarding her impurity, the woman gives five testimonies negating this accusation. Four times with the phrase: "I bear witness by Allah that he is among the liars in the accusation of adultery he has leveled against me" (اشهد باللَّه انه لمن الكاذبين فيما رمانى به من الزنا).

And on the fifth time, she says: "The wrath of Allah be upon me if he is among the truthful" (ان غضب اللَّه على ان كان من الصادقين).

Performing this procedure, which in Islamic jurisprudence is called "Li'an" (mutual imprecation) due to the word "curse" (la'n) in the above phrases, will result in four definitive rulings for these two spouses:

First, they are immediately separated without the need for a divorce formula.

Second, this man and woman become forbidden to each other forever; that is, there is no possibility of them returning to a remarriage with one another.

Third, the punishment for slander is removed from the man, and the punishment for adultery is removed from the woman (however, if either of them refuses to perform this procedure, the punishment for slander is applied to the man, or the punishment for adultery to the woman).

Fourth, the child born from this incident is dissociated from the man, meaning the child will have no lineage (nasab) to him, but the lineage to the woman remains intact.

Of course, the details of these rulings are not mentioned in the above verses. To this extent, in the final verse under discussion, the Quran says: "And were it not for the grace of Allah upon you and His mercy, and that Allah is Accepting of repentance and All-Wise, [many of people would have perished or faced severe punishments]!" (وَ لَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ رَحْمَتُهُ وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ تَوَّابٌ حَكِيمٌ‌).

In fact, this verse is a brief allusion to emphasize the aforementioned rulings, as it shows that the "Li'an" procedure is a divine grace and correctly resolves the problem of the relationship between the two spouses in this regard.

On one hand, it does not force the man to remain silent if he sees a wrongdoing regarding his wife and not come to the religious judge for justice.

On the other hand, it does not expose the woman to the punishment for adultery (Zina al-Muhsanah) merely upon an accusation and grants her the right to defend herself.

Thirdly, it does not require the husband, if faced with such a scene, to go after four witnesses and expose this painful secret.

Fourthly, it separates this man and woman who are no longer able to continue their shared marital life and does not even allow them to marry in the future. For if the accusation is true, they are psychologically unable to continue marital life, and if it is false, the woman's emotions have been so wounded that returning to life together is difficult, as the result of such a matter is not just coldness but enmity and hostility.

And fifthly, it clarifies the status of the child.

This is the grace and mercy of Allah and His being Accepting of repentance and All-Wise toward His servants, having opened the problem with this delicate, calculated, and just solution. If we reflect deeply, the original ruling—the requirement of four witnesses—has not been entirely broken; rather, each of these four "testimonies" in the case of the husband and wife has been made a substitute for one "witness," and some of its rulings have been applied to it.

Key Points

1. Why was the ruling on slander (Qadhf) specified for spouses?

The first question that arises here is: what special characteristic do spouses have that this exceptional ruling was issued regarding their accusations?

The answer to this question can be found, on one hand, in the circumstances of the verse's revelation: whenever a man sees his wife with a stranger, if he wants to remain silent, it is impossible for him. How can his sense of honor allow him to show no reaction to the violation of his sanctity? If he wants to go to the judge and cry out, the punishment for slander would immediately be applied to him, for how does the judge know he is telling the truth? It might be a lie. And if he wants to seek four witnesses, this also does not align with his dignity and reputation; furthermore, the incident might end in the meantime.

On the other hand, strangers are quick to accuse one another, but a husband and wife are less likely to accuse each other of such matters. For this reason, in the case of strangers, bringing four witnesses is necessary, otherwise the punishment for slander is carried out, but in the case of spouses, it is not so, and for this reason, the mentioned ruling is one of their unique characteristics.

2. The Special Procedure of "Li'an"

From the explanations provided in the commentary of the verses, we concluded that to avert the punishment for slander from a man who has accused his wife of adultery, it is necessary for him to call Allah to witness four times that he is telling the truth. In reality, each of these four testimonies in this specific case has replaced a witness. On the fifth time, for further emphasis, he invites the curse of Allah upon himself if he is a liar.

Given that the implementation of these regulations usually takes place in an Islamic environment coupled with religious commitments, when someone sees that he must so decisively call Allah to witness and invoke a curse upon himself before an Islamic judge, he will often refrain from such a transgression. This serves as a barrier against him and false accusations—this is regarding the man.

As for the woman, the fact that she must call Allah to witness four times that the accusation is a lie to defend herself is to establish a balance between the testimony of the man and the woman. Since the woman has been subjected to accusation, in the fifth stage, she defends herself with a phrase more severe than the man's, inviting the wrath of Allah upon herself if the man has told the truth. We know that "curse" (la'n) is distance from mercy, but "wrath" (ghadhab) is something higher than distance from mercy, for wrath entails a penalty and punishment more than just being distanced from mercy. Therefore, in the commentary of Surah al-Fatihah, we said that those who have earned wrath (maghdhubi 'alayhim) are worse than those who are astray (dallin), even though the "astray" are certainly distant from Allah's mercy.

3. The Omitted Result (Jaza) in the Verse

The last verse under discussion is in the form of a conditional sentence whose result (jaza) is not mentioned. It only says: "And were it not for the grace of Allah and His mercy, and that Allah is Accepting of repentance and All-Wise..." but it does not say what would have happened. However, given the context of the speech, the result of this condition is clear. Sometimes, omission and silence regarding a matter give it more grandeur and importance, provoking many possibilities in the human mind, each of which gives the statement a new meaning.

For example, here the result of the condition might be: "If it were not for the grace and mercy of Allah, He would have lifted the veil from your deeds and exposed your actions so that you would be disgraced."

Or: "If it were not for the grace and mercy of Allah, He would have immediately punished and destroyed you."

Or: "If it were not for the grace and mercy of Allah, He would not have decreed such calculated rulings for the education of you human beings."

In fact, this omission of the result of the condition directs the listener's mind toward all these matters. [1]

[1]: In Tafsir al-Mizan, the answer to the condition is cited in a comprehensive manner that includes many other interpretations. It says: "The estimation is actually thus: 'Were it not for what Allah has bestowed upon you of the blessing of religion, His repentance for your sinners, and the legislation of laws to organize the affairs of your lives, wretchedness would have been inevitable for you, sin and error would have destroyed you, and the system of your lives would have been disrupted by ignorance.'"

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ جَآءُو بِٱلْإِفْكِ عُصْبَةٌ مِّنكُمْ ۚ لَا تَحْسَبُوهُ شَرًّا لَّكُم ۖ بَلْ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۚ لِكُلِّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُم مَّا ٱكْتَسَبَ مِنَ ٱلْإِثْمِ ۚ وَٱلَّذِى تَوَلَّىٰ كِبْرَهُۥ مِنْهُمْ لَهُۥ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (١١)
Indeed those who initiated the calumny are a group from among yourselves. Do not suppose it is bad thing for you. No, it is for your good. Each man among them bears [the onus for] his share in the sin, and as for him who assumed its major burden from among them, there is a great punishment for him. (11)
لَّوْلَآ إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ ظَنَّ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتُ بِأَنفُسِهِمْ خَيْرًا وَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَآ إِفْكٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٢)
When you [first] heard about it, why did not the faithful, men and women, think well of their folks, and say, ‘This is an obvious calumny’? (12)
لَّوْلَا جَآءُو عَلَيْهِ بِأَرْبَعَةِ شُهَدَآءَ ۚ فَإِذْ لَمْ يَأْتُوا۟ بِٱلشُّهَدَآءِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰذِبُونَ (١٣)
Why did they not bring four witnesses to it? So when they could not bring the witnesses, they are liars in Allah’s sight. (13)
وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَمَسَّكُمْ فِى مَآ أَفَضْتُمْ فِيهِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٤)
Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you in this world and the Hereafter, there would have befallen you a great punishment for what you ventured into, (14)
إِذْ تَلَقَّوْنَهُۥ بِأَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَتَقُولُونَ بِأَفْوَاهِكُم مَّا لَيْسَ لَكُم بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ وَتَحْسَبُونَهُۥ هَيِّنًا وَهُوَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ عَظِيمٌ (١٥)
when you were receiving it on your tongues, and were mouthing something of which you had no knowledge, supposing it to be a light matter, while it was a grave [matter] with Allah. (15)
وَلَوْلَآ إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ قُلْتُم مَّا يَكُونُ لَنَآ أَن نَّتَكَلَّمَ بِهَـٰذَا سُبْحَـٰنَكَ هَـٰذَا بُهْتَـٰنٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٦)
And why did you not, when you heard it, say, ‘It is not for us to say such a thing. [O Allah!] You are immaculate! This is a monstrous calumny!’ (16)

Occasion of Revelation

Two occasions of revelation have been narrated for the above verses:

The first occasion, which is more famous and appears in the books of Tafsir of the Ahl al-Sunnah and has also been narrated indirectly in Shi'a commentaries, is as follows:

"Aisha," the wife of the Prophet of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), says:

Whenever the Prophet of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) intended to go on a journey, he would cast lots among his wives. Whomever the lot fell upon, he would take her with him. In one of the battles [^1], the lot fell to me. I set out with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and since the verse of Hijab had been revealed, I stayed in a covered litter (howdah). The war ended, and we returned. As we approached Medina, it was night. I distanced myself slightly from the camp to attend to a need. When I returned, I realized that a necklace I had made of Yemeni beads had broken and fallen. I went back to search for it and was delayed. When I returned to the camp, I found that the army had moved on. They had placed my litter onto the camel and departed, assuming I was inside it, because women at that time were light-bodied due to food shortages; furthermore, I was quite young. In any case, I remained there all alone and thought that when they reached the halting place and did not find me, they would return for me. I stayed the night in that wilderness.

By chance, Safwan, one of the members of the Muslim army who had also fallen behind the camp, was in that wilderness that night. In the morning, he saw me from afar and approached. When he recognized me, without speaking a single word to me except for reciting "إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَ إِنَّا إِلَيْهِ راجِعُونَ" (Indeed we belong to Allah and indeed to Him we shall return), he knelt his camel. I mounted it while he held the camel's lead rope in his hand until we reached the army camp. This sight caused a group to spread rumors about me and, because of this, they brought destruction (and divine punishment) upon themselves.

The one who fueled this slander more than anyone else was "Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul."

We reached Medina, and this rumor spread throughout the city while I knew nothing of it. At this time, I fell ill. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) would come to visit, but I did not see in him the same kindness as before, and I did not know what the matter was. My condition improved, I went out, and gradually I learned of the rumors created by the hypocrites from some nearby women.

My illness intensified. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) came to visit me, and I asked his permission to go to my father's house. When I arrived at my father's house, I asked my mother, "What are the people saying?" She said to me, "Do not grieve; by Allah, whenever there are women of distinction who are envied by others, much is said about them."

At this time, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) consulted "Ali ibn Abi Talib" (عليه السلام) and "Usama ibn Zayd" regarding what to do about these talks.

Usama said: "O Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), she is your family, and we have seen nothing but good from her (do not pay attention to the people's words)."

But Ali (عليه السلام) said: "O Prophet! Allah has not made the matter difficult for you; there are many women besides her. Inquire from her maidservant about this."

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) summoned my maidservant and asked her, "Have you ever seen anything that raises doubt or suspicion regarding Aisha?" The maidservant said: "By the One who sent you with the truth, I have never seen any improper act from her."

At this point, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) decided to share these words with the people. He went upon the pulpit, faced the Muslims, and said: "O group of Muslims! If a man (referring to Abdullah ibn Ubayy) distresses me regarding my family, from whom I have seen nothing but purity, would I be excused if I punished him?! And likewise, if the scope of this accusation reaches a man from whom I have never seen any evil, what is the verdict?"

"Sa'd ibn Mu'adh al-Ansari" stood up and said: "You have the right. If he is from the tribe of 'Aws,' I will strike his neck (Sa'd ibn Mu'adh was the leader of the Aws tribe), and if he is from our brothers of the 'Khazraj' tribe, give us the command and we shall execute it."

"Sa'd ibn 'Ubada," who was the leader of the Khazraj and a righteous man, was overcome by tribal zeal at this point (as Abdullah ibn Ubayy, who was fueling this false rumor, was from the Khazraj tribe). He turned to Sa'd and said: "You lie! By Allah, you will not have the power to kill such a person if he is from our tribe!" "Usayd ibn Hudayr," who was the cousin of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, turned to Sa'd ibn 'Ubada and said: "You lie! By Allah, we will kill such a person; you are a hypocrite defending the hypocrites!"

At this moment, the tribes of Aws and Khazraj were on the verge of attacking one another and starting a war, while the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was standing on the pulpit. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) finally calmed them and silenced them.

This situation continued, and intense grief and sorrow overwhelmed me. For one month, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) never sat beside me.

I knew within myself that I was innocent of this slander and that Allah would eventually clarify the matter.

Finally, one day the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) came to me while he was smiling, and his first words were: "Glad tidings to you, for Allah has cleared you of this accusation." It was at this time that the verses "إِنَّ الَّذِينَ جاؤُ بِالْإِفْكِ..." until the end of the verses were revealed.

(Following the revelation of these verses, those who had spread this lie were all subjected to the prescribed punishment for slander [Hadd al-Qadhf]). [^1]

[^1]: The Battle of Banu Mustaliq in the fifth year of the Hijra. [^1]: What we have presented above is the substance of a narration found in most books of Tafsir with slight variations, and we have mentioned it with some brevity.

The second occasion of revelation, which is mentioned alongside the first in some books, is as follows: "Maria the Copt," one of the wives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), was accused by "Aisha" because she had a son from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) named Ibrahim. When Ibrahim passed away, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was deeply grieved. Aisha said, "Why are you so upset? He was not actually your son; he was the son of 'Jurayh the Copt'!!" When the Messenger of Allah heard this, he commissioned Ali (عليه السلام) to kill Jurayh for allowing himself such a betrayal.

When Ali (عليه السلام) went after Jurayh with a drawn sword and Jurayh saw the signs of anger on the Imam's face, he fled and climbed a date palm tree. When he felt that Ali (عليه السلام) might reach him, he threw himself down from the top of the tree. At that moment, his garment was pulled up, and it became clear that he had no male genitalia at all.

Ali (عليه السلام) came to the Prophet and said, "Should I proceed decisively in carrying out your command or should I investigate?" He replied, "You must investigate." Ali (عليه السلام) reported the incident. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) offered thanks to Allah and said, "Praise be to Allah who has kept evil and impurity away from our household."

At this time, the above verses were revealed, recounting the importance of this matter. [^1]

Analysis

Although the first occasion of revelation—as we mentioned—appears in many Islamic sources, there is room for discussion, questioning, and ambiguous points within it, including:

  1. From the various expressions of this Hadith—with its differences—it is clearly understood that the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was influenced by the wave of rumors to the extent that he sat in discussion and consultation with his companions regarding this matter, and even changed his behavior toward Aisha, withdrawing from her for a long period, along with other behaviors that all indicate the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), according to this narration, accepted the rumor to a large extent.

This matter is not only inconsistent with the station of Infallibility (Ismah), but even a steadfast and faithful Muslim should not be so influenced by baseless rumors. Even if a rumor leaves an unconscious impression on one's mind, one should not change their method in practice or surrender to it, let alone an Infallible whose station is clear.

Can it be believed that the severe rebukes and censures that will appear in the following verses—asking why a group of believers were influenced by this rumor? Why did they not demand four witnesses?—would also include the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself?! This is one of the major criticisms that leaves us, at the very least, in doubt regarding the authenticity of this occasion of revelation.

  1. While the apparent meaning of the verses indicates that the ruling regarding "Qadhf" (the accusation of an unchaste act) was revealed before the story of "Ifk," why did the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) not summon "Abdullah ibn Ubayy" and the others on the very day such a slander was spread and execute the divine punishment upon them? (Unless it is said that the verse of Qadhf and the verses related to Ifk were all revealed at once, or in other words, that ruling was also legislated in connection with this matter, in which case this objection is dismissed, but the first objection remains fully in force). [^1]

As for the second occasion of revelation, the problem is even greater because:

Firstly: According to this occasion of revelation, the person who committed the slander was only one person, whereas the verses explicitly state that a group (usbah) was active in this matter and spread the rumor so widely that it almost enveloped the environment. Therefore, the pronouns regarding the rebuke and censure of the believers involved in this matter are all in the plural form, which is in no way consistent with the second occasion of revelation.

Secondly: The question remains that if Aisha had committed such a slander and it was later proven otherwise, why did the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) not execute the punishment for slander (Hadd) upon her?

Thirdly: How is it possible for the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to issue a death sentence for an accused person based solely on the testimony of one woman, especially since rivalry among wives of the same husband is common? This fact would necessitate considering the possibility of deviation from truth and justice, or at least a mistake or error on her part.

In any case, what is important for us is not these specific occasions of revelation; what is important is to know that it is understood from the totality of the verses that an innocent person was accused of an unchaste act at the time of the revelation of these verses, and this rumor had spread in society.

Also, from the existing clues in the verse, it is understood that this slander concerned an individual who held special importance in the society of that day.

Furthermore, a group of hypocrites and outward Muslims wanted to exploit this incident for their own biased interests and to the detriment of the Islamic society. Thus, the above verses were revealed and confronted this incident with unparalleled decisiveness, firmly putting the foul-mouthed deviants and dark-hearted hypocrites in their place.

It is obvious that these rulings, regardless of whom the occasion of revelation concerns, are not restricted to that person or that time and place, and are applicable in every environment and every age.

After all these discussions, we turn to the commentary of the verses to see how the Quran, with complete eloquence and clarity, followed and scrutinized this specific incident and ultimately resolved it.

[^1]: Abridged from Tafsir al-Mizan, Nur al-Thaqalayn, and al-Safi. [^1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 15, p. 111.

Commentary

The Eventful Story of Ifk (The Great Slander)

The first verse under discussion, without mentioning the original incident itself, says:

"Indeed, those who brought forth that great slander were a group among you" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ جاؤُ بِالْإِفْكِ عُصْبَةٌ مِنْكُمْ).

For one of the techniques of eloquence and clarity is to omit redundant sentences and rely on the implied meaning of the words.

The word "Ifk" (rhyming with 'fikr'), according to "Raghib," is applied to anything that is turned away from its original and natural state. For example, contrary winds that have deviated from their original path are called "mu'tafikah." Subsequently, any speech that deviates from the truth and inclines toward falsehood, including lies and slander, is called "Ifk."

The late "Tabarsi" in "Majma' al-Bayan" believes that "Ifk" is not used for every simple lie, but rather it is a great lie that transforms a matter from its original form. Therefore, the word "Ifk" itself expresses the importance of this incident and the lie and slander that were proposed in this regard.

The word "Usbah" (rhyming with 'ghussah') originally comes from the root "asab," meaning the specific fibers that bind human muscles together, the collection of which is called the nervous system. Subsequently, a group that is united and has bonds, connection, cooperation, and shared thought is called an "Usbah." The use of this word shows that the conspirators in the story of "Ifk" had a close and firm connection with each other and formed a cohesive and powerful network for the conspiracy.

Some have said that this expression is usually used for ten to forty people. [^1] In any case, following this sentence, the Quran consoles the believers who were deeply distressed by the emergence of such an accusation against a chaste person, saying: "Do not think it is bad for you; rather, it is good for you" (لا تَحْسَبُوهُ شَرًّا لَكُمْ بَلْ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ).

This is because it pulled back the curtain from the evil intentions of a group of defeated enemies and dark-hearted hypocrites, and disgraced these fair-seeming but foul-natured individuals. It is good for the touchstone of experience to come forth so that those who have dross may be blackened; perhaps if this incident had not occurred and they had remained unknown, they would have struck a harder and more dangerous blow in the future.

This incident taught the Muslims a lesson: that following rumor-mongers leads them to dark days; they must stand firmly against such actions.

Another lesson this incident taught the Muslims was not to look only at the outward appearance of events; for many distressing and outwardly bad events contain "much good" (khayr kathir) within them.

Interestingly, by mentioning the pronoun "lakum" (for you), He considers all believers as participants in this incident, and truly it is so, for believers are not separate or alienated from one another in terms of social dignity; they are partners and participants in sorrows and joys.

Then, in the continuation of this verse, He points to two points: First, He says:

"For every person among them is what he has earned of the sin" (لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ مَا اكْتَسَبَ مِنَ الْإِثْمِ).

This is an indication that the great responsibility of the leaders and founders of a sin will never prevent the responsibility of others; rather, everyone, to whatever extent and in whatever amount they are a partner and participant in a conspiracy, carries the burden of its sin on their shoulders.

The second point is: "And he who took upon himself the greater part of it among them, for him is a great punishment" (وَ الَّذِي تَوَلَّى كِبْرَهُ مِنْهُمْ لَهُ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ).

Commentators have said this person was "Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul," who was considered the head of the "Companions of Ifk." Others have also named "Mistah ibn Uthatha" and "Hassan ibn Thabit" as examples of this statement.

In any case, the one who was more active than anyone else in this incident, who kindled the first flames of the fire of Ifk, and was considered the leader of this group, has a greater punishment in proportion to the greatness of his sin (it is not unlikely that the expression "tawalla" refers to the matter of his leadership).

[^1]: In the Tafsir "Ruh al-Ma'ani," this meaning is quoted from the book "Sihah."

Then, the discourse turns to the believers who were deceived in this incident and were influenced, severely rebuking them over several verses, saying:

"Why, when you heard it, did the believing men and believing women not think well of themselves?!" (لَوْ لا إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ ظَنَّ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَ الْمُؤْمِناتُ بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ خَيْراً).

Meaning, why, when you listened to the words of the hypocrites regarding the believers, did you not approach it with a good opinion of other believers who are as your own selves?

"And why did they not say, 'This is an obvious lie'?" (وَ قالُوا هذا إِفْكٌ مُبِينٌ).

You who knew the ugly and disgraceful record of this group of hypocrites.

You who were well aware of the chastity of the person under accusation.

You who were certain, based on various clues, that such an accusation was impossible.

You who were aware of the conspiracies being carried out against the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) by the enemies.

With all this, it is a place for blame and rebuke that you should hear such false rumors and choose silence, let alone becoming the agent of its spread, whether consciously or unconsciously!

Interestingly, in the above verse, instead of saying "you should have had a good opinion of the one accused of this slander," it says: "you should have had a good opinion of yourselves." This expression, as we said, indicates that the souls of the believers are not separate from each other and they are all as a single soul; if an accusation is directed at one of them, it is as if it were directed at all of them. If time causes pain to one limb, no rest remains for the other limbs. Just as everyone considers themselves obligated to defend themselves against accusations, they must defend their other religious brothers and sisters to the same extent. [^1] The use of the word "anfus" (selves) in such cases is also seen in other verses of the Quran, including verse 11 of Surah al-Hujurat: "وَ لا تَلْمِزُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ" (And do not insult yourselves [one another]!).

And the fact that the emphasis is placed on "believing men and believing women" indicates that faith is a quality that can act as a barrier and deterrent against ill thoughts.

[^1]: And what some have said—that here a "mudaf" (possessive) is omitted and the estimation was: "the believing men and believing women thought well of one another"—is a baseless statement that destroys the elegance and subtlety of the verse.

Up to this point, the rebuke and blame have moral and spiritual aspects, for by any account, it was not right for the believers to remain silent in the face of such an ugly slander or to become tools in the hands of the dark-hearted rumor-mongers.

Then, He turns attention to the judicial dimension of the matter, saying: "Why did they not bring four witnesses to it?" (لَوْ لا جاؤُ عَلَيْهِ بِأَرْبَعَةِ شُهَداءَ).

"Since they have not brought the witnesses, they are, in the sight of Allah, the liars" (فَإِذْ لَمْ يَأْتُوا بِالشُّهَداءِ فَأُولئِكَ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ هُمُ الْكاذِبُونَ).

This questioning and rebuke show that the command to produce four witnesses, as well as the punishment for slander (Hadd al-Qadhf) in the event of its absence, had been revealed before the verses of "Ifk."

As for the question of why the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself did not take action to execute this punishment, the answer is clear: because without cooperation from the people, taking action in such a matter is not possible. Tribalistic bonds sometimes caused negative resistance against the execution of certain rulings, even if temporarily, as was the case in this incident according to historical reports.

Finally, He summarizes all these rebukes, saying: "And if it were not for the favor of Allah upon you and His mercy in this world and the Hereafter, a great punishment would have touched you for that [rumor] into which you plunged" (وَ لَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ رَحْمَتُهُ فِي الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةِ لَمَسَّكُمْ فِيما أَفَضْتُمْ فِيهِ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ).

Considering that "afadtum" is from the root "ifadah," meaning the outflow of water in abundance and excess, and also sometimes meaning to be immersed in water, it appears from this expression that the rumor of the said accusation spread to such an extent that it even immersed the believers within it!

The next verse is, in fact, an explanation and clarification of the previous discussion, showing how they became submerged in this great sin due to negligence. It says: "Remember when you were receiving it with your tongues and this rumor was being taken from one another" (إِذْ تَلَقَّوْنَهُ بِأَلْسِنَتِكُمْ).

"And you were saying with your mouths that of which you had no knowledge" (وَ تَقُولُونَ بِأَفْواهِكُمْ ما لَيْسَ لَكُمْ بِهِ عِلْمٌ).

"And you thought it was a light matter while in the sight of Allah it was great" (وَ تَحْسَبُونَهُ هَيِّناً وَ هُوَ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ عَظِيمٌ).

In reality, this verse points to three parts of their great sins in this regard:

"First": Going to meet this rumor and taking it from one another's tongues (acceptance of the rumor).

"Second": Spreading a rumor for which they had no knowledge or certainty and recounting it to others (spreading the rumor without any investigation).

"Third": Considering it a simple and small act, while it was not only connected to the dignity of two Muslim individuals but was tied to the dignity and honor of the Islamic society (belittling the rumor and using it as a means of entertainment).

Interestingly, in one instance the expression "bi-alsinatikum" (with your tongues) is used, and in another "bi-afwahikum" (with your mouths), even though all speech occurs with the tongue and through the mouth. This indicates that you neither demanded proof in accepting this rumor nor relied on proof in spreading it; only words that were mere wind—the result of the turning of the tongue and the movements of the mouth—were your capital in this incident.

And since this was a very important incident that a group of Muslims had considered light and small, He emphasizes it once more in the following verse, raining down a new wave of rebuke upon them and striking a firmer whip upon their souls, saying:

"And why, when you heard it, did you not say, 'It is not for us to speak of this (for it is a slander without proof). Exalted are You, [O Lord]; this is a great slander'!" (وَ لَوْ لا إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ قُلْتُمْ ما يَكُونُ لَنا أَنْ نَتَكَلَّمَ بِهذا سُبْحانَكَ هذا بُهْتانٌ عَظِيمٌ).

In fact, previously they were blamed only for why they did not look with a good opinion toward those who were accused. But here, He says that in addition to a good opinion, you should have never allowed yourselves to even open your lips to such a slander, let alone becoming the agent of its spread.

You should have been overwhelmed with wonder at this great slander, and remembered the purity and transcendence of the Lord, and sought refuge in Allah from becoming tainted by the spread of such an accusation.

But unfortunately, you passed by it simply and easily—nay—you even fueled it and unknowingly became tools of the conspiring and rumor-mongering hypocrites.

Regarding the importance of the sin of "rumor-mongering," its "motives," and the "way to combat it," as well as other points surrounding this subject, we will discuss them, Allah willing, under the upcoming verses.

Ayahs

يَعِظُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَن تَعُودُوا۟ لِمِثْلِهِۦٓ أَبَدًا إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٧)
Allah advises you lest you should ever repeat the like of it, should you be faithful. (17)
وَيُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (١٨)
Allah clarifies the signs for you, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (18)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُحِبُّونَ أَن تَشِيعَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةُ فِى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (١٩)
Indeed those who want indecency to spread among the faithful—there is a painful punishment for them in the world and the Hereafter, and Allah knows and you do not know. (19)
وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٢٠)
Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, and that Allah is all-kind, all-merciful… (20)

Commentary

Spreading Indecency is Forbidden

Once again, these verses speak of the "Event of Ifk" (the Great Slander) and the sinister and painful consequences of rumor-mongering and making accusations against the honor of pure individuals. This matter is so significant that the Quran finds it necessary to analyze it several times through various effective methods, establishing such firm preventive measures so that such a scene is never repeated in Muslim society in the future.

First, It says: "Allah admonishes you that you should never repeat the like of it, if you are believers" (يَعِظُكُمُ اللَّهُ أَنْ تَعُودُوا لِمِثْلِهِ) [^1]. This means it is a sign of faith that a person does not pursue such enormous sins; if one commits them, it is either a sign of lack of faith or weakness of faith. In reality, this sentence outlines one of the pillars of repentance, for regret over the past alone is not enough; one must also resolve not to repeat the sin in the future for the repentance to be comprehensive.
