Then, to emphasize further so they realize these are not ordinary words, but rather it is the All-Knowing and All-Wise Allah who has set out to explain and clarify life-altering truths, It says: "And Allah makes the verses clear to you, and Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise" (وَ يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمُ الْآياتِ وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ). By virtue of His knowledge, He is aware of every detail of your actions, and by virtue of His wisdom, He gives the necessary commands.

[^1]: This sentence actually has an implied word, which is "not" (Allah admonishes you not to ever return to the like of it). If we do not assume an implied word, the phrase "admonishes you" (يعظكم) must mean "forbids you" (ينهاكم); i.e., Allah forbids you from returning to such acts.

Or, in other words, by virtue of His knowledge, He is aware of your needs and the factors of your good and evil, and by virtue of His wisdom, He harmonizes His commands and laws with them.

Again, to reinforce the matter, the discourse shifts from a specific personal incident to the statement of a general and comprehensive law, saying: "Indeed, those who love that indecency should spread among those who believe will have a painful punishment in this world and the Hereafter" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُحِبُّونَ أَنْ تَشِيعَ الْفاحِشَةُ فِي الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ فِي الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةِ). It is noteworthy that It does not say "those who spread indecency," but rather "those who love that such a thing should happen," which is the ultimate emphasis in this regard.

In other words, let it not be imagined that all this insistence and emphasis was because the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) wife or someone of her stature was accused; rather, in the case of any believer where such a situation arises, all those emphases and insistences apply to them, as it is not a personal or private matter—though other dimensions may be added depending on the case.

Furthermore, it should be noted that "spreading indecency" (ishā‘at al-fāḥishah) is not limited to a person publishing a baseless lie or slander regarding a believing man or woman and accusing them of an act contrary to chastity. This is one of its instances, but it is not limited to it. This expression has a broad meaning that includes any kind of publishing of corruption, spreading of ugliness and vices, and helping in their expansion.

Of course, the word "fāḥishah" or "fuḥshā’" in the Holy Quran is mostly used in cases of sexual deviations and violations of honor. However, from a linguistic perspective, as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "fuḥsh," "fuḥshā’," and "fāḥishah" mean any behavior or speech whose ugliness is great. In the Holy Quran, it is also sometimes used in this broad sense, such as: "And those who avoid the major sins and indecencies..." (وَ الَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبائِرَ الْإِثْمِ وَ الْفَواحِشَ) (Surah Ash-Shura, Verse 37). Thus, the breadth of the verse's concept becomes completely clear.

As for the statement that they have a painful punishment in this world, it may refer to the legal penalties (Hudud) and discretionary punishments (Ta'zirat), social repercussions, and the sinister individual effects that afflict the perpetrators of these acts in this very world. Additionally, their deprivation of the right to testify and being condemned to wickedness (fisq) and disgrace are also among its worldly effects. As for the painful punishment of the Hereafter, it is distance from Allah's mercy, Divine wrath, and the fire of Hell.

At the end of the verse, It says: "And Allah knows and you do not know" (وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَ أَنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ). He is well aware of the sinister consequences and deadly effects of spreading indecency in this world and the Hereafter, but you are not aware of the various dimensions of this issue. He knows those in whose hearts lies the love for this sin, and He recognizes those who engage in this sinister act under deceptive names, but you do not know and do not recognize them. And He knows how to reveal His laws to prevent this ugly and vile act.

In the last verse under discussion, which is also the final verse regarding the "Ifk" and the struggle against "spreading indecency" and "slandering" (qadhf) chaste believers, It repeats and emphasizes this truth once more: "And were it not for the grace of Allah upon you and His mercy, and that Allah is Kind and Merciful, [you would have been ruined]" (وَ لَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ رَحْمَتُهُ وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ رَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ) [^1]. That is, if Allah were not Merciful and Kind toward you, He would have prescribed such a great and painful punishment for you in this world that your days would have turned dark and your lives would have been ruined.

1- What is Spreading Indecency?

Since man is a social being, the large society in which he lives is, in one sense, like his home, and its sanctity is like the sanctity of his house. The purity of society helps his purity, and its pollution contributes to his pollution. Based on this principle, Islam severely combats any action that poisons or pollutes the atmosphere of society.

If we see that Islam severely combats backbiting (ghaybah), one of its philosophies is that backbiting reveals hidden flaws and wounds the dignity of society. If we see that covering the faults of others is commanded, one reason is so that sin does not take on a public and universal aspect. If we see that an open sin is more significant than a hidden and concealed sin, it is to the extent that we read in a narration from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام: "The one who publicizes a sin is forsaken and rejected, and the one who conceals a sin is forgiven." [^2]

[^1]: This sentence, like its counterpart a few verses prior, has an omitted part, and its estimation is: "Were it not for the grace of Allah upon you... a great punishment would have touched you for what you ventured into."

[^2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Concealing Sins.

And if we see that in the above verses, the subject of spreading indecency is condemned with a very severe and extraordinarily crushing tone, the reason is the same. Fundamentally, sin is like fire. When this fire is lit in a part of society, effort must be made to extinguish the fire, or at least contain it. But if we fan the flames and carry it from one point to another, the conflagration will spread everywhere, and no one will be able to control it.

Beyond this, the gravity of sin in the eyes of the general public and maintaining the outward appearance of society from pollution is itself a great barrier against corruption. Spreading indecency, publishing sin, and openly committing wickedness breaks this barrier, trivializes the sin, and makes polluting oneself with it seem simple.

In a hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Whoever publicizes an indecency is like the one who initiated it." [^1] In another hadith from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام, we read that a man came to him and said: "May I be sacrificed for you, something was reported to me about one of my religious brothers that I disliked. I asked him about it and he denied it, while a group of reliable people had reported this to me." The Imam said: "Belie your hearing and your sight regarding your brother. Even if fifty people swear to you that he did something and he tells you he did not, believe him and belie them. Never spread anything about him that would disgrace him or destroy his character, for then you would be among those about whom Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: 'Indeed, those who love that indecency should spread among those who believe will have a painful punishment in this world and the Hereafter.'" [^1] [^2]

It is also necessary to mention that "spreading indecency" takes various forms: Sometimes it is by fanning the flames of a lie or slander and recounting it to this person and that. Sometimes it is by establishing centers that cause corruption and the publication of indecency. Sometimes it is by providing the means of sin to people or encouraging them to sin. And finally, sometimes it is achieved by tearing the veil of modesty and committing sin in public. All of these are instances of "spreading indecency," as the concept of this word is broad and extensive (reflect on this).

2- The Scourge of Rumor-mongering

The fabrication and dissemination of false and alarming rumors is one of the most important branches of psychological warfare used by conspirators. When the enemy is unable to cause harm through direct confrontation, they resort to spreading rumors, and through this, they worry and preoccupy public opinion, diverting it from necessary and sensitive issues. Rumor-mongering is one of the destructive weapons used to wound the reputation of the good and pure and to scatter people from around them.

In the verses under discussion—according to the well-known occasions of revelation—the hypocrites, in order to tarnish the reputation of the Prophet of Allah and undermine his public standing, resorted to fabricating the most cowardly rumors and spreading them. They questioned the purity of some of the wives of the great Prophet of Islam—taking advantage of a suitable opportunity—and for a relatively long period, they so disturbed and distressed the minds of the Muslims that the steadfast and true believers writhed like someone bitten by a snake, until Divine revelation came to their aid and gave such a severe reprimand to the rumor-mongering hypocrites that it became a lesson for everyone.

Although in societies where political suppression exists, the publication of rumors is considered a type of struggle, other motives such as revenge, settling private scores, destroying public trust, tarnishing the character of great individuals, and diverting thoughts from fundamental issues are also factors in spreading rumors. It is not enough for us to know what motive causes rumor-mongering; what is important is to warn society against becoming a tool in the hands of rumor-mongers, helping to spread them, and providing the means for its own destruction by its own hand. We must make people realize that we must bury every rumor right where we hear it; otherwise, we have made the enemy happy and victorious. Furthermore, we will be subject to the painful punishment of this world and the Hereafter mentioned in the verses above.

3- Trivializing Sin

Among the matters condemned in the above verses was that you commit a sin such as publishing a calumny and slander, yet you consider it trivial. Truly, trivializing a sin is itself one of the errors. One who commits a sin and considers it great and is upset by their action will set out to repent and compensate. But the one who considers it small and attaches no importance to it, and even sometimes says: "How fortunate I am if this is my only sin!"—such a person is on a dangerous path and continues in their sin. For this reason, we read in a hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام: "The most severe of sins is that which its perpetrator deems light." [^1]

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Reproaching.

[^1]: Kitab Thawab al-A'mal, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 582.

[^2]: Of course, this matter has exceptions, including the subject of testimony in court or cases where there is no way to forbid the evil except by lifting the veil from the wickedness of a person.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 348.

Ayahs

۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۚ وَمَن يَتَّبِعْ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ فَإِنَّهُۥ يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَٱلْمُنكَرِ ۚ وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ مَا زَكَىٰ مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَدًا وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُزَكِّى مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢١)
O you who have faith! Do not follow in Satan’s steps. Whoever follows in Satan’s steps [should know that] he indeed prompts [you to commit] indecent and wrongful acts. Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, not one of you would ever become pure. But Allah purifies whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (21)
وَلَا يَأْتَلِ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْفَضْلِ مِنكُمْ وَٱلسَّعَةِ أَن يُؤْتُوٓا۟ أُو۟لِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينَ وَٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَلْيَعْفُوا۟ وَلْيَصْفَحُوٓا۟ ۗ أَلَا تُحِبُّونَ أَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٢٢)
The well-off and the opulent among you should not vow that they will not give [any longer] to the relatives and the needy, and to those who have migrated in the way of Allah, and let them excuse and forbear. Do you not love that Allah should forgive you? Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (22)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ ٱلْغَـٰفِلَـٰتِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ لُعِنُوا۟ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (٢٣)
Indeed those who accuse chaste and unwary faithful women shall be cursed in this world and the Hereafter, and there shall be a great punishment for them (23)
يَوْمَ تَشْهَدُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَلْسِنَتُهُمْ وَأَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَرْجُلُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٢٤)
on the day when witness shall be given against them by their tongues, their hands, and their feet concerning what they used to do. (24)
يَوْمَئِذٍ يُوَفِّيهِمُ ٱللَّهُ دِينَهُمُ ٱلْحَقَّ وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٢٥)
On that day, Allah will pay them in full their due recompense, and they shall know that Allah is the Manifest Reality. (25)

Commentary

Punishment Also Has a Measure!

Although these verses do not explicitly continue the story of the Ifk (the Great Slander), they are considered a completion of the content of that discussion. They serve as a warning to all believers that the influence of satanic thoughts and actions is sometimes gradual and subtle. If not controlled in the very first steps, by the time a person realizes it, the matter has gone too far. Therefore, when the first whispers of spreading indecency or any other sin appear, one must stand against them right there so that the pollution does not spread.

In the first verse, addressing the believers, He says: "O you who have believed, do not follow the footsteps of Satan. And whoever follows the footsteps of Satan—indeed, he enjoins immorality and wrongdoing" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَتَّبِعُوا خُطُواتِ الشَّيْطَانِ وَ مَنْ يَتَّبِعْ خُطُواتِ الشَّيْطَانِ فَإِنَّهُ يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشَاءِ وَ الْمُنْكَرِ) [^1]. If we interpret "Satan" in the broad sense of the word—meaning "any harmful, wicked, and destructive being"—the extensiveness of this warning in all dimensions of life becomes clear.

A chaste and faithful person can never be thrown into the embrace of corruption all at once; rather, he traverses this path step by step:

Step one: Socializing and befriending the polluted. Step two: Participating in their gatherings. Step three: Thinking about sin. Step four: Committing doubtful acts and engaging in ambiguities. Step five: Committing minor sins. And finally, in the subsequent steps, he becomes trapped in the worst of major sins. He is exactly like someone who has placed his reins in the hands of a criminal who leads him step by step toward a precipice until he falls and is destroyed. Yes, these are the "footsteps of Satan." [^1]

Then, referring to one of His greatest blessings upon humanity in the path of guidance, He says: "And if not for the favor of Allah upon you and His mercy, not one of you would have been pure, ever, but Allah purifies whom He wills, and Allah is Hearing and Knowing" (وَ لَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ رَحْمَتُهُ ما زَكى‌ مِنْكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَداً وَ لكِنَّ اللَّهَ يُزَكِّي مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ اللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ).

Undoubtedly, it is the favor and mercy of Allah that brings about the salvation of humans from pollutions, deviations, and sins. For on one hand, He has granted the gift of intellect, and on the other, the gift of the Prophet's existence and the laws revealed through revelation. However, beyond these gifts, His specific success (tawfiq) and His unseen aids—which encompass those who are ready, capable, and deserving—are the most important factors for purity and self-purification.

The phrase "مَنْ يَشاءُ" (whom He wills), as we have said many times, does not mean a will or desire without reason. Rather, as long as there is no striving and effort from the side of the servants, guidance and blessings from Allah's side do not occur. He takes the hand of the one who seeks this path, steps into it, and strives; He protects him from the whispers of the devils and brings him to the intended destination.

In other words, Allah's favor and mercy sometimes have a "legislative" (tashri'i) aspect, which is through the appointment of prophets, the sending of heavenly books, and the legislation of laws, glad tidings, and warnings. At other times, it has a "creative/existential" (takwini) aspect, which is through spiritual and divine aids. The verses under discussion (by the evidence of the phrase "whom He wills") mostly refer to the second part.

[^1]: The sentence "وَ مَنْ يَتَّبِعْ خُطُواتِ الشَّيْطانِ فَإِنَّهُ يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشاءِ" actually has an omitted part (the result of the condition), and the estimation is: "And whoever follows the footsteps of Satan will commit indecency and wrongdoing, for he [Satan] enjoins both" (Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 18, p. 112, under the verses discussed). And it should be noted that the phrase "فَإِنَّهُ يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشاءِ" cannot be the result of the condition itself.

[^1]: Regarding the difference between "fuhsha" (indecency) and "munkar" (wrongdoing), we discussed this in Vol. 11, p. 370, under verse 90 of Surah An-Nahl. Additionally, it should be noted that "zakat" and "tazkiyah" originally mean "to grow" and "to make grow," but in many cases, they are used to mean "to be purified" and "to purify." Both may return to a single root, because as long as there is no purification from obstacles, corruptions, and vices, growth and development will not be possible.

A group of commentators has narrated an occasion of revelation for the second verse under discussion which clarifies its link with the preceding verses: "This verse was revealed concerning a group of the Companions who, after the 'story of the Ifk,' swore that they would not provide financial help to any of those involved in this incident who fueled this great slander, and would not show them any kindness. The above verse was revealed, restraining them from this severity and harshness, and commanding them to pardon and forgive." This occasion of revelation was narrated by Al-Qurtubi in his commentary from Ibn Abbas and Dahhak, and by the late Tabarsi from Ibn Abbas and others, under the verses discussed, and it has a general aspect.

However, a group of Sunni commentators insists that this verse was revealed specifically regarding Abu Bakr, who, after the story of the Ifk, cut off his financial aid to Mistah ibn Uthatha—who was his cousin or nephew and had fueled the issue of the Ifk. This is while all the pronouns used in the verse are in the plural form, indicating that a group of Muslims had decided to cut off their aid to these offenders after this incident, and the verse forbade them from doing so.

In any case, we know that the verses of the Quran are not restricted to their occasion of revelation; rather, they include all believers until the Day of Resurrection. That is, it recommends that Muslims should not be overcome by intense and heated emotions in such cases and should not take harsh decisions against the slips and mistakes of sinners.

With this occasion of revelation in mind, we return to the commentary of the verse:

The Quran says: "And let not those of virtue among you and wealth swear [or decide] not to give to near kindred and the needy and the emigrants in the cause of Allah" (وَ لا يَأْتَلِ أُولُوا الْفَضْلِ مِنْكُمْ وَ السَّعَةِ أَنْ يُؤْتُوا أُولِي الْقُرْبى‌ وَ الْمَساكِينَ وَ الْمُهاجِرِينَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ).

This expression shows that some of those who were tainted by the Ifk were among the emigrants in the way of Allah who were deceived by the hypocrites. Because of their past, Allah did not allow them to be ostracized from the Islamic society or for decisions to be made regarding them that exceeded what they deserved.

Additionally, the phrase "لا يَأْتَلِ" is from the root "alyah" (rhyming with atiyyah), meaning to swear an oath, or from the root "alw" (rhyming with dalw), meaning to fall short or to abandon. Therefore, according to the first meaning, the verse forbids swearing an oath to cut off such aid [^1], and according to the second meaning, it forbids falling short in or abandoning this act.

Then, to encourage and motivate Muslims to continue such good deeds, it adds: "and let them pardon and overlook" (وَ لْيَعْفُوا وَ لْيَصْفَحُوا).

"Do you not love that Allah should forgive you?" (أَ لا تُحِبُّونَ أَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ).

Just as you expect divine forgiveness for your slips, you must not forget pardon and forgiveness regarding others.

[^1]: In this case, the word "la" (not) must be assumed before "yu'tu" (they give), making the estimation: "And let them not swear... that they will not give."

"And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful" (وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

It is remarkable that on one hand, with all those crushing verses, He severely condemns the "companions of the Ifk," yet to prevent extremist individuals from exceeding the limit, He controls their emotions with three phrases, each more captivating and attractive than the other:

First, He commands pardon and forgiveness. Then He says: Do you not love that Allah should forgive you? So you should forgive too. And finally, He mentions two of Allah's attributes, "Forgiving" and "Merciful," as an emphasis, indicating that you cannot be more fervent than the command of the Lord. Allah, who is the primary owner of this decree, is Forgiving and Merciful; He commands not to cut off aid—so what are you saying?! Undoubtedly, not all Muslims involved in the incident of the Ifk did so with prior conspiracy. Some hypocrites posing as Muslims were the founders, and a group of deceived Muslims were followers. Undoubtedly, they were all at fault and sinful, but there was a great difference between these two groups, and they should not have been treated identically.

In any case, the above verses are a very great lesson for the Muslims of today and tomorrow and all future generations: that when some individuals become tainted with a sin or a slip, one must not exceed the bounds of moderation in punishment, they must not be ostracized from Islamic society, and the doors of aid must not be closed to them such that they suddenly fall into the lap of the enemies and join their ranks.

The above verses are, in fact, a depiction of the balance between Islamic "attraction" and "repulsion": The verses of the Ifk and the severe punishment for those who slander the honor of people constitute the great force of repulsion, and the verse under discussion, which speaks of pardon, forgiveness, and Allah being Forgiving and Merciful, expresses attraction!

Then, once again, He returns to the issue of "qadhf" (slander) and accusing chaste, believing women of honor-related charges, and says emphatically and decisively: "Indeed, those who [falsely] accuse chaste, unaware, believing women are cursed in this world and the Hereafter; and they will have a great punishment" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ الْمُحْصَناتِ الْغافِلاتِ الْمُؤْمِناتِ لُعِنُوا فِي الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةِ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ). In fact, three attributes are mentioned for these women, each of which is a reason for the gravity of the injustice inflicted upon them through slander: "Muhsanat" (chaste women), "ghafilat" (unaware/distant from any pollution), and "mu'minat" (believing women). In this way, it shows to what extent making false accusations against such individuals is unjust, cowardly, and deserving of great punishment. [^1] Additionally, the term "ghafilat" is an interesting expression that specifies their ultimate purity from any deviation or indecency; that is, they are so indifferent to sexual pollutions that it is as if they are completely unaware of them. For a person's stance against sin sometimes becomes such that the very thought of sin leaves their mind, as if such an act does not exist externally at all, and this is a high stage of piety.

There is also the possibility that "ghafilat" refers to women who are unaware of the false accusations made against them and therefore do not defend themselves. Consequently, the verse under discussion raises a new point regarding such accusations. While the previous verses spoke of slanderers who were known and punished, here the talk is of rumor-mongers who have hidden themselves from punishment and the legal penalty (hadd). The Quran says: let them not imagine that by this act they can keep themselves away from divine punishment forever; Allah distances them from His mercy in this world, and in the Hereafter, they have a great punishment.

Although the above verse is placed after the story of the Ifk and it seems its revelation was not unrelated to this incident, like all verses revealed in specific cases whose concept is general, it is not restricted to a specific instance.

It is strange that some commentators like Fakhr al-Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir and others insist on limiting the concept of this verse to slandering the wives of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), placing this sin on the level of disbelief (kufr) and considering the word "curse" (la'n) mentioned in the verse as evidence for that.

However, while slander is a very great sin—and if it concerns the wives of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), it is considered an even greater and more momentous sin—it does not by itself cause disbelief. Therefore, in the story of the Ifk, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) did not treat such individuals as "apostates" (murtad); rather, in the verses following it, which we have read, he recommended against excessive harshness toward them, which is inconsistent with disbelief.

As for "curse" (la'n), it is being distanced from Allah's mercy, which applies to both the disbeliever and the perpetrator of major sins. Thus, in these very verses concerning the penalty for slander (in the rulings related to li'an), the word "curse" is used twice regarding liars.

In Islamic narrations as well, the word "curse" has been used repeatedly regarding some perpetrators of major sins. The hadith: "Allah has cursed ten groups regarding wine..." is well-known.

The next verse specifies the state of this group of slanderers in the great court of God, saying: "They will have a great punishment, on a Day when their tongues, their hands and their feet will bear witness against them as to what they used to do" (يَوْمَ تَشْهَدُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَلْسِنَتُهُمْ وَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ). Their tongues will move without them wanting to and will recount the facts. After seeing the definitive proofs and evidence of the crime, these criminals, contrary to their inner desire, will explicitly confess to the sin and reveal everything because they see no room for denial.

Their hands and feet will also speak, and even according to Quranic verses, the skin of their bodies will speak. It is as if they are tape recorders that have recorded all of a person's sounds, and the effects of sins throughout life are imprinted upon them. Yes, they appear there, where it is the "Day of Manifestation" and the day when all secrets are revealed.

And if we see in some verses of the Quran it mentions regarding the Day of Resurrection: "This Day, We shall seal up their mouths, and their hands will speak to Us, and their feet will bear witness to what they used to earn" (الْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلى‌ أَفْواهِهِمْ وَ تُكَلِّمُنا أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ تَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ) (Surah Ya-Sin, Verse 65), it does not contradict the verse under discussion. For it is possible that at the beginning, tongues are disabled and other limbs testify, and when the testimony of the hands and feet reveals the facts, the tongue begins to move and says what needs to be said and confesses to the sins.

Then He says: "That Day, Allah will pay them in full their true recompense" (يَوْمَئِذٍ يُوَفِّيهِمُ اللَّهُ دِينَهُمُ الْحَقَّ).

"And they will know that Allah is the Manifest Truth" (وَ يَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ الْمُبِينُ).

If today, and in this world, they doubt or hesitate regarding the truth of the Lord, or lead people astray, on that day the signs of His greatness, power, and truth will become so clear that they will force even the most stubborn of the obstinate to confess.

[^1]: Al-Mizan, under the verses discussed, Vol. 15, p. 122.

Ayah

ٱلْخَبِيثَـٰتُ لِلْخَبِيثِينَ وَٱلْخَبِيثُونَ لِلْخَبِيثَـٰتِ ۖ وَٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتُ لِلطَّيِّبِينَ وَٱلطَّيِّبُونَ لِلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مُبَرَّءُونَ مِمَّا يَقُولُونَ ۖ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ (٢٦)
Vicious women are for vicious men, and vicious men for vicious women. Good women are for good men, and good men for good women. These are absolved of what they say [about them]. For them is forgiveness and a noble provision. (26)

Commentary

Light seeks light! ...

The above verse is, in fact, a follow-up and an emphasis on the verses of Ifk (the Slander) and the verses preceding it. It expresses a natural law in the world of creation with which Divine legislation is also harmonized.

It states: "Evil and impure women belong to evil and impure men, just as impure men belong to impure women" (الْخَبِيثاتُ لِلْخَبِيثِينَ وَ الْخَبِيثُونَ لِلْخَبِيثاتِ).

And on the opposite side, "Good and pure women belong to good and pure men, and pure and good men belong to pure and good women" (وَ الطَّيِّباتُ لِلطَّيِّبِينَ وَ الطَّيِّبُونَ لِلطَّيِّباتِ).

At the end of the verse, referring to the latter group—namely, the chaste men and women—it says: "They are innocent of the false accusations made against them" (أُولئِكَ مُبَرَّؤُنَ مِمَّا يَقُولُونَ).

And for this reason, "Divine forgiveness and a generous provision await them" (لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَ رِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ).

Key Points

1- Who are the "Evil" (Khabithat) and the "Good" (Tayyibat)?

Regarding who is meant by "evil women" (khabithat) and "evil men" (khabithin), as well as "good women" (tayyibat) and "good men" (tayyibin) in the verse under discussion, commentators have offered various explanations:

1- It is sometimes said that the meaning refers to impure words, slander, defamation, and lies, which belong to contaminated individuals; conversely, pure words belong to pure and God-fearing men. "From the vessel leaks only what is within it."

2- It is sometimes said that khabithat means "evil deeds" (sayyi'at) and absolute bad actions and unpleasant works, which are the program of impure men; conversely, "good deeds" (hasanat) belong to the pure.

3- Khabithat and khabithun refer to women and men of unchaste character, while tayyibat and tayyibun refer to chaste women and men. Apparently, this is the intended meaning of the verse, as there are clues that support this latter interpretation:

a) These verses follow the verses of Ifk and also the verse: "The fornicator does not marry except a fornicatress or a polytheist, and the fornicatress—none marries her except a fornicator or a polytheist, and that has been forbidden to the believers." This interpretation is consistent with the concept of those verses.

b) The phrase "They (the chaste women and men) are innocent of the false accusations made against them" (أُولئِكَ مُبَرَّؤُنَ مِمَّا يَقُولُونَ) is another clue for this interpretation.

c) Fundamentally, the principle of parallelism itself indicates that khabithat is a literal feminine plural referring to impure women, in contrast to khabithun, which is a literal masculine plural.

d) Beyond all this, a hadith is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام stating that "This verse is like 'The fornicator does not marry except a fornicatress or a polytheist,' for there were a group who decided to marry unchaste women, so Allah forbade them from this and deemed this act detestable." [^1]

e) In the narrations of the Book of Marriage (Kitab al-Nikah), we also read that the companions of the Imams would sometimes ask about marrying "evil" (khabithah) women and were met with a negative response. This itself shows that khabithah refers to impure women, not "words" or "actions." [^2]

Another question is whether the "evilness" or "goodness" of these men and women refers specifically to aspects of chastity and honor, or if it includes every kind of intellectual, practical, and verbal impurity.

If we consider the context of the verses and the narrations mentioned in its commentary, limiting the concept of the verse to the first meaning (chastity) seems more correct. However, it can be inferred from some narrations that "evil" and "good" here have a broad meaning, and the concept is not limited to sexual impurity or purity. From this perspective, it does not seem unlikely that the primary meaning of the verse is that specific sense, but in terms of its criteria, philosophy, and cause, it is capable of generalization and expansion.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed. [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 14, p. 337, Chapter 14 of the chapters on what is prohibited by marriage and the like.

In other words, the above verse actually expresses the inclination of homogeneity (sankhiyyat), although given the subject of discussion, it speaks of homogeneity in sexual purity and impurity (take note).

2- Is this a Creative (Takwini) or Legislative (Tashri'i) Decree?

Undoubtedly, the law that "beings of light seek beings of light" and "beings of fire attract beings of fire," and the famous proverb "birds of a feather flock together," as well as the well-known Arabic proverb "Homogeneity is the cause of union," all refer to a creative law (sunnat takwini) that encompasses every atom of existence in the earth and the heavens, which attract their own kind like straw and amber.

In any case, everywhere, like-minded individuals seek out their own kind, and every group and category is warm and intimate with those of their own nature.

However, this reality does not prevent the above verse—like the verse "The fornicatress—none marries her except a fornicator or a polytheist"—from referring to a legislative decree (hukm shar'i) that marriage with unchaste women is prohibited, at least in cases where they are notorious and well-known for acts contrary to chastity.

Do not all legislative decrees have creative roots? Are not the Divine laws in legislation and creation harmonized? (For further explanation, refer to the commentary we provided under the aforementioned verse).

3- Answering a Question

A question arises here: throughout history or in our own living environments, we sometimes see cases that do not align with this law. For example, the Quran itself mentions that the wife of Noah and the wife of Lot were bad women and betrayed them (Surah At-Tahrim, Verse 10), and conversely, the wife of Pharaoh was a faithful and chaste woman who was trapped in the clutches of that faithless tyrant (Surah At-Tahrim, Verse 11).

Regarding the great leaders of Islam, similar examples have been seen more or less, as the history of Islam bears witness.

In response, besides the fact that every general law has exceptions, two points must be noted:

1- In the commentary of the verse, we said that the primary meaning of "evilness" (khabathat) is contamination with acts contrary to chastity, and "goodness" (tayyib) is its opposite. Thus, the answer to the question becomes clear, because none of the wives of the Prophets and Imams certainly had any sexual deviation or impurity. The "betrayal" mentioned in the story of Noah and Lot refers to spying in favor of the disbelievers, not a betrayal of honor.

Fundamentally, this flaw is considered one of the "repulsive defects," and we know that the personal living environment of the Prophets must be free from attributes that cause people to be repulsed, so that the goal of prophethood—which is to attract people to the religion of God—is not frustrated.

2- Furthermore, the wives of the Prophets and Imams were not even disbelievers or faithless at the beginning; sometimes they were led astray after the prophethood, at which point the Prophets certainly did not continue their relationships with them as before. Similarly, when Pharaoh's wife married Pharaoh, she had not yet believed in Moses; in fact, Moses was not even born yet. Later, when Moses was commissioned, she believed and had no choice but to continue a life coupled with struggle—a struggle that ultimately ended in the martyrdom of this believing woman.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَدْخُلُوا۟ بُيُوتًا غَيْرَ بُيُوتِكُمْ حَتَّىٰ تَسْتَأْنِسُوا۟ وَتُسَلِّمُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٧)
O you who have faith! Do not enter houses other than your own until you have announced [your arrival] and greeted their occupants. That is better for you. Maybe you will take admonition. (27)
فَإِن لَّمْ تَجِدُوا۟ فِيهَآ أَحَدًا فَلَا تَدْخُلُوهَا حَتَّىٰ يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ ۖ وَإِن قِيلَ لَكُمُ ٱرْجِعُوا۟ فَٱرْجِعُوا۟ ۖ هُوَ أَزْكَىٰ لَكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ (٢٨)
But if you do not find anyone in them, do not enter them until you are given permission. And if you are told: ‘Turn back,’ then do turn back. That is more decent for you. Allah knows best what you do. (28)
لَّيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ بُيُوتًا غَيْرَ مَسْكُونَةٍ فِيهَا مَتَـٰعٌ لَّكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا تَكْتُمُونَ (٢٩)
There will be no sin upon you in entering [without announcing] uninhabited houses wherein you have goods belonging to you. Allah knows whatever you disclose and whatever you conceal. (29)

Commentary

Do Not Enter People's Houses Without Permission

In these verses, a portion of Islamic etiquette and social instructions closely related to the preservation of public chastity is explained, specifically regarding the manner of entering people's homes and how to obtain permission to enter.

First, it says: "O you who have believed, do not enter houses other than your own houses until you have ascertained welcome [asked permission] and greeted their inhabitants" (so that you inform them of your intention to enter beforehand and secure their consent) (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَدْخُلُوا بُيُوتًا غَيْرَ بُيُوتِكُمْ حَتَّىٰ تَسْتَأْنِسُوا وَتُسَلِّمُوا عَلَىٰ أَهْلِهَا).

"That is best for you; perhaps you will be reminded" (ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ).

It is interesting that the phrase "tasta’nisu" (تستانسوا) is used here instead of "tasta’dhinu" (تستاذنوا). While the latter merely means seeking permission, the former—derived from the root "uns" (انس)—implies seeking permission accompanied by affection, kindness, familiarity, and sincerity. It indicates that even the act of seeking permission must be completely polite, friendly, and devoid of any harshness.

Therefore, if we analyze this phrase, many of the etiquettes related to this subject are summarized within it. Its meaning is: do not shout, do not pound on the door loudly, do not seek permission with dry or offensive expressions, and when permission is granted, do not enter without a greeting (Salam)—a greeting that is a sign of peace and serenity, and a messenger of love and friendship.

It is noteworthy that Allah accompanies this ruling, whose humane and emotional aspects are clear, with two phrases: "That is best for you" and "perhaps you will be reminded." This itself is evidence that such rulings are rooted in the depths of human emotions, reason, and consciousness; if a person reflects a little upon them, they will realize that their own good and benefit lie therein.

In the following verse, this instruction is completed with another sentence: "And if you do not find anyone therein, do not enter them until permission has been given you" (فَإِنْ لَمْ تَجِدُوا فِيهَا أَحَدًا فَلَا تَدْخُلُوهَا حَتَّىٰ يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ). This expression might mean that sometimes there are people in the house, but the one who can give you permission—the person in authority or the master of the house—is not present; in this case, you do not have the right to enter.

Or it could mean that there is absolutely no one in the house, but the owner might be at a neighbor's house or nearby, and upon hearing the knocking or your voice, they may come and grant permission. In that case, you have the right to enter. In any case, the point is: do not enter without permission.

Then it adds: "And if it is said to you, 'Go back,' then go back; it is purer for you" (وَإِنْ قِيلَ لَكُمُ ارْجِعُوا فَارْجِعُوا ۖ هُوَ أَزْكَىٰ لَكُمْ).

This indicates that a refusal should never upset you. Perhaps the homeowner is in a state where seeing you would cause them discomfort, or perhaps their situation or the state of their home is not ready to receive guests. Since hearing a negative response sometimes triggers curiosity in some people, leading them to try to discover the secrets of the house through door cracks or by eavesdropping, the end of the verse states:

"And Allah is Knowing of what you do" (وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ).

And since every rule has exceptions through which necessities and problems are reasonably resolved, the final verse under discussion states: "There is no blame upon you for entering houses not inhabited in which there is convenience [property] for you" (لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَنْ تَدْخُلُوا بُيُوتًا غَيْرَ مَسْكُونَةٍ فِيهَا مَتَاعٌ لَكُمْ).

And at the end, it adds: "And Allah knows what you reveal and what you conceal" (وَاللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا تَكْتُمُونَ).

This is perhaps an allusion to the fact that some individuals might abuse this exception, entering uninhabited houses under the pretext of this ruling to uncover secrets, or entering inhabited houses claiming they did not know they were occupied. However, Allah is aware of all these matters and knows the abusers well.

Key Points

1- Security and Freedom within the Home Environment

Undoubtedly, human existence has two dimensions: the individual and the social. For this reason, there are two types of life: private and public, each having its own characteristics, etiquettes, and regulations.

A person is forced to endure many constraints in the social environment regarding clothing, manner of movement, and social interaction. However, it is clear that continuing this state throughout the entire twenty-four hours of the day is exhausting and troublesome.

[Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 14; p. 430]

He wishes to be free for a portion of the day and night, to cast off constraints, to rest, to engage in private conversations with his family and children, and to benefit from this freedom as much as possible. For this reason, he takes refuge in his private home, and by closing the doors to others, he temporarily separates his life from society, thereby becoming free from the multitude of constraints he was forced to impose upon himself in the social environment.

Now, in this free environment, with this clear philosophy, there must be sufficient security. If everyone were to enter this environment unannounced and violate its secure sanctuary, that freedom, rest, and peace would no longer exist, and it would turn into the environment of the street and the market.

For this reason, there have always been specific regulations among humans in this regard. In all the laws of the world, entering people's houses without their permission is prohibited and punishable. Even where necessity—for the sake of maintaining security or other aspects—requires entering without permission, only limited and specific authorities have the right to grant such permission.

In Islam, very emphatic instructions have been given in this regard, and there are etiquettes and nuances that are rarely seen elsewhere.

In a Hadith, we read: "Abu Sa'id," one of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, sought permission to enter while standing directly in front of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله door. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "When seeking permission, do not stand directly in front of the door!" In another narration, we read that the Prophet himself, when he came to someone's door, would not stand directly in front of it but would stand to the right or left and say: "As-Salamu Alaykum" (and thus seek permission to enter), because in those days, it was not yet customary to hang curtains in front of doors [1]. We even read in Islamic narrations that a person should seek permission when they want to enter the house of their mother, father, or even their own child.

In a narration, it is mentioned that a man asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Should I seek permission when I want to enter my mother's house?" He replied: "Yes." The man said: "My mother has no servant other than me; must I still seek permission?" He replied: "Would you like to see her naked?" He said: "No." The Prophet said: "Then seek permission from her!" [2]. In another narration, we read that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله wanted to enter the house of his daughter Fatima عليه السلام, he first came to the door, placed his hand on it, and pushed it back slightly. Then he said: "As-Salamu Alaykum." Fatima عليه السلام replied to her father's greeting. Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Do I have permission to enter?" She said: "Enter, O Messenger of Allah!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Does the one with me also have permission to enter?" Fatima said: "I do not have a head-covering (miqna'ah) on." When she had covered herself with the Islamic Hijab, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله greeted again, and Fatima عليه السلام replied. He again sought permission for himself, and after Fatima's عليه السلام affirmative response, he sought permission for his companion, Jabir ibn Abdullah [3]. This Hadith clearly shows to what extent the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, who was a model and example for all Muslims, strictly observed these points.

[1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Vol. 23, p. 198, under the verse in question. [2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 586. [3]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 587.

In some narrations, we even read that permission should be sought three times: the first so they hear, the second so they prepare themselves, and the third so they may grant permission if they wish, or refuse if they wish! [1] Some have even considered it necessary for there to be an interval between these three permissions, because sometimes the homeowner is not wearing suitable clothing, or is in a state where they do not want anyone to see them, or the room is in disarray, or there are secrets they do not want others to know. They must be given a chance to compose themselves, and if they do not grant permission, one must turn away without the slightest feeling of resentment.

2- What is meant by "Uninhabited Houses"?

There is discussion among commentators regarding the answer to this question. Some have said: it refers to buildings where no specific person resides, but rather have a public and communal nature, such as caravanserais, inns, as well as public baths and the like—this meaning is explicitly stated in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام [2]. Others have interpreted it as ruins that have no doors or walls and anyone can enter; this interpretation seems very unlikely, as no one would be willing to leave their belongings in such a house.

Others consider it a reference to the warehouses of merchants and shops where people's goods are kept as a trust, and the owner of any goods has the right to go there to retrieve their property.

[1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 14, p. 161—Abwab Muqaddimat al-Nikah, Chapter 123. [2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 14, p. 161.

This interpretation also does not align well with the apparent meaning of the verse.

There is also the possibility that it refers to houses that have no inhabitants but where a person has left their belongings in trust, having obtained the implicit consent of the owner to check on or retrieve the belongings at the time of leaving them.

Of course, some of these interpretations are not mutually exclusive, but the first interpretation is more consistent with the meaning of the verse.

Furthermore, it becomes clear from this explanation that a person cannot open the door and enter a house without the owner's permission simply because they have belongings there, even if no one is in the house at that time.

3- Punishment for One Who Looks into People's Houses Without Permission

It is stated in books of jurisprudence and Hadith that if someone intentionally looks into people's houses and gazes at the faces or naked bodies of women, the inhabitants can forbid him the first time. If he does not desist, they can drive him away with stones. If he still persists, they can defend themselves and their honor with lethal instruments, and if the intruder and aggressor is killed in this confrontation, his blood is wasted (no retaliation or blood money is due). Of course, when preventing this act, they must observe the hierarchy of response—that is, as long as it is possible through easier means, they should not resort to harsher ones.

Ayahs

قُل لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا۟ مِنْ أَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ وَيَحْفَظُوا۟ فُرُوجَهُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ أَزْكَىٰ لَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَصْنَعُونَ (٣٠)
Tell the faithful men to cast down their looks and to guard their private parts. That is more decent for them. Allah is indeed well aware of what they do. (30)
وَقُل لِّلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ يَغْضُضْنَ مِنْ أَبْصَـٰرِهِنَّ وَيَحْفَظْنَ فُرُوجَهُنَّ وَلَا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَّ إِلَّا مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا ۖ وَلْيَضْرِبْنَ بِخُمُرِهِنَّ عَلَىٰ جُيُوبِهِنَّ ۖ وَلَا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَّ إِلَّا لِبُعُولَتِهِنَّ أَوْ ءَابَآئِهِنَّ أَوْ ءَابَآءِ بُعُولَتِهِنَّ أَوْ أَبْنَآئِهِنَّ أَوْ أَبْنَآءِ بُعُولَتِهِنَّ أَوْ إِخْوَٰنِهِنَّ أَوْ بَنِىٓ إِخْوَٰنِهِنَّ أَوْ بَنِىٓ أَخَوَٰتِهِنَّ أَوْ نِسَآئِهِنَّ أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُنَّ أَوِ ٱلتَّـٰبِعِينَ غَيْرِ أُو۟لِى ٱلْإِرْبَةِ مِنَ ٱلرِّجَالِ أَوِ ٱلطِّفْلِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَظْهَرُوا۟ عَلَىٰ عَوْرَٰتِ ٱلنِّسَآءِ ۖ وَلَا يَضْرِبْنَ بِأَرْجُلِهِنَّ لِيُعْلَمَ مَا يُخْفِينَ مِن زِينَتِهِنَّ ۚ وَتُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ جَمِيعًا أَيُّهَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٣١)
And tell the faithful women to cast down their looks and to guard their private parts, and not to display their charms, beyond what is [acceptably] visible, and let them draw their scarfs over their bosoms, and not display their charms except to their husbands, or their fathers, or their husband’s fathers, or their sons, or their husband’s sons, or their brothers, or their brothers’ sons, or their sisters’ sons, or their women, or their slave girls, or male dependants lacking [sexual] desire, or children who are not yet conscious of female sexuality. And let them not thump their feet to make known their hidden ornaments. Rally to Allah in repentance, O faithful, so that you may be felicitous. (31)

Occasion of Revelation

In the book al-Kafi, regarding the occasion of revelation for the first of the aforementioned verses, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that a young man from the Ansar met a woman on his path—at that time, women used to place their head-coverings (miqna'ah) behind their ears (consequently, their necks and a portion of their chests would be visible). The woman's face attracted the young man's attention, and he fixed his gaze upon her. As the woman passed by, the young man continued to follow her with his eyes while walking forward until he entered a narrow alley. Still looking back at her, his face suddenly struck a wall, and a sharp bone or piece of glass protruding from the wall slashed his face! When the woman had passed, the young man came to his senses and saw blood flowing down his face, onto his clothes and chest. (Deeply distressed), he said to himself: "By Allah, I shall go to the Prophet and recount this incident." When the eyes of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله fell upon him, He asked: "What has happened?"

The young man recounted the story. At that moment, Jibril, the messenger of Allah's revelation, descended and brought the aforementioned verse: (قُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ‌ ...) [1]

Commentary

Combating Lustful Glances and the Abandonment of Hijab

We have previously stated that this Surah is, in truth, the Surah of chastity, purity, and cleansing from sexual deviations. Its various discussions possess a clear cohesion from this perspective. The verses under discussion, which state the rulings on looking, lustful glances, and Hijab, are also completely related to this matter. Furthermore, the connection between this discussion and the discussions regarding accusations against honor is hidden from no one.

First, it says: "Tell the believing men to lower their gaze (from looking at non-mahram women and that which is forbidden to look upon), and to guard their private parts" (قُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ وَ يَحْفَظُوا فُرُوجَهُمْ‌).

"Yaghuddu" is derived from the root "ghadd," which originally means to reduce or diminish. In many instances, it is used for lowering the voice or reducing the gaze. Therefore, the verse does not say that believers must close their eyes, but rather that they must reduce and shorten their gaze. This is a subtle expression; for if a person truly wanted to close their eyes completely when encountering a non-mahram woman, continuing to walk and the like would be impossible for them. However, if they avert their gaze from her face and body and cast their eyes downward, it is as if they have reduced their looking and completely removed that forbidden scene from their field of vision.

It is noteworthy that the Quran does not specify what they should lower their gaze from (omitting the object of the verb), which serves as an indication of generality; meaning they should avert their eyes from observing everything that is forbidden to look at.

However, considering the context of the verses, especially the following verse which speaks of the issue of Hijab, it becomes quite clear that the intention is not looking at non-mahram women. The occasion of revelation mentioned above also confirms this point. [1] From what we have said, it becomes clear that the concept of the above verse is not that men should not stare at the faces of women—as some might infer that non-staring glances are permissible—but rather the intention is that when looking, a person usually takes in a wide area. Whenever a non-mahram woman enters his field of vision, he should lower his gaze such that the woman is excluded from his field of vision; meaning he should not look at her, yet still see his path. This is what is meant by "ghadd" being defined as "reduction" (reflect on this).

The second command in the above verse is the issue of "guarding the private parts" (hifz al-furuj).

"Farj," as we have said before, originally means a "gap" or the distance between two things, but in these contexts, it is an allusion to the private parts. To preserve its allusive meaning in Persian [and English], we use the word "daman" [or "chastity/modesty"].

The meaning of "guarding the private parts," as stated in the narrations, is covering them from the sight of others. In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Every verse in the Quran that mentions guarding the private parts refers to guarding against adultery (zina), except for this verse, where it refers to guarding against the gaze of others." [1] And since it might sometimes be asked why Islam has prohibited this act—which is in harmony with the lust and desire of many hearts—at the end of the verse, He says: "That is purer for them" (ذلِكَ أَزْكى‌ لَهُمْ‌).

Then, as a warning to those who cast lustful and intentional glances at non-mahram women and sometimes portray it as involuntary, He says: "Indeed, Allah is Aware of what you do" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ خَبِيرٌ بِما يَصْنَعُونَ‌).

In the next verse, He addresses the duties of women in this regard. First, He mentions duties similar to those of men, saying: "And tell the believing women to lower their gaze (and refrain from looking at non-mahram men) and to guard their private parts" (وَ قُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِناتِ يَغْضُضْنَ مِنْ أَبْصارِهِنَّ وَ يَحْفَظْنَ فُرُوجَهُنَ‌). Thus, "lustful glancing" is forbidden for women just as it is for men, and covering the private parts from the gaze of others—whether from men or women—is obligatory for women just as it is for men.

Then, He refers to the issue of Hijab, which is specific to women, within three phrases:

1- "They should not display their adornment except that which is apparent thereof" (وَ لا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَّ إِلَّا ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها).

There is much discussion among commentators regarding what is meant by the "adornment" (zinat) that women must cover, as well as the "apparent adornment" which they are permitted to display.

Some have taken "hidden adornment" to mean natural adornment (the beautiful body of the woman), while the word "zinat" is rarely applied to this meaning.

Others have taken it to mean the "place of adornment," because displaying the jewelry itself, such as earrings, bracelets, and armlets, is not prohibited on its own. If there is a prohibition, it relates to the place of these adornments—namely the ears, neck, hands, and arms.

Others have taken it to mean the "jewelry" itself, but while it is placed on the body; naturally, displaying such adornment is accompanied by displaying the body part upon which the adornment rests.

(These last two interpretations are identical in their result, though the issue is approached from two different paths).

The truth is that we should interpret the verse without prejudice and according to its literal meaning, which is the third meaning. Therefore, women do not have the right to display adornments that are usually hidden, even if their limbs are not visible. Thus, displaying special decorative clothes worn under the ordinary dress or chador is not permitted, as the Quran has forbidden the displaying of such adornments.

In numerous narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, this same meaning is seen, where "inner adornment" is interpreted as the "necklace," "armlet," and "anklet" (the ornament Arab women wore on their ankles). [1] Since in many other narrations "apparent adornment" is interpreted as the ring, kohl, and the like, we understand that "inner adornment" also refers to the ornaments themselves that are hidden and covered (reflect on this).

2- The second ruling stated in the verse is: "And they should draw their head-coverings over their bosoms" (وَ لْيَضْرِبْنَ بِخُمُرِهِنَّ عَلى‌ جُيُوبِهِنَ‌).

"Khumur" is the plural of "khimar," which originally means a covering, but it is usually applied to that which women use to cover their heads (headscarf).

"Juyub" is the plural of "jayb," meaning the collar of a shirt, which is referred to as the neckline, and sometimes it is applied to the upper part of the chest due to its proximity.

From this phrase, it is understood that before the revelation of the verse, women used to throw the ends of their headscarves behind their shoulders or the back of their heads, such that their necks and a portion of their chests were visible. The Quran commands them to cast their headscarves over their necklines so that both the neck and that part of the chest which is exposed are covered. (This meaning is also clearly derived from the occasion of revelation mentioned earlier).

3- In the third ruling, He explains the instances where women can remove their Hijab and display their hidden adornment with this expression:

"They should not display their adornment (وَ لا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَ‌) except (in twelve cases):

1- To their husbands" (إِلَّا لِبُعُولَتِهِنَ‌). 2- "Or their fathers" (أَوْ آبائِهِنَ‌). 3- "Or their husbands' fathers" (أَوْ آباءِ بُعُولَتِهِنَ‌). 4- "Or their sons" (أَوْ أَبْنائِهِنَ‌). 5- "Or their husbands' sons" (أَوْ أَبْناءِ بُعُولَتِهِنَ‌). 6- "Or their brothers" (أَوْ إِخْوانِهِنَ‌). 7- "Or their brothers' sons" (أَوْ بَنِي إِخْوانِهِنَ‌). 8- "Or their sisters' sons" (أَوْ بَنِي أَخَواتِهِنَ‌). 9- "Or their women (fellow Muslim women)" (أَوْ نِسائِهِنَ‌). 10- "Or their slaves" (their female servants) (أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُنَ‌). 11- "Or male attendants who lack desire" (feeble-minded or simple individuals who possess no sexual desire) (أَوِ التَّابِعِينَ غَيْرِ أُولِي الْإِرْبَةِ مِنَ الرِّجالِ‌). 12- "Or children who are not yet aware of the private aspects of women (sexual matters)" (أَوِ الطِّفْلِ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يَظْهَرُوا عَلى‌ عَوْراتِ النِّساءِ).

4- Finally, He states the fourth ruling as follows: "And let them not stamp their feet so as to make known what they hide of their adornment" (and the sound of the anklets they wear be heard) (وَ لا يَضْرِبْنَ بِأَرْجُلِهِنَّ لِيُعْلَمَ ما يُخْفِينَ مِنْ زِينَتِهِنَ‌).

They must be so meticulous and strict in observing chastity and avoiding matters that inflame the fire of lust in the hearts of men—which might lead to deviation from the path of modesty—that they should even refrain from letting the sound of the anklets on their feet reach the ears of stranger men. This is a testament to the precision of Islam in this field.

And finally, He ends the verse with a general invitation to all believers, both men and women, to repent and return to Allah, saying: "And turn to Allah in repentance, all of you, O believers, that you may succeed" (وَ تُوبُوا إِلَى اللَّهِ جَمِيعاً أَيُّهَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ‌).

If in the past you have committed wrong acts in this regard, now that the truths of Islamic rulings have been clarified for you, repent for your mistakes and come toward Allah for salvation and success, for prosperity is found only at His door. There are dangerous pitfalls in your path from which salvation is impossible except by His grace; entrust yourselves to Him! It is true that before the revelation of these rulings, sin and disobedience regarding these matters had no definition, but we know that a portion of the issues related to sexual impurities has a rational aspect and is, in technical terms, among the "independent dictates of reason" (mustaqillat al-aqliyyah), where the judgment of reason alone is sufficient to create responsibility.

Key Points

1- The Philosophy of Hijab

Undoubtedly, in our era—which some have named the era of nakedness and sexual freedom, and where Westernized individuals consider the lack of restraint in women as part of her freedom—speaking of Hijab is unpleasant for this group and sometimes seen as a myth belonging to past times! However, the countless corruptions and the ever-increasing problems and difficulties arising from these unconditional freedoms have caused a listening ear for this discourse to gradually emerge.

Of course, in Islamic and religious environments, especially in the environment of Iran after the Islamic Republic revolution, many issues have been resolved, and sufficient and convincing answers have been practically given to many of these questions. Yet, the importance of the subject necessitates that this issue be discussed more extensively.

The question is whether women (with utmost apologies) should be at the disposal of all men for exploitation through hearing, sight, and touch (excluding sexual intercourse), or should these matters be exclusive to their husbands?

The discussion is whether women should be involved in an endless competition of showing their bodies and stimulating the lusts and polluted desires of men, or should these issues be removed from the social environment and dedicated to the environment of the family and marital life?! Islam favors the second program, and Hijab is considered a part of this program, while Westerners and lustful Westernized people favor the first! Islam says that sexual gratifications—including intercourse and pleasures of hearing, sight, and touch—are exclusive to spouses, and anything else is a sin and a source of pollution and impurity for society, to which the phrase "That is purer for them" in the above verses refers.

The philosophy of Hijab is not something hidden or secret because:

1- The nakedness of women, which naturally brings consequences such as makeup, coquetry, and the like, keeps men—especially the youth—in a state of constant stimulation. This stimulation causes the crushing of their nerves, the creation of morbid nervous excitements, and sometimes becomes the source of psychological illnesses. How much excitement can human nerves carry? Do not all psychiatrists say that continuous excitement is a factor of illness?

Especially considering that the sexual instinct is the most powerful and deep-rooted human instinct and has been the source of deadly events and horrific crimes throughout history, to the extent that it has been said: "You will find no important event unless a woman's hand is involved in it"! Is the constant fanning of this instinct through nakedness and inflaming it not playing with fire? Is this a wise thing to do?

Islam wants Muslim men and women to have a calm soul, healthy nerves, and pure eyes and ears, and this is one of the philosophies of Hijab.

2- Definitive and documented statistics show that with the increase of nakedness in the world, divorce and the disintegration of marital life have continuously risen. For "whatever the eye sees, the heart remembers," and whatever the "heart"—meaning rebellious desires—wants, it pursues at any cost. Thus, every day the heart attaches to one beauty and bids farewell to another.

In an environment where there is Hijab (and other Islamic conditions are observed), the two spouses belong to each other, and their feelings, love, and affections are exclusive to one another.

But in the "free market of nakedness," where women have practically become a common commodity (at least in the stage prior to sexual intercourse), the sanctity of the marriage covenant can no longer have meaning, and families disintegrate as quickly as a spider's web, leaving children without guardians.

3- The expansion of the scope of vice and the increase of illegitimate children are among the most painful consequences of the lack of Hijab, which we believe requires no figures or statistics, and its reasons are quite apparent, especially in Western societies. It is so evident that there is no need for explanation.

We do not say that the primary factor of vice and illegitimate children is exclusively the lack of Hijab; we do not say that shameful colonialism and destructive political issues are not influential in it. Rather, we say that one of its effective factors is the issue of nakedness and the lack of Hijab.

And considering that "vice" and, worse than that, "illegitimate children" have been and are the source of various crimes in human societies, the dangerous dimensions of this issue become clearer.

When we hear that in England, according to statistics, five hundred thousand illegitimate babies are born every year, and when we hear that a group of British scientists has warned the authorities of that country in this regard—not because of moral or religious issues, but because of the dangers that illegitimate children have created for the security of society, such that they are involved in many criminal cases—we fully realize the importance of this issue. We know that the issue of the spread of vice is catastrophic even for those who place no importance on religion and moral programs. Therefore, anything that expands the scope of sexual corruption in human societies is considered a threat to the security of societies, and its consequences, however we calculate them, are to the detriment of that society.

Studies by educational scientists have also shown that in schools where boys and girls study together, and in centers where men and women work together and lack of restraint prevails in their interaction, low productivity, backwardness, and lack of responsibility are clearly observed.

4- The issue of the "trivialization of woman" and the "fall of her personality" is also of great importance here and needs no figures or statistics. When society wants a woman with a naked body, it is natural that day by day it demands more makeup and increased self-display from her. When woman is made a means of advertising goods and a decoration for waiting rooms through her sexual attraction, and a factor for attracting tourists and travelers and the like, in such a society, the personality of the woman falls to the level of a doll or a worthless commodity. Her high human values are completely forgotten, and her only pride becomes her youth, beauty, and self-display.

Thus, she will be transformed into a means for satisfying the rebellious lusts of a handful of polluted deceivers and demon-like humanoids! In such a society, how can a woman manifest through her moral characteristics, her science, awareness, and wisdom, and attain a high status?! Truly, it is painful that in Western and Westernized countries, and in our country before the Islamic Revolution, the most name, fame, money, income, and position were for polluted and unrestrained women who were known as "artists and actresses." Wherever they stepped, the managers of this polluted environment would scramble for them and welcome their arrival! Thanks be to Allah that those trappings were dismantled, and woman emerged from her former state of triviality and the position of a foreign doll and worthless commodity, regaining her personality. She donned the Hijab, but without becoming isolated; she appeared in all useful and constructive social scenes, even on the battlefield, with that same Islamic Hijab.

These were some of the living and clear philosophies of the subject of Hijab in Islam, suitable for this interpretative discussion.

Criticisms of the Opponents of Hijab

Here we come to the objections raised by the opponents of Hijab, which must be briefly examined:

1- The most important thing they all agree upon and mention as a fundamental objection to the issue of Hijab is that women constitute half of society, but Hijab causes the isolation of this vast population. Naturally, it pushes them back intellectually and culturally. Especially in the era of economic flourishing when there is a great need for active human force, no use will be made of the power of women in the economic movement, and their place in cultural and social centers will also be empty! Thus, they become consuming beings and a burden on society.

But those who resort to this logic have either completely overlooked or feigned ignorance of several matters.

Because:

Firstly: Who said that Islamic Hijab isolates woman and distances her from the social scene? If in the past it was necessary for us to take the trouble of arguing this point, today, after the Islamic Revolution, there is no need for argument. For with our own eyes, we see groups upon groups of women who, while having Islamic Hijab, are present everywhere: in offices, in workshops, in marches and political demonstrations, on radio and television, in hospitals and health centers—especially in medical care for the war-wounded—in culture and university, and finally, on the scene of war and struggle against the enemy.

In short, the current situation is a crushing response to all these objections. If we previously spoke of the "possibility" of such a situation, today we are faced with its "occurrence," and philosophers have said that the best proof for the possibility of something is its occurrence. This is an evident reality that needs no explanation.

Secondly: Aside from this, is managing the home, raising noble children, and building humans who can move the great wheels of society in the future with their powerful arms not "work"?

Those who do not consider this great mission of woman as positive work are unaware of the role of family and upbringing in building a healthy, prosperous, and dynamic society. They imagine that the way is for our men and women, like Western men and women, to leave the house early in the morning for offices and factories and the like, and entrust their children to nurseries, or leave them in a room and lock the door on them, making them taste the bitter taste of prison from the time they are unblossomed buds.

They are unaware that by this act, they crush their personalities and raise soulless children lacking human affections who will endanger the future of society.

2- Another objection they have is that Hijab is a cumbersome garment and is not compatible with social activities, especially in the age of modern machines. Should a woman with Hijab protect herself, or her chador, or her child, or her program?! But these objectors overlook one point: Hijab does not always mean a chador; rather, it means the covering of the woman. Where it is possible with a chador, all the better; and where it is not, one settles for the covering.

Our female farmers and villagers, especially the women who undertake the most important and difficult work of planting and harvesting the rice crop in the paddies, have practically answered these notions. They have shown that a village woman, while having Islamic Hijab, works in many cases even more and better than a man, without her Hijab hindering her work.

3- Another objection is that they say Hijab, by creating a distance between men and women, makes the greedy nature of men more covetous. Instead of being an extinguisher, it inflames the fire of their greed further, for "Man is greedy for what is forbidden"! The answer to this objection—or more correctly, this sophistry and fallacy—is provided by comparing our society today, where Hijab prevails in almost all centers without exception, with the era of the Taghut regime when women were forced to remove their Hijab.

In those days, every street and corner was a center of corruption. A strange lack of restraint prevailed in families, divorce statistics were exceptionally high, the level of illegitimate births was up, and there were thousands of other miseries.

We do not say that today all of these have been uprooted, but undoubtedly they have greatly decreased, and our society has regained its health in this regard. If, by Allah's will, the situation continues in this manner and other disorders are also set right, our society will reach the desired stage in terms of the purity of families and the preservation of woman's value.

2- The Exception of the Face and Hands

There is much discussion among jurists as to whether the ruling of Hijab includes the face and the hands up to the wrists or not. Many believe that covering these two (the face and hands) is exempt from the ruling of Hijab, while a group has issued a fatwa on the obligation of covering them, or at least observe precaution. Of course, those who do not consider covering these two as obligatory also condition it on the situation not being a source of corruption or deviation; otherwise, it is obligatory.

In the above verse, there are indications for this exception and support for the first view, including:

A: The exception of "apparent adornment" in the above verse—whether it means the place of adornment or the adornment itself—is a clear proof that covering the face and hands is not necessary.

B: The command given in the above verse regarding casting the corner of the head-covering over the neckline—the concept of which is covering the entire head, neck, and chest, and there is no mention of covering the face in it—is another indication for this claim.

To explain: As we said in the occasion of revelation, Arabs at that time wore a headscarf or head-covering whose ends they threw over their shoulders and the back of their heads, such that the head-covering was placed behind their ears and only covered the head and the back of the neck. However, the part under the throat and a bit of the chest above the neckline were visible. Islam came and corrected this situation, commanding that the ends of the head-covering be brought forward from behind the ears or the back of the head and cast over the neckline and chest. The result was that only the circle of the face remained, and the rest was covered.

C: Numerous narrations have also been recorded in this regard in Islamic sources and books of Hadith, which are a living witness to the claim [1], although there are conflicting narrations that are not as explicit as these. Reconciling them is quite possible by considering the covering of the face and hands as recommended (mustahabb), or applying them to cases that are a source of corruption and deviation.

Historical evidence also shows that wearing a veil (niqab) over the face was not a general practice in the early days of Islam (more detail regarding the jurisprudential and narrative discussion of this issue is found in the discussions of marriage in jurisprudence).

But again, we emphasize and repeat that this ruling applies as long as it does not lead to abuse and deviation.

It is also necessary to mention that the exception of the face and hands from the ruling of Hijab does not mean that it is permissible for others to look intentionally; rather, it is actually a type of facilitation for women in the affairs of life.

3- What is meant by "Nisa'ihinn"?

As we read in the commentary of the verse, the ninth group that is exempted and before whom a woman has the right to display her inner adornment are "women." However, considering the expression "Nisa'ihinn" (their women), it is inferred that Muslim women can only remove their Hijab before Muslim women, but before non-Muslim women, they must maintain Islamic Hijab. The philosophy of this matter, as stated in narrations, is that they might go and describe what they have seen to their husbands, and this is not proper for Muslim women.

In a narration found in the book Man La Yahduruhu al-Faqih, we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"It is not appropriate for a Muslim woman to uncover herself before a Jewish or Christian woman, for they will describe that to their husbands." [1]

4- Commentary on the phrase: (أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُنَ‌)

The literal meaning of this phrase has a broad concept and indicates that a woman can appear without Hijab before her slave. However, in some Islamic narrations, it is specified that the intention is appearing before female slaves, even if they are non-Muslim, and it does not include male slaves. In a Hadith from Imam Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام, we read that he said:

"A male slave should not look at the hair of the woman who is his mistress." [1] However, from some other narrations, a general application is inferred, but it is certainly contrary to precaution.

5- Commentary on: (أُولِي الْإِرْبَةِ مِنَ الرِّجالِ‌)

"Irbah" is originally from the root "arab"—as Raghib says in al-Mufradat—meaning intense need for which a person seeks a remedy. Sometimes it is also used for need in an absolute sense.

The meaning of "uli al-irbah min al-rijal" here refers to those who have sexual desire and a need for a spouse. Therefore, "ghayr uli al-irbah" includes those in whom this desire does not exist.

There is discussion among commentators as to who is meant by this title:

Some have taken it to mean old men whose sexual lust has been extinguished, like "al-qawa'id min al-nisa'" (women who have passed the age of marriage and have retired in this regard).

Others have interpreted it as eunuchs (khasi).
