And others have interpreted it as hermaphrodites (khuntha) who lack male organs entirely.

However, what can be relied upon most, and which has been narrated in several authentic Hadiths from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, is that this expression refers to simple-minded men who have no sexual feeling whatsoever and are usually used for simple tasks and service. The expression "al-tabi'in" (attendants/followers) also reinforces this meaning. [2]

However, since this description—namely the lack of sexual desire—is true for a group of the elderly, it is not unlikely that we expand the concept of the verse, and this group of old men would also be included in the meaning of the verse.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Kazim عليه السلام, this group of old men is also emphasized.

But in any case, the concept of the verse is not that this group of men are like mahrams; what is certain is that covering the head or a bit of the hands and the like is not obligatory before this group.

6- Which children are excluded from this ruling?

We said that the twelfth group before whom Hijab is not obligatory are children who do not yet have any share of sexual lust.

The phrase "lam yazharu" has sometimes been interpreted as "lam yattali'u" (they are not aware) and sometimes as "lam yaqdiru" (they are not capable), because this root has come with both meanings and is used in the Quran sometimes for one and sometimes for the other.

For example, in verse 20 of Surah al-Kahf, we read: "Indeed, if they come to know of you (yazharu 'alaykum), they will stone you."

And in verse 8 of Surah al-Tawbah, we read: "How [can there be a treaty] while, if they gain dominance over you (yazharu 'alaykum), they do not observe concerning you any tie of kinship or covenant of protection?"

But in any case, this difference in the verse under discussion does not make much difference in terms of the result. The intention is children who, due to a lack of sexual feeling, have neither the capability nor the awareness.

Therefore, children who have reached an age where this desire and capability have awakened in them, Muslim ladies must observe Hijab before them.

7- Why are the paternal and maternal uncles not mentioned among the mahrams?

Among the questions raised is that in the above verse, while stating the mahrams, there is no mention whatsoever of the paternal uncle ('am) and maternal uncle (khal), even though they are certainly mahrams and Hijab is not necessary before them.

The point may be that the Quran wants to use the utmost eloquence and rhetoric in expressing matters and not say even one extra word. Since the exception of the "brother's son" and "sister's son" shows that a person's "paternal aunt" and "maternal aunt" are mahram to him, it becomes clear that a woman's paternal and maternal uncles are also mahram to her. In clearer terms, mahram status is reciprocal; when on one side a person's sister's and brother's children become mahram to him, it is natural that on the other side and the opposite direction, the paternal and maternal uncles are also mahram (reflect on this).

8- All types of stimulating factors are forbidden!

The final word in this discussion is that at the end of the above verse, it is stated that women should not stamp their feet when walking so that the sound of their anklets is heard! This matter shows that Islam is so strict and meticulous in matters related to public chastity that it does not even allow such an act. Naturally, by way of priority (a fortiori), it will not allow various factors that fan the fire of the youth's lust, such as the publication of provocative photos, seductive films, and sexual novels and stories. Undoubtedly, the Islamic environment must be pure and free from these kinds of issues that drive customers to centers of corruption and lead young boys and girls into pollution and vice.

Ayahs

وَأَنكِحُوا۟ ٱلْأَيَـٰمَىٰ مِنكُمْ وَٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ مِنْ عِبَادِكُمْ وَإِمَآئِكُمْ ۚ إِن يَكُونُوا۟ فُقَرَآءَ يُغْنِهِمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٣٢)
Marry off those who are single among you, and the upright among your male and female slaves. If they are poor, Allah will enrich them out of His grace, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. (32)
وَلْيَسْتَعْفِفِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَجِدُونَ نِكَاحًا حَتَّىٰ يُغْنِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ يَبْتَغُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِمَّا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ فَكَاتِبُوهُمْ إِنْ عَلِمْتُمْ فِيهِمْ خَيْرًا ۖ وَءَاتُوهُم مِّن مَّالِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ ۚ وَلَا تُكْرِهُوا۟ فَتَيَـٰتِكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْبِغَآءِ إِنْ أَرَدْنَ تَحَصُّنًا لِّتَبْتَغُوا۟ عَرَضَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَمَن يُكْرِههُّنَّ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ إِكْرَٰهِهِنَّ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٣٣)
Those who cannot afford marriage should be continent until Allah enriches them out of His grace. As for those who seek an emancipation deal from among your slaves, make such a deal with them if you know any good in them, and give them out of the wealth of Allah which He has given you. Do not compel your female slaves to prostitution when they desire to be chaste, seeking the transitory wares of the life of this world. Should anyone compel them, Allah will be forgiving and merciful to them following their compulsion. (33)
وَلَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتٍ مُّبَيِّنَـٰتٍ وَمَثَلًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَمَوْعِظَةً لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (٣٤)
Certainly We have sent down to you manifest signs and a description of those who passed before you, and an advice for the Godwary. (34)

Commentary

Encouraging Simple Marriage

From the beginning of this Surah until this point, various calculated methods for preventing sexual impurity have been presented, each of which plays a significant role in pre-empting or combating these corruptions.

In the verses under discussion, another of the most important methods for fighting indecency is mentioned: simple, easy, sincere, and unpretentious marriage. This is because it is an established fact that to dismantle the foundations of sin, one must approach through the correct and legitimate satisfaction of instincts. In other words, no "negative struggle" will be effective without a "positive struggle."

Therefore, in the first verse under discussion, He says: "And marry off the single among you and the righteous among your male slaves and female slaves" (وَ أَنْكِحُوا الْأَيامى‌ مِنْكُمْ وَ الصَّالِحِينَ مِنْ عِبادِكُمْ وَ إِمائِكُمْ‌).

"Ayama" (أَيامى‌) is the plural of "Aym" (ايم), which originally referred to a woman without a husband, and was later applied to a man without a wife as well. Thus, all single men and women are included in the concept of this verse, whether they are virgins or widowed.

The expression "Ankihu" (أَنْكِحُوا - marry them off), despite marriage being a voluntary matter dependent on the will of both parties, implies that you should provide the preliminaries for their marriage. This includes financial assistance if needed, finding a suitable spouse, encouraging the matter of marriage, and finally, mediating to solve the problems that usually cannot be resolved in such cases without the intervention of others. In short, the concept of the verse is so broad that it encompasses every step, word, and penny spent in this path.

Undoubtedly, the principle of Islamic cooperation requires Muslims to help one another in all fields, but the explicit mention of this regarding marriage indicates its special importance.

The importance of this issue is to such an extent that we read in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام: "The best of intercessions is that you intercede between two people regarding marriage until Allah joins them together!" [^1]

In another Hadith from Imam Kadhim, Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام, we read: "There are three groups who will be under the shade of Allah's Throne on the Day of Resurrection, a day when there is no shade but His shade: a man who provides the means for his Muslim brother to marry, or provides a servant for him when he needs service, or conceals a secret for his Muslim brother." [^1]

And finally, in a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "For every step a person takes in this path and every word he speaks, the reward of one year of worship is written in his record of deeds." (كان له بكل خطوة خطاها، او بكل كلمة تكلم بها فى ذلك، عمل سنة قيام ليلها و صيام نهارها) ^2

Since a nearly universal excuse and a common pretext for fleeing the responsibility of marriage and forming a family is the issue of poverty and lack of financial means, the Quran addresses this by saying: Do not worry about their poverty and hardship, and strive for their marriage, because "if they are poor, Allah will enrich them from His bounty" (إِنْ يَكُونُوا فُقَراءَ يُغْنِهِمُ اللَّهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ‌).

And Allah is capable of such a thing, for "Allah is All-Encompassing, All-Knowing" (وَ اللَّهُ واسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ‌). His power is so vast that it encompasses the entire realm of existence, and His knowledge is so expansive that He is aware of everyone's intentions, especially those who undertake marriage with the intention of preserving chastity and purity, and He will include them all in His grace and generosity.

In this regard, we have a clear analysis, as well as numerous narrations that will be mentioned at the end of this discussion.

However, since sometimes, despite all the efforts and struggles made by the individual and others, the means for marriage are not provided, and inevitably a person is forced to spend some time in deprivation, lest those in this stage imagine that sexual impurity is permissible for them or that necessity dictates it, the next verse immediately gives the command for piety, however difficult it may be: "And let those who find not the means for marriage keep themselves chaste, until Allah enriches them from His bounty" (وَ لْيَسْتَعْفِفِ الَّذِينَ لا يَجِدُونَ نِكاحاً حَتَّى يُغْنِيَهُمُ اللَّهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ‌).

They must not surrender to impurity during this critical stage and this period of divine trial, nor consider themselves excused, for no excuse is accepted; rather, the power of faith, character, and Taqwa (piety) must be tested in such a stage.

Then, since Islam shows special care and attention to the freedom of slaves whenever they are mentioned, it moves from the discussion of marriage to the discussion of freeing slaves through "Mukatabah" (contracting for slaves to work and pay an amount in installments to their owner to become free), saying: "And those who seek a contract [for emancipation] from among those whom your right hands possess, then make a contract with them if you know there is good in them" (وَ الَّذِينَ يَبْتَغُونَ الْكِتابَ مِمَّا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ فَكاتِبُوهُمْ إِنْ عَلِمْتُمْ فِيهِمْ خَيْراً).

The meaning of the phrase "if you know there is good in them" (عَلِمْتُمْ فِيهِمْ خَيْراً) is that they possess sufficient maturity and competence to enter this contract and subsequently the ability to fulfill it, being able to start an independent life after paying the "Mal al-Kitabah" (the contracted amount). However, if they lack the ability for these matters and this action overall results in their harm, making them a burden on society, it should be postponed to another time when this competence and ability are attained.

Then, so that the slaves do not face hardship when paying these installments, He commands: "And give them from the wealth of Allah which He has given you" (وَ آتُوهُمْ مِنْ مالِ اللَّهِ الَّذِي آتاكُمْ‌).

Regarding what wealth is meant here that should be given to these slaves, there is discussion among the commentators: A large group has said: It means that a portion of Zakat should be paid to them, as mentioned in verse 60 of Surah At-Tawbah, so they can pay their debt and become free. Others have said it means the owner of the slave should forgive a portion of the installments or return what he has received to him, so he may have more strength to save himself from captivity and slavery. There is also the possibility that at the beginning, when slaves lack the ability to provide wealth, they should be given something as an allowance or a small capital so they can engage in business, both to support themselves and to pay their debt installments.

Of course, these three interpretations are not contradictory and may all be included in the concept of the verse; the real goal is for Muslims to bring this oppressed group under the cover of their assistance so they may find release as soon as possible.

In a Hadith from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that in interpreting this verse he said: "You should reduce for him from his installments that which you did not intend to decrease, and do not increase beyond what was in your mind." [^1] This refers to the fact that some, in order to create a legal loophole and claim they helped and gave a discount to their slaves according to the above verse, would write the amount of the contract higher than what they intended, so that when giving the discount, they would receive exactly the amount they wanted without any loss! Imam Sadiq عليه السلام forbade this and said: "The discount must be from what he truly intended to take from him!"

Following the verse, it points to one of the very ugly acts of some worldly people regarding slaves, saying: "And do not compel your slave girls to prostitution, seeking the temporary goods of worldly life, if they desire to remain chaste" (وَ لا تُكْرِهُوا فَتَياتِكُمْ عَلَى الْبِغاءِ إِنْ أَرَدْنَ تَحَصُّناً لِتَبْتَغُوا عَرَضَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا).

Some commentators have said regarding the occasion of revelation of this sentence: "Abdullah ibn Ubayy" had six slave girls whom he forced to earn income for him through prostitution! When the Islamic ruling regarding the struggle against acts contrary to chastity was issued (in this Surah), they came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and complained about this matter; the above verse was revealed and forbade this act. [^1]

This verse shows to what extent people in the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) were afflicted with moral decline and fall, such that even after the emergence of Islam, some continued their actions until the above verse was revealed and ended this disgraceful situation. Unfortunately, in our era, which some have named the "Jahiliyyah of the 20th Century," in some countries that claim civilization and human rights, this act continues intensely. Even in our country, during the era of the Taghut (tyranny), it existed in a horrific form where innocent girls and unaware women were deceived and lured to centers of corruption, and through specific satanic plans, they were forced into prostitution, with all paths of escape closed to them, in order to generate massive incomes. The description of this story is very painful and beyond the scope of this discussion.

Although slavery in its previous form apparently no longer exists, in the so-called civilized world, crimes are committed that are far more horrific than the era of slavery. May Allah protect the people of the world from the evil of these "civilized-looking" humans, and thank God that in our environment after the Islamic Revolution, these disgraceful acts were ended.

It is also necessary to mention that the phrase "if they desire to remain chaste" (إِنْ أَرَدْنَ تَحَصُّناً) does not mean that if the women themselves are willing, then compelling them is permissible. Rather, this expression is of the type where the "ruling is negated by the negation of the subject," because the term "compulsion" (ikrah) is only applicable in the case of lack of desire; otherwise, prostitution and encouraging it is a great sin in any case. This expression is used so that if the owners of these slave girls have a shred of honor, it would strike at their pride. The meaning of the verse is: these slave girls, who are apparently at a lower level, do not desire this impurity; why do you, who claim so much, surrender to such baseness?!

At the end of the verse—as is the method of the Quran—so as not to close the door of return to sinners but rather to encourage them toward repentance and reform, it says: "And whoever compels them, then indeed, Allah, after their compulsion, is Forgiving and Merciful" (وَ مَنْ يُكْرِهْهُنَّ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِكْراهِهِنَّ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

This sentence, as we said, may refer to the state of the owners of those slave girls who regret their dark and disgraceful past and are ready for repentance and self-reform, or it refers to those women who were forced and compelled into this act.

In the final verses under discussion—as is the method of the Quran—it refers to the previous discussions as a summary, saying: "And We have certainly sent down to you verses clarifying many truths" (وَ لَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ آياتٍ مُبَيِّناتٍ‌). And also "examples and reports from those who passed away before you" (whose fate is a lesson for you today) (وَ مَثَلًا مِنَ الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ‌). And also "an admonition and advice for the righteous" (وَ مَوْعِظَةً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ‌).

Key Points

1- Marriage is a Divine Sunnah

Although today the issue of marriage has become so entangled in wrong customs and even superstitions that it has become a difficult or impassable road for young people, regardless of these embellishments, marriage is an innate (fitri) decree harmonized with the law of creation. Humans need healthy marriage for the survival of the generation, peace of body and soul, and solving life's problems.

Islam, which moves in harmony with creation, also has interesting and effective expressions in this regard, including the famous Hadith of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Marry and procreate to increase in number, for I will boast of you over other nations on the Day of Resurrection, even with the miscarried child." [^1]

In another Hadith from him, we read: "Whoever marries has secured half of his religion, so let him fear Allah regarding the remaining half." ^2 This is because the sexual instinct is the most powerful and rebellious of human instincts, equaling other instincts on its own, and its deviation will endanger half of a person's religion and faith.

Again, in another Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "The worst of you are your bachelors." [^1] For this reason, in the verses under discussion and also in numerous narrations, Muslims are encouraged to cooperate in the marriage of singles and provide every possible help. Specifically, Islam has placed a heavy responsibility on the shoulders of fathers regarding their children, and has considered fathers who are indifferent to this vital issue as accomplices in the deviation of their children. As we read in a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Whoever has a child reach maturity and has the means to marry them off but does not, and consequently the child commits a sin, the sin is shared between them!" ^2

And for this same reason, emphatic instructions have been given to keep the costs of marriage light and easy, including the dowry (Mahr) and other parts, so that no obstacle arises in the path of the marriage of singles—especially regarding heavy dowries which are often a stumbling block for low-income individuals. In a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "The ill-omen of a woman is the highness of her dowry." [^3] And in another Hadith following the one above, we read: "One of the signs of her ill-omen is the heaviness of her maintenance (or the cost of her marriage)." ^4

Since many men and women, to escape this divine and human responsibility, resort to excuses such as lack of financial means, it is explicitly stated in the above verses that "poverty" cannot be an obstacle to marriage; rather, marriage often becomes the cause of wealth and self-sufficiency. The reason for this becomes clear upon reflection, because as long as a person is single, they do not feel responsibility; they neither mobilize their initiative, power, and talent sufficiently to earn a legitimate income, nor do they strive to preserve and grow it when they do find an income. For this reason, singles are often homeless and empty-handed! But after marriage, a person's character transforms into a social character, and they feel intensely responsible for protecting their spouse, the family's honor, and providing for the needs of future children. Therefore, they employ all their intelligence, initiative, and talent, and strive to preserve their income and practice economy, and in a short time, they can overcome poverty.

It is not without reason that we read in a Hadith from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام: "Provision (Rizq) is with wives and family." [^1] And in another Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "A man came to him and complained of poverty and need. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'Marry.' So he married, and expansion was found in his affairs!" ^2

Undoubtedly, divine assistance and mysterious spiritual forces also come to the aid of such individuals who undertake marriage to fulfill a human duty and preserve their purity. Every person of faith can be heartened and believe in this divine promise. In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated: "Whoever leaves marriage out of fear of poverty has thought ill of Allah, for Allah the Almighty says: 'If they are poor, Allah will enrich them from His bounty.'" [^1] Of course, narrations in Islamic sources in this field are numerous; if we were to quote them all, we would deviate from the exegetical discussion.

2- What is meant by the phrase "and the righteous among your male slaves and female slaves"?

It is noteworthy that in the verses under discussion, when speaking of the marriage of single men and women, it gives a general command to take action for their marriage. However, when it comes to slaves, it qualifies it with "being righteous" (Salihin).

A group of commentators (such as the esteemed author of Tafsir al-Mizan and also Tafsir al-Safi) have interpreted it to mean competence for marriage, whereas if this were the case, this condition would also be necessary for free men and women. Others have said it means being righteous in terms of ethics and belief, as the righteous hold special importance in this matter. But the question remains: why was this condition not mentioned for non-slaves?

We suspect it means something else: in the living conditions of that time, many slaves were at a low level of culture and ethics, such that they felt no responsibility in shared life. If they were married off in that state, they would easily abandon their spouse and make them miserable. Therefore, it was commanded to take action for the marriage of those who have moral competence, and the implication is that for the rest, efforts should first be made for their moral competence so they become ready for married life, and then their marriage should be undertaken.

3- The Contract of Mukatabah?

We said that Islam designed a program for the "gradual freedom of slaves," and for this reason, it used every opportunity to free them. One of the items of this program is the issue of "Mukatabah," which is mentioned as a command in the verses under discussion.

"Mukatabah" is from the root "Kitabat," and "Kitabat" is originally from "Katb" (on the weight of Kasb) meaning "collection." Writing is called Kitabat because it collects letters and words in a phrase. Since in Mukatabah a contract is written between the "Master" (Mawla) and the "Slave" (Abd), it is called Mukatabah.

The contract of Mukatabah is a type of agreement made between these two people, where the slave becomes obligated to provide wealth through free earning and pay it in installments that are bearable for him to the Master to regain his freedom. It is commanded that the total of these installments should not exceed the price of the slave.

Also, if for some reason the slave is unable to pay the installments, they must be paid from the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) and the portion of Zakat, and he should be freed. Some jurists have even specified that if Zakat is due from the Master himself, he should count the installments of the slave's debt as Zakat.

This contract is a binding contract (Lazim), and neither party has the right to rescind it. It is clear that with this plan, many slaves regain their freedom and also gain the ability for independent life during this period when they are required to work and pay installments, and their owners do not suffer loss and will not show a negative reaction against the slaves. "Mukatabah" has many rulings and branches which are mentioned in the books of jurisprudence under "Kitab al-Mukatabah."

Ayahs

۞ ٱللَّهُ نُورُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ مَثَلُ نُورِهِۦ كَمِشْكَوٰةٍ فِيهَا مِصْبَاحٌ ۖ ٱلْمِصْبَاحُ فِى زُجَاجَةٍ ۖ ٱلزُّجَاجَةُ كَأَنَّهَا كَوْكَبٌ دُرِّىٌّ يُوقَدُ مِن شَجَرَةٍ مُّبَـٰرَكَةٍ زَيْتُونَةٍ لَّا شَرْقِيَّةٍ وَلَا غَرْبِيَّةٍ يَكَادُ زَيْتُهَا يُضِىٓءُ وَلَوْ لَمْ تَمْسَسْهُ نَارٌ ۚ نُّورٌ عَلَىٰ نُورٍ ۗ يَهْدِى ٱللَّهُ لِنُورِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَيَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلَ لِلنَّاسِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٣٥)
Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth. The parable of His Light is a niche wherein is a lamp—the lamp is in a glass, the glass as it were a glittering star—lit from a blessed olive tree, neither eastern nor western, whose oil almost lights up, though fire should not touch it. Light upon light. Allah guides to His Light whomever He wishes. Allah draws parables for mankind, and Allah has knowledge of all things. (35)
فِى بُيُوتٍ أَذِنَ ٱللَّهُ أَن تُرْفَعَ وَيُذْكَرَ فِيهَا ٱسْمُهُۥ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ فِيهَا بِٱلْغُدُوِّ وَٱلْـَٔاصَالِ (٣٦)
In houses Allah has allowed to be raised and wherein His Name is celebrated; He is glorified therein, morning and evening, (36)
رِجَالٌ لَّا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجَـٰرَةٌ وَلَا بَيْعٌ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَإِقَامِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَإِيتَآءِ ٱلزَّكَوٰةِ ۙ يَخَافُونَ يَوْمًا تَتَقَلَّبُ فِيهِ ٱلْقُلُوبُ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ (٣٧)
by men whom neither trade nor bargaining distracts from the remembrance of Allah and the maintenance of prayer and the giving of zakat. They are fearful of a day wherein the hearts and the sights will be transformed, (37)
لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَحْسَنَ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَيَزِيدَهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (٣٨)
so that Allah may reward them by the best of what they have done, and enhance them out of His grace, and Allah provides for whomever He wishes without any reckoning. (38)

Commentary

The Verse of Light!

Much has been said regarding the commentary of the above verses, and Islamic commentators, philosophers, and mystics each have extensive discussions. The connection between these verses and the preceding ones lies in the fact that the previous verses discussed the issue of chastity and the struggle against indecency through various means and methods. Since the guarantee for the implementation of all Divine commands—especially the control of rebellious instincts, particularly the sexual instinct which is the most powerful among them—is impossible without the support of "Faith," the discussion ultimately leads to Faith and its powerful effects.

First, He says: "Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth" (اللَّهُ نُورُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ).

What a beautiful, fascinating, and precious sentence! Yes, Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth; the brightness and the Giver of light to them all.

A group of commentators has interpreted the word "Light" here to mean "The Guide." Some have interpreted it as "The Illuminator." And some as "The Beautifier."

All these meanings are correct, yet the concept of the verse is even broader than this. To explain: In the Holy Quran and Islamic narrations, several things have been referred to as "Light":

  1. The Holy Quran—as we read in verse 15 of Surah Al-Ma'idah: قَدْ جاءَكُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ نُورٌ وَ كِتابٌ مُبِينٌ: "There has come to you from Allah a light and a clear Book." And in verse 157 of Surah Al-A'raf: وَ اتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ مَعَهُ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ: "And follow the light which has been sent down with him, it is they who will prosper."

  2. "Faith"—as stated in verse 257 of Al-Baqarah: اللَّهُ وَلِيُّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُخْرِجُهُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ: "Allah is the Guardian of those who believe; He brings them out of darkness (of polytheism and disbelief) into the light (of faith)."

  3. "Divine Guidance" and insight—as mentioned in verse 122 of Surah Al-An'am: أَ وَ مَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَ جَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها: "Is he who was dead and We gave him life and made for him a light by which he walks among the people like one who is in darkness, never to emerge from it?"

  4. "The Religion of Islam"—as we read in verse 32 of Surah At-Tawbah: وَ يَأْبَى اللَّهُ إِلَّا أَنْ يُتِمَّ نُورَهُ وَ لَوْ كَرِهَ الْكافِرُونَ: "And Allah refuses except to perfect His light (Islam), although the disbelievers dislike it."

  5. The person of the Prophet (ص)—in verse 46 of Surah Al-Ahzab regarding the Prophet, we read: وَ داعِياً إِلَى اللَّهِ بِإِذْنِهِ وَ سِراجاً مُنِيراً: "And one who invites to Allah by His permission, and an illuminating lamp."

  6. The Imams and Infallible Leaders—as stated in Ziyarat al-Jamiah: خلقكم اللَّه انوارا فجعلكم بعرشه محدقين: "Allah created you as lights and placed you encircling His Throne." And also in the same Ziyarat: و انتم نور الاخيار و هداة الأبرار: "And you are the light of the good and the guides of the righteous."

  7. Finally, "Knowledge" is also referred to as light, as in the famous Hadith: العلم نور يقذفه اللَّه فى قلب من يشاء: "Knowledge is a light that Allah casts into the heart of whomever He wills." ... These are all on one hand.

On the other hand, we must carefully examine the properties and characteristics of light. Through a brief study, it becomes clear that light possesses the following properties:

  1. Light is the most beautiful and subtle of existents in the material world and is the source of all beauty and subtlety!
  2. Light has the highest speed in the material world according to what is known among scientists. Light, at a speed of 300,000 kilometers per second, can circle the Earth seven times in the blink of an eye (less than a second). For this reason, the incredibly vast and staggering astronomical distances are measured only by the speed of light, and the unit of measurement for them is the "light-year"—the distance light travels in one year at its staggering speed.
  3. Light is the means for clarifying objects and observing the various existents of this world; without it, nothing can be seen. Therefore, it is both "manifest" (zahir) and "manifesting" (muzhir - making others manifest).
  4. Sunlight, which is the most important light in our world, is the nurturer of flowers and plants, and indeed the secret to the survival of all living beings. It is impossible for a creature to stay alive without using light (directly or indirectly).
  5. Today it is proven that all the colors we see are the result of the radiation of sunlight or similar lights; otherwise, objects have no color in absolute darkness!
  6. All energies present in our environment (except atomic energy) originate from sunlight. The movement of winds, the falling of rain, the flow of rivers, floods, and waterfalls, and ultimately the movement of all living beings, with a little scrutiny, lead back to sunlight. The source of warmth and heat, and that which keeps the habitat of creatures warm, is that very sunlight. Even the heat of fire obtained from wood, coal, oil, or its derivatives is from the heat of the sun, because according to scientific research, all these trace back to plants and animals that took heat from the sun and stored it within themselves. Therefore, the movement of engines is also by its blessing.
  7. Sunlight is the destroyer of various microbes and harmful organisms. If it were not for the radiation of this blessed light, the Earth would turn into a massive hospital where all its inhabitants would be struggling with death!

In short, the more we look at this wondrous phenomenon of the world of creation (light) and the more precise we become, its precious effects and great blessings become more apparent.

Now, considering these two premises, if we wish to choose a comparison or metaphor from the sensory existents of this world for the Pure Essence of God (though His exalted station is beyond any peer or likeness), can any word other than "Light" be used?! The same God who is the Originator of the entire universe, the Illuminator of the world of creation; all living beings are alive by the blessing of His command, and all creatures are at the table of His bounty. If He were to withdraw His eye of grace for a moment, all would sink into the darkness of annihilation and non-existence.

Interestingly, every being, in whatever proportion it relates to Him, acquires luminosity and brightness to that same extent: The Quran is light because it is His Word. The religion of Islam is light because it is His religion. The Prophets are lights because they are His messengers. The Infallible Imams are Divine lights because they are the protectors of His religion after the Prophets. "Faith" is light because it is the secret of connection with Him. Knowledge is light because it is the cause of knowing Him. Therefore: Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth.

In fact, if we use "light" in the broad sense of the word—meaning "anything that is manifest in its essence and makes others manifest"—then using the word "Light" for His Pure Essence would not even be a metaphor, for nothing in the world of creation is more manifest than Him, and everything other than Him is manifest by the blessing of His existence.

In the book At-Tawhid, it is narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام: He was asked for the commentary of the verse "Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth." He said: هاد لاهل السماوات و هاد لاهل الارض: "He is the Guide for the inhabitants of the heavens and the Guide for the inhabitants of the earth."

In reality, this is one of the properties of Divine Light, but it is certainly not limited to it. Thus, all the commentaries mentioned regarding this verse can be gathered into what we have stated, as each points to one of the dimensions of this unique Light and peerless brightness.

Interestingly, in the forty-seventh section of the Jawshan Kabir supplication, which is a collection of the attributes of Allah Almighty, we read: "O Light of lights, O Illuminator of light, O Creator of light, O Planner of light, O Ordainer of light, O Light of every light, Light before every light, O Light after every light, O Light above every light, O Light like unto which there is no light!" In this way, all the lights of existence draw their essence from His Light and culminate in the Light of His Pure Essence.

After stating the above reality, the Quran specifies the nature of Divine Light here by mentioning a beautiful and precise example, saying: "The parable of His Light is as a niche wherein is a lamp; the lamp is in a glass, the glass as it were a glittering star" (مَثَلُ نُورِهِ كَمِشْكاةٍ فِيها مِصْباحٌ الْمِصْباحُ فِي زُجاجَةٍ الزُّجاجَةُ كَأَنَّها كَوْكَبٌ دُرِّيٌ).

And "this lamp is lit from a blessed tree, an olive, neither of the east nor of the west" (يُوقَدُ مِنْ شَجَرَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ زَيْتُونَةٍ لا شَرْقِيَّةٍ وَ لا غَرْبِيَّةٍ).

"Its oil is so refined and pure that it would almost glow even if no fire touched it!" (يَكادُ زَيْتُها يُضِي‌ءُ وَ لَوْ لَمْ تَمْسَسْهُ نارٌ).

It is "Light upon Light" (نُورٌ عَلى‌ نُورٍ).

And "Allah guides to His Light whom He wills" (يَهْدِي اللَّهُ لِنُورِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ).

"And Allah sets forth parables for mankind" (وَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثالَ لِلنَّاسِ).

"And Allah is All-Knowing of all things" (وَ اللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَلِيمٌ).

To explain this parable, attention to several matters is necessary:

Mishkat (niche) originally refers to an opening or a small space created in a wall where traditional lamps were placed to be protected from the disturbance of wind and storms. Sometimes, a small shelf was made inside a room, and the side facing the courtyard was covered with glass, so that both the room and the courtyard would be illuminated while remaining protected from the wind. It also refers to glass enclosures made in a rectangular shape with a door and a vent at the top for air, where a lamp was placed. In short, the mishkat was a housing for the lamp against wind and storms, and since it was often built into the wall, it also concentrated and reflected the lamp's light.

Zujajah means "glass," and originally refers to transparent stones. Since glass is also made from mineral materials and is transparent, it is called zujajah. Here, it refers to the globe or chimney placed over the lamp to protect the flame and regulate the airflow from bottom to top, increasing the light and brightness of the flame.

Misbah means the "lamp" itself, which was usually lit with a wick and a flammable oily substance.

The phrase "lit from a blessed tree, an olive, neither of the east nor of the west" refers to the exceptionally predisposed energy source for this lamp. Olive oil, taken from a fruitful and blessed tree, is one of the best oils for combustion—especially a tree whose every side is equally exposed to sunlight, not situated on the east side of a garden next to a wall, nor on the west side where only one side sees the sun, resulting in fruit that is half-ripe and half-raw and oil that is impure.

With this explanation, we conclude that to utilize the perfect light of such a lamp with maximum brilliance and radiation, four factors are needed: 1. A niche (lamp-holder) that protects it from all sides without diminishing its light, but rather concentrating it. 2. A glass globe that regulates the airflow around the flame but is so transparent that it in no way obstructs the radiation of light. 3. A lamp which is the center of light production at its wick. 4. Finally, an energy source that is clear, pure, and translucent, so ready for ignition that it almost flares up without even touching a flame.

These all, on one hand, describe the physical and apparent form.

On the other hand, the great Islamic commentators have various interpretations regarding what the content of this comparison is and what the "object of comparison" (mushabbah) for this Divine Light is: Some have said it refers to the light of guidance that Allah has kindled in the hearts of the believers; in other words, it refers to Faith which has taken place in the chamber of the believers' hearts. Others have considered it to mean the Quran, which acts as a searchlight within the human heart. Others have interpreted the parable as referring to the person of the Prophet (ص). Some refer it to the proofs of the Oneness (Tawhid) and Justice of the Lord. And some have interpreted it as the spirit of obedience and piety (taqwa), which is the source of every good and happiness.

In fact, all the instances mentioned for spiritual light in the Quran and Islamic narrations have been cited here as commentary. The essence of all of them is actually one thing: the light of "Guidance" that originates from the Quran, revelation, and the existence of the Prophets, is nourished by the proofs of Tawhid, and results in submission to God's command and piety.

To explain: The light of faith in the hearts of believers possesses the same four factors present in a brilliant lamp: The misbah (lamp) is the flames of faith that appear in the believer's heart, from which the radiance of guidance spreads. The zujajah (glass) is the believer's heart which regulates faith within his being. The mishkat (niche) is the believer's chest, or in other words, the collection of his personality, awareness, knowledge, and thoughts, which protects his faith from the storms of events. The shajarah mubarakah zaytunah (blessed olive tree) is the Divine revelation, the essence of which is in the utmost clarity and purity, through which the faith of the believers is ignited and becomes fruitful.

In reality, this is the Light of Allah; the same Light that has illuminated the heavens and the earth, emerging from the center of the believers' hearts and illuminating their entire existence and being. The proofs they have grasped through intellect and reason are blended with the light of revelation and become the manifestation of "Light upon Light." It is here that ready and predisposed hearts are guided to this Divine Light, and the meaning of "Allah guides to His Light whom He wills" is realized in them.

Therefore, to preserve this Divine Light (the light of guidance and faith), a set of knowledge, awareness, self-building, and ethics is necessary to protect this lamp like a niche. It also requires a predisposed and ready heart to regulate its program like a glass globe. And it requires aid from the side of revelation to provide it with energy like the blessed olive tree. This light of revelation must be free from contamination by materialist and deviant Eastern or Western tendencies that cause its decay and gloom. It must be so clear, pure, and free from any syncretism or deviation that, without needing anything else, it mobilizes all the powers of human existence and becomes the manifestation of "its oil would almost glow even if no fire touched it."

Any interpretation based on personal opinion (tafsir bi-ra'y), incorrect preconceptions, personal tastes, imposed beliefs, leaning to the left or right, and any superstitions that contaminate the product of this blessed tree, diminishes the brilliance of this lamp and sometimes extinguishes it. This is the parable Allah has set forth for His Light in this verse, and He is All-Knowing of all things.

From what we have said above, this point becomes clear: if in the narrations of the Infallible Imams عليه السلام regarding the commentary of this verse, the mishkat is sometimes interpreted as the heart of the Prophet of Islam (ص), the misbah as the light of knowledge, the zujajah as his successor Ali عليه السلام, the shajarah mubarakah as Abraham the Friend (Khalil) from whom the root of this family originates, and the phrase "neither of the east nor of the west" as the negation of Jewish and Christian tendencies—this is, in fact, another face of the same light of guidance and faith, and the expression of a clear instance of it, not that it is limited to this instance alone. Similarly, if some commentators have interpreted this Divine Light as the Quran, or intellectual proofs, or the person of the Prophet of Islam (ص), that too shares a common root with the above commentary.

Up to this point, we have observed the characteristics and specifications of this Divine Light—the light of guidance and faith—within the metaphor of a brilliant lamp. Now we must see where this luminous lamp is located. What is its location like? So that everything necessary in this regard is clarified by mentioning this location. Therefore, in the next verse, He says: "This niche is in houses which Allah has permitted to be raised and exalted" (so that they may be safe from the encroachment of enemies, devils, and the lustful) (فِي بُيُوتٍ أَذِنَ اللَّهُ أَنْ تُرْفَعَ).

"Houses in which His Name is mentioned" (and the verses of the Quran and the realities of revelation are recited therein) (وَ يُذْكَرَ فِيهَا اسْمُهُ).

Many commentators have considered the above verse, as we have interpreted it, to be related to the previous verse [^1], though some link it to the subsequent sentence, which does not seem very correct.

As for what some have said regarding the effect of the presence of this brilliant lamp in houses whose characteristics are described in this verse, the answer is clear: for a house with these specifications—whose walls are raised and where determined, awake, and vigilant men are engaged in guarding—guarantees the protection of this brilliant lamp. Furthermore, those who are in search of such a source of light and brightness will become aware of its location and hasten toward it.

But what is meant by these "houses" (buyut)? The answer becomes clear from the characteristics mentioned for them at the end of the verse, where it says: "In them He is glorified morning and evening" (يُسَبِّحُ لَهُ فِيها بِالْغُدُوِّ وَ الْآصالِ) [^2].

[^1]: The grammatical estimation of the verse was actually: "This niche is in houses..." or "This lamp is in houses..." "This blessed tree is in houses..." "The Light of Allah is in houses," whereas in the second interpretation, they consider "in houses" to be related to "glorifies," making the meaning: "In houses which Allah has permitted to be raised, men glorify God morning and evening." But this interpretation is not consistent with the mention of the word "fiha" (in them), as it would be considered a repetition; furthermore, it does not harmonize with the narrations regarding the verse's commentary (take note).

[^2]: Ghuduw (on the pattern of 'uluw) means mornings. Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Ghuduw is said for the beginning of the day and in the Quran it is placed opposite asal, while ghadat comes opposite 'ashi." Asal is the plural of asl (on the pattern of rusul), which in turn is the plural of asil meaning "afternoon." As for why the word ghuduw appears in the singular and asal in the plural? Fakhr al-Razi says it is because ghuduw has an infinitive (masdar) aspect, and an infinitive is never pluralized.

"Men whom neither trade nor sale distracts from the remembrance of Allah and the establishing of prayer and the giving of zakat" (رِجالٌ لا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجارَةٌ وَ لا بَيْعٌ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ وَ إِقامِ الصَّلاةِ وَ إِيتاءِ الزَّكاةِ).

"They fear a Day in which hearts and eyes will be transformed and overturned" (يَخافُونَ يَوْماً تَتَقَلَّبُ فِيهِ الْقُلُوبُ وَ الْأَبْصارُ).

These characteristics show that these "houses" are those centers which: have been strengthened by the command of the Lord, are centers for the remembrance of God, and from which the realities of Islam and the laws of God are disseminated. In this broad and expansive meaning, mosques, the houses of the Prophets and the Awliya (Saints), especially the house of the Prophet (ص) and the house of Ali عليه السلام, are included.

The fact that some commentators have interpreted it exclusively as mosques or the houses of the Prophets and the like has no evidence for such exclusivity. If we observe in some narrations, such as the one narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام where he said: "هى بيوت الانبياء و بيت على منها" "These are the houses of the Prophets, and the house of Ali is among them" [^1], or in another Hadith from the Prophet (ص) where he was asked while reciting this verse which houses are meant? He said: "The houses of the Prophets." Abu Bakr asked: "Is this house (pointing to the house of Fatimah and Ali) also among them?" The Prophet (ص) said: "Yes, it is among the most superior of them" [^2]. All of these point to the most prominent instances, for we know it is common for narrations to state the clearest examples during commentary.

Yes, every center established by the command of God, where the name of God is mentioned, and where every morning and evening men of faith—whose material lives do not preoccupy them or make them heedless of God's remembrance—are engaged in glorification and sanctification; such houses are the centers for the niche of Divine lights, faith, and guidance.

In reality, these houses have several characteristics: First, they are founded by the command of God. Second, their foundations and walls are so firm and high that they protect them from the influence of devils. Third, they are centers for the remembrance of God. And finally, men guard them who are engaged in glorifying God morning and evening, and the attractions of the deceptive world do not make them heedless of the Truth. These houses, with these characteristics, are the source of guidance and faith.

It is also necessary to mention that in this verse, both "trade" (tijarah) and "sale" (bay') are mentioned, even though they might seem to have the same meaning. However, the difference between the two might be that "trade" refers to a continuous and ongoing business, while "sale" refers to a single stage or a transient transaction.

It is also essential to note that it does not say they are men who do not go toward trade and sale, but rather it says that their trade and sale do not make them heedless of the remembrance of God, the establishing of prayer, and the giving of zakat. They are constantly fearful of the Day of Resurrection and the court of the Lord's justice, where, due to the intensity of its terror, hearts and eyes are transformed (note that the phrase "yakhafun" [they fear], being a present/future tense verb, indicates the continuity of their fear of the Resurrection—a fear that compels them to fulfill their responsibilities and missions).

In the last verse under discussion, He refers to the great reward of these guardians of the light of guidance and lovers of truth and reality, saying: "That Allah may reward them for the best of what they did and increase for them out of His grace" (لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنَ ما عَمِلُوا وَ يَزِيدَهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ).

And this is not surprising, for the bounty of God for those worthy of His bounty is not limited: "And Allah provides for whom He wills without account" and grants them His infinite blessings (وَ اللَّهُ يَرْزُقُ مَنْ يَشاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ).

Regarding what is meant by "the best of what they did" (أَحْسَنَ ما عَمِلُوا) in this verse, some have said it refers to all good deeds, whether obligatory or recommended, small or large. Others believe it indicates that God rewards a good deed tenfold, and sometimes seven hundredfold or more, as we read in verse 160 of Al-An'am: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثالِها: "Whoever brings a good deed shall have ten times the like thereof," and in verse 261 of Surah Al-Baqarah, a reward equivalent to seven hundredfold or double that is mentioned for those who spend in charity.

There is also the possibility in the commentary of the above phrase that it means God rewards all their deeds based on the standard and scale of their best deeds; even their less significant and average deeds will be ranked with their best deeds in reward! This is not far from the grace of God, for in the station of justice and punishment, equality is necessary, but when it reaches the station of grace and generosity, the bounties and gifts are without account, for His Pure Essence is unlimited, and "His blessing is infinite, His generosity endless."

Key Points

The points of these verses were explained within the commentary as they were intertwined with it, but several sections of narrations remain, the mention of which is necessary to complete this exegetical discussion:

  1. In the book Rawdat al-Kafi, we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that in the commentary of the Verse of Light, he said: ان المشكاة قلب محمد (ص)، و المصباح النور الذى فيه العلم، و الزجاجة قلب على او نفسه: "The mishkat is the heart of Muhammad (ص), the misbah is the light of knowledge within it, and the zujajah refers to Ali عليه السلام or his heart, in which this lamp was placed after the passing of the Prophet." [^1]

  2. In another Hadith found in Tawhid al-Saduq, we read: Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام said: ان المشكاة نور العلم فى صدر النبى (ص) و الزجاجة صدر على ... ونُورٌ عَلى‌ نُورٍ امام مؤيد بنور العلم و الحكمة فى اثر الامام من آل محمد، و ذلك من لدن آدم الى ان تقوم الساعة، فهؤلاء الاوصياء الذين جعلهم اللَّه عز و جل خلفاء فى ارضه و حججه على خلقه، لا تخلو الارض فى كل عصر من واحد منهم: "The mishkat is the light of knowledge in the chest of the Prophet (ص), and the zujajah is the chest of Ali عليه السلام... and 'Light upon Light' are the Imams from the Family of Muhammad (ص) who come one after another and are aided by the light of knowledge and wisdom. This chain has continued and will continue from the beginning of Adam's creation until the end of the world. These are the successors whom Allah, Mighty and Majestic, has appointed as vicegerents on His earth and His proofs over His creation; the face of the earth is never devoid of one of them in any age or time." [^2]

  3. In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, the mishkat is interpreted as Fatimah عليها السلام, the misbah as Hasan عليه السلام, and the zujajah as Husayn عليه السلام. [^3] Of course, as we previously mentioned, the verses have a broad concept, and each of the above narrations expresses a clear instance of it, without disregarding the generality of the verse; thus, there is no contradiction in the narrations.

  1. In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read that he had a conversation with Qatadah, the famous jurist of Basra. During it, Qatadah expressed amazement at his presence in the Imam's assembly and the special awe of the Imam that had filled his entire heart. The Imam said to him: "Do you know where you are sitting? Before those about whom God said: 'In houses which Allah has permitted to be raised and His Name mentioned therein; in them He is glorified morning and evening by men whom neither trade nor sale distracts from the remembrance of Allah and the establishing of prayer and the giving of zakat.'" Then he said: "So you are there, and we are those!" Qatadah replied: "You have spoken the truth, by Allah, may Allah make me your ransom; by Allah, they are not houses of stone and clay" (meaning they are houses of revelation, faith, and guidance). [^1]
  1. In another Hadith, it is narrated regarding this group of Divine men who are the guardians of revelation and guidance: هم التجار الذين‌لا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجارَةٌ وَ لا بَيْعٌ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ‌، اذا دخل مواقيت الصلاة ادوا الى اللَّه حقه فيها: "They are the traders whom neither trade nor sale makes heedless of the remembrance of God; when the times for prayer arrive, they fulfill God's right therein." [^2] This indicates that while they are engaged in constructive and positive economic activities, all their activities are overshadowed by the name of God, and they prioritize nothing over it.

[^1]: and [^2]: and [^3]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, under the verses discussed (with some abridgment), Vol. 3, pp. 602-603. [^1]: and [^2]: Same source, p. 609.

  1. The olive tree, as we read in the above verses, is described as a "blessed tree" (shajarah mubarakah). If the importance of this expression was not clear to everyone on the day the Quran was revealed, it is clear and manifest to us today. Great scientists who have spent many years of their lives studying the various properties of plants tell us that from this blessed tree, a product is obtained which is among the most useful and valuable oils and plays an effective role in the health of the body. Ibn Abbas says every part of this tree is useful and beneficial, even in its ash there is benefit and profit. It was the first tree to grow after the flood of Noah عليه السلام, and the Prophets have prayed for it to be a blessed tree.
  1. Great commentators have various expressions in interpreting the phrase "Light upon Light" (نُورٌ عَلى‌ نُورٍ). The late Tabarsi says in Majma' al-Bayan: "It refers to the Prophets who come one after another from one generation and one root and continue the path of guidance." Fakhr al-Razi says in his commentary: "It refers to the gathering of light rays and their accumulation, just as it has been narrated regarding the believer: 'The believer exists within four states: if a blessing reaches him, he thanks God; if a calamity strikes, he is patient and steadfast; if he speaks, he speaks the truth; and if he judges, he seeks justice. Among the masses of ignorant people, he is like a living person among the dead. He moves among five lights: his speech is light, his action is light, his place of entry is light, his place of exit is light, and his destination is the Light of God on the Day of Resurrection.'"

There is also the possibility that the first light mentioned in the verse refers to the light of Divine guidance through revelation, and the second light is His guidance through intellect; or the first light is the light of legislative guidance (tashri') and the second light is the light of existential guidance (takwini). Thus, it is Light upon Light. In this way, this phrase is sometimes interpreted as different sources of light (Prophets), sometimes as different types of light, and sometimes as its various stages. At the same time, all of them may be gathered within the concept of the verse, which is expansive (take note).

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ كَسَرَابٍۭ بِقِيعَةٍ يَحْسَبُهُ ٱلظَّمْـَٔانُ مَآءً حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَهُۥ لَمْ يَجِدْهُ شَيْـًٔا وَوَجَدَ ٱللَّهَ عِندَهُۥ فَوَفَّىٰهُ حِسَابَهُۥ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٣٩)
As for the faithless, their works are like a mirage in a plain, which the thirsty man supposes to be water. When he comes to it, he finds it to be nothing; but there he finds Allah, who will pay him his full account, and Allah is swift at reckoning. (39)
أَوْ كَظُلُمَـٰتٍ فِى بَحْرٍ لُّجِّىٍّ يَغْشَىٰهُ مَوْجٌ مِّن فَوْقِهِۦ مَوْجٌ مِّن فَوْقِهِۦ سَحَابٌ ۚ ظُلُمَـٰتٌۢ بَعْضُهَا فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ إِذَآ أَخْرَجَ يَدَهُۥ لَمْ يَكَدْ يَرَىٰهَا ۗ وَمَن لَّمْ يَجْعَلِ ٱللَّهُ لَهُۥ نُورًا فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن نُّورٍ (٤٠)
Or like the manifold darkness in a deep sea, covered by billow upon billow, overcast by clouds; manifold [layers of] darkness, one on top of another: when he brings out his hand, he can hardly see it. One whom Allah has not granted any light has no light. (40)

Commentary

Deeds Like a Mirage!

Since the preceding verses spoke of the Light of Allah, the light of faith, and guidance, to complete this discussion and clarify the state of the faithful in comparison to others, the verses under discussion speak of the darkness of disbelief, ignorance, and faithlessness. They describe the dark-hearted disbelievers and the misguided hypocrites. They speak of those whose existence is "darkness, some of it over others" (ظُلُماتٌ بَعْضُها فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ), in contrast to the believers whose lives and thoughts were "Light upon Light" (نُورٌ عَلى‌ نُورٍ). It speaks of those who, in the dry and scorching desert of life, pursue a mirage instead of water and perish from thirst, while the believers, in the radiance of faith, have found the crystal spring of guidance and rested beside it.

First, It says: "As for those who disbelieve, their deeds are like a mirage in a desert, which a thirsty man deems to be water" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَعْمالُهُمْ كَسَرابٍ بِقِيعَةٍ يَحْسَبُهُ الظَّمْآنُ ماءً).

"But when he comes to it, he finds it to be nothing" (حتى اذا جاءه لم يجده شيئا).

"But he finds Allah with his deeds, and He settles his account"! (وَ وَجَدَ اللَّهَ عِنْدَهُ فَوَفَّاهُ حِسابَهُ‌).

"And Allah is swift in reckoning"! (وَ اللَّهُ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ‌).

"Sarāb" (mirage) is originally derived from the root "sarab" (rhyming with sharaf), meaning to walk on a downward slope, and "sarab" (rhyming with harb) means a downward path. For this reason, "sarāb" refers to the shimmer that appears from afar in deserts and slopes, making it seem as though water exists there, while it is nothing but the reflection of sunlight. [^1] "Qī'ah," according to some, is the plural of "qā'ah," meaning a vast, level expanse of land that has neither water nor vegetation; in other words, it refers to desert-like lands where a mirage is most frequently seen.

However, a group of commentators and philologists consider this word to be singular, with its plural being "qī'ān" or "qī'āt." [^2] Although there is not much difference in meaning here, the context of the verse suggests it is singular, because "sarāb" (mirage) is mentioned in the singular form, and naturally, such a mirage would be in one desert, not in multiple deserts (take note).

Then He proceeds to the second parable and says: "Or [their deeds are] like darkness in a vast, deep ocean, covered by waves, upon which are waves, over which are dark clouds" (أَوْ كَظُلُماتٍ فِي بَحْرٍ لُجِّيٍّ يَغْشاهُ مَوْجٌ مِنْ فَوْقِهِ مَوْجٌ مِنْ فَوْقِهِ سَحابٌ‌). And thus, they are "darknesses, some of them over others"! (ظُلُماتٌ بَعْضُها فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ‌).

"Such that if one were to stretch out his hand, he could hardly see it"! (إِذا أَخْرَجَ يَدَهُ لَمْ يَكَدْ يَراها).

[^1]: Modern physicists say that when the air becomes hot, the layer of air adjacent to the ground expands more due to the intensity of the heat and differs from the adjacent mass; light waves refract within it, and the mirage is due to this refraction of light.

[^2]: Refer to the commentaries of Majma' al-Bayan, Ruh al-Ma'ani, Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, and Raghib's Mufradat.

Indeed, the true light in the lives of human beings is only the light of faith, and without it, the atmosphere of life will be dim, murky, and dark. However, this light of faith comes only from Allah: "And he for whom Allah has not appointed any light, for him there is no light" (وَ مَنْ لَمْ يَجْعَلِ اللَّهُ لَهُ نُوراً فَما لَهُ مِنْ نُورٍ).

To understand the depth of this parable, it is first necessary to pay attention to the meaning of the word "lujjī."

"Lujjī" (rhyming with gurjī) means a deep and vast sea. It is originally derived from the root "lujāj," meaning to persist in an action (usually said regarding incorrect actions); subsequently, it was applied to the persistence of sea waves and their following one after another. Since the deeper and more expansive a sea is, the more waves it has, this term is used for deep and vast seas.

Now, consider a roaring and wavy sea that is very deep and profound. We know that sunlight, which is the strongest of lights, penetrates the sea only to a certain extent, and its last rays vanish and are destroyed at a depth of approximately seven hundred meters, such that in greater depths, permanent darkness and eternal night prevail, because absolutely no light penetrates there.

We also know that if water is clear and without turbulence, it reflects light better, but turbulent waves break the rays of light, and a smaller amount of it is transmitted to the depths of the water.

If we add to these roaring waves the fact that a dark and murky cloud has cast its shadow over them, the resulting darkness is an exceptionally dense darkness. [^1]

[^1]: It should be noted that "sahāb," as stated in Lisan al-Arab, means a rain-bearing cloud; given that rain-bearing clouds are often dense, they are usually darker and murkier.

The darkness of the water's depths on one hand, the darkness of the roaring waves on another, and the darkness of the murky cloud on a third—these are darknesses layered upon one another. It is obvious that in such darkness, even the closest objects would not be visible; even if a person were to place his hand near his eyes, he would not see it.

Disbelievers who are deprived of the light of faith are like someone caught in such multiplied darkness, unlike the enlightened believers who are the manifestation of "Light upon Light."

Some commentators have said that these three darknesses in which faithless individuals are submerged consist of the darkness of false belief, the darkness of incorrect speech, and the darkness of evil deeds. In other words, the deeds of faithless individuals are dark in terms of their foundation, dark in terms of the reflection they have in their speech, and dark from every aspect due to their harmony with their other ugly deeds.

Others have said these three darknesses represent the stages of their ignorance: First, they do not know; second, they do not know that they do not know; third, despite this, they think they know—which is "compounded ignorance" (jahl-e murakkab).

Others have said that since the primary means of cognition, according to the explicit text of the Quran, are three things: the heart, the eyes, and the ears (the heart meaning the intellect), as stated in Surah An-Nahl, verse 78: "And Allah brought you forth from the wombs of your mothers while you knew nothing, and He gave you hearing, sight, and hearts" (وَ اللَّهُ أَخْرَجَكُمْ مِنْ بُطُونِ أُمَّهاتِكُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً وَ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَ الْأَبْصارَ وَ الْأَفْئِدَةَ). However, the disbelievers have lost the light of the heart as well as the light of hearing and sight, and are submerged in these darknesses! [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, under the verse being discussed.

However, it is clear that these three interpretations are not contradictory, and it is possible that the verse refers to all of them.

In any case, in these two verses, we reach a final summary: the deeds of faithless individuals are first likened to a false light that appears like a mirage in a dry and scorching desert—a mirage that not only fails to quench the thirst of the thirsty but increases it due to more running.

Then, from this false light—which represents the deceptive outward deeds of faithless hypocrites—it enters the internal stage of these deeds: a terrifying interior filled with dense and frightening darknesses, a horrific interior where all human senses cease to function and the closest objects surrounding him become hidden; he cannot even see himself, let alone others! It is obvious that in such terrifying darkness, man sinks into absolute loneliness and complete ignorance and unawareness. He neither finds the path, nor has he any fellow travelers, nor does he recognize his own position, nor does he have any means at his disposal, because he has not acquired illumination from the Source of Light, namely "Allah," and has sunk into the veil of selfishness, ignorance, and folly.

If you recall, we said that light is the source of all beauty, radiance, life, vitality, and movement. Conversely, darkness is the source of ugliness, death, non-existence, stillness, and silence. Darkness is the center of terror and loathing; darkness is accompanied by coldness and depression. Such is the state of those who lose the light of faith and sink into the darkness of disbelief.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلطَّيْرُ صَـٰٓفَّـٰتٍ ۖ كُلٌّ قَدْ عَلِمَ صَلَاتَهُۥ وَتَسْبِيحَهُۥ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا يَفْعَلُونَ (٤١)
Have you not regarded that Allah is glorified by everyone in the heavens and the earth, and the birds spreading their wings. Each knows his prayer and glorification, and Allah knows best what they do. (41)
وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٤٢)
To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and toward Allah is the destination. (42)

Commentary

Everyone Glorifies Him

In the preceding verses, the discussion focused on the Light of Allah, the light of guidance and faith, and the dense layers of darkness of disbelief and misguidance. In the verses under discussion, it speaks of the proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid), which are the signs of Divine lights and the means of guidance.

First, addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says: "Do you not see that all those who are in the heavens and the earth glorify Allah?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ).

"And the birds with wings outspread [in the sky] are engaged in His glorification?" (وَ الطَّيْرُ صَافَّاتٍ).

"Each one knows its own [mode of] prayer and glorification" (كُلٌّ قَدْ عَلِمَ صَلاتَهُ وَ تَسْبِيحَهُ).

"And Allah is Well-Aware of all that they do" (وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِما يَفْعَلُونَ).

And since this universal glorification by all beings is a proof of the Creator's Lordship, and His Creatorship is a proof of His ownership over the entire realm of existence, and also a proof that all beings return to Him, it adds: "And to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth, and to Allah is the final return [of all beings]" (وَ لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ إِلَى اللَّهِ الْمَصِيرُ).

There is also the possibility regarding the connection of this verse to the previous one that the final sentence of the preceding verse spoke of Allah's knowledge of the deeds of all humans and those who glorify Him, and this verse points to His court of justice in the Hereafter, and Allah's ownership of all the heavens and the earth, and His right of judgment and decree.

Key Points

1- The phrase "أَ لَمْ تَرَ" (Do you not see?)

According to many commentators, this means "Do you not know?" (أ لم تعلم), because the universal glorification of the beings of the world is not something seen with the physical eye; rather, in whatever sense it may be, it is perceived with the heart and the intellect. However, because this matter is so clear that it is as if it is seen with the eye, the expression "أَ لَمْ تَرَ" has been used.

It is also noteworthy that although the addressee in this verse is the person of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, according to a group of commentators, it refers to people in general, and there are many similar examples of this in the Quran.

However, some have said that this address, in the stage of vision and observation, is specific to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, for Allah had granted him a perception and vision through which he observed the glorification and praise of all beings in this world. Likewise, the special servants of Allah who follow his school of thought reach the station of "objective witnessing" (shuhud 'ayni), but for the general public, it takes the form of intellectual and scientific witnessing, not objective witnessing. [^1]

2- The Universal Glorification of the Beings of the World

Various verses of the Quran speak of four types of worship regarding all beings of this great universe: Glorification (Tasbih), Praise (Hamd), Prostration (Sujud), and Prayer (Salat). In the verse under discussion, "Prayer" and "Glorification" were mentioned.

In verse 15 of Surah Ar-Ra'd, it speaks of universal "Prostration": (وَ لِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ).

And in verse 44 of Surah Al-Isra, it speaks of the "Glorification" and "Praise" of all beings in the realm of existence: (وَ إِنْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ).

Regarding the reality of the universal "Praise" and "Glorification" of the world's beings, and the various interpretations offered in this regard, we have discussed it in detail under verse 44 of Surah Al-Isra, a summary of which we provide here:

There are two noteworthy interpretations here:

1- All particles of this universe—whether those we consider sentient, inanimate, or non-sentient—all possess a type of perception and consciousness, and in their own world, they utter the glorification and praise of God, even if we are unable to perceive it. Evidence from Quranic verses has also been presented for this interpretation.

2- What is meant by glorification and praise is what we call "the language of disposition" (zaban-e hal). That is, the entire system of the universe and the wondrous secrets hidden within each of the beings of this world, with a wordless tongue, explicitly and clearly speak of the power and greatness of their Creator, and His infinite knowledge and wisdom. For every innovative being and every wondrous work—even an exquisite painting or a beautiful and subtle piece of poetry—speaks the praise and glorification of its originator. That is, on one hand, it expresses his prominent attributes (Praise), and on the other hand, it negates any flaw or deficiency from him (Glorification), let alone this magnificent universe with all its endless wonders and marvels.

(For further explanation, please refer to Volume 12 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 133 onwards).

Of course, if we take the phrase "يُسَبِّحُ لَهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ" to mean the glorification of those who are in the heavens and the earth, and maintain the literal meaning of the word "man" (who) as referring to rational beings, then "Glorification" here would be in the first sense, which is a conscious and voluntary glorification. However, the implication of this statement is that we must also attribute such consciousness to birds, as birds in the above verse are placed alongside "those in the heavens." This is not strange, as in some other verses of the Quran, such perception is mentioned for certain birds (refer to the commentary provided under verse 38 of Surah Al-An'am, Volume 5, page 221).

3- The Specific Glorification of Birds

In why the above verse emphasizes the glorification of birds among all the beings of the world—and specifically in the state where they have spread their wings across the sky—there is a point: birds, in addition to their extraordinary variety, have characteristics that attract the eye and heart of every rational person. These heavy bodies, contrary to the law of gravity, move across the heavens with great and lightning speed, especially when they keep their wings flat and ride the airwaves, turning and advancing effortlessly in whichever direction they desire; it is a fascinating sight.

Their extraordinary awareness of meteorological matters and their deep knowledge of the earth's geography during travel and migration from one continent to another—even from the North Pole to the South Pole—and the mysterious and wondrous guidance system that leads them on this long journey even when the sky is covered with clouds, are among the most amazing issues and the clearest proofs of Monotheism.

The special radar located within bats, by which they see all obstacles in their path in the pitch darkness of night, and sometimes even target a fish beneath the water's waves and pull it out with a lightning movement, is among the astonishing features of this bird! In any case, wonders are hidden within the existence of birds, which is why the Quran has specifically emphasized them.

4- Commentary on the phrase "كُلٌّ قَدْ عَلِمَ صَلاتَهُ وَ تَسْبِيحَهُ"

A group of commentators have returned the pronoun in "knows" (علم) to "each" (كل), according to which the meaning of the above sentence becomes: "Each of those who are in the earth and heaven, as well as the birds, is aware of its own prayer and glorification."

However, others have returned it to Allah, meaning Allah is aware of the prayer and glorification of each of them. But the first interpretation is more consistent with the meaning of the verse. Accordingly, each of the glorifiers knows the way and method of its "glorification" and the conditions and characteristics of its "prayer." If "conscious glorification" is intended, the meaning of this statement is clear. But if it is through the "language of disposition," its meaning is that each has a specific system that in some way expresses the greatness of the Lord, and each is a manifestation of His power and wisdom.

5- What is meant by "Salat" (Prayer)?

A group of commentators, such as the late Tabarsi in "Majma' al-Bayan" and Alusi in "Ruh al-Ma'ani," have interpreted "Salat" here to mean "supplication" (du'a), which is its original linguistic meaning. Thus, the beings of the earth and heaven, through the language of disposition or the language of speech, supplicate in the presence of Allah and request grace from His presence. And He, who is the Absolute Bestower, grants to them according to their capacities and does not withhold.

However, each in its own world knows what need it has, what it should ask for, and what supplication it should make.

Furthermore, according to the verses we previously mentioned, they are humble in His magnificent presence and submissive to the laws of creation. On the other hand, with their entire being, they recount the perfect attributes of Allah and negate any deficiency from Him. In this way, their fourfold acts of worship—"Praise," "Glorification," "Supplication," and "Prostration"—are completed.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the verse being discussed.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُزْجِى سَحَابًا ثُمَّ يُؤَلِّفُ بَيْنَهُۥ ثُمَّ يَجْعَلُهُۥ رُكَامًا فَتَرَى ٱلْوَدْقَ يَخْرُجُ مِنْ خِلَـٰلِهِۦ وَيُنَزِّلُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مِن جِبَالٍ فِيهَا مِنۢ بَرَدٍ فَيُصِيبُ بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَصْرِفُهُۥ عَن مَّن يَشَآءُ ۖ يَكَادُ سَنَا بَرْقِهِۦ يَذْهَبُ بِٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ (٤٣)
Have you not regarded that Allah drives the clouds, then He composes them, then He piles them up, whereat you see the rain issuing from its midst? And He sends down hail from the sky, out of the mountains that are in it, and He strikes with it whomever He wishes, and turns it away from whomever He wishes. The brilliance of its lightening almost takes away the sight. (43)
يُقَلِّبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ (٤٤)
Allah alternates the night and the day. There is indeed a lesson in that for those who have insight. (44)
وَٱللَّهُ خَلَقَ كُلَّ دَآبَّةٍ مِّن مَّآءٍ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مَّن يَمْشِى عَلَىٰ بَطْنِهِۦ وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَمْشِى عَلَىٰ رِجْلَيْنِ وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَمْشِى عَلَىٰٓ أَرْبَعٍ ۚ يَخْلُقُ ٱللَّهُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٤٥)
Allah created every animal from water. Among them are some that creep upon their bellies, and among them are some that walk on two feet, and among them are some that walk on four. Allah creates whatever He wishes. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (45)

Commentary

Another Glimpse into the Wonders of Creation

In these verses, we once again encounter another aspect of the wonders of creation and the knowledge, wisdom, and grandeur hidden behind them, all of which are evidences of the Oneness of His Pure Essence.

Addressing the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) again, He says: "Do you not see that Allah gently drives the clouds, then joins them together, then makes them into a heap?" (أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يُزْجِي سَحَابًا ثُمَّ يُؤَلِّفُ بَيْنَهُ ثُمَّ يَجْعَلُهُ رُكَامًا).

"And then you see the rain-drops coming out from their midst" and falling upon mountains, plains, gardens, and deserts (فَتَرَى الْوَدْقَ يَخْرُجُ مِنْ خِلَالِهِ).

"Yuzji" (يُزْجِي) is derived from the root "izja’" (إزجاء), meaning to drive gently—a driving intended to align scattered entities. This expression applies precisely to clouds, as each fragment rises from different corners of the seas; then the hand of the Lord’s power drives them toward one another, joins them, and makes them dense.

"Rukam" (رُكَامًا) refers to objects that are piled or accumulated upon one another.

As for "wadq" (الْوَدْقَ), many commentators believe it means the drops of rain that emerge from within the clouds. However, according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, it has another meaning: the very fine particles of water scattered in the air like mist when rain falls. Nevertheless, the first meaning is more appropriate here, because that which more significantly signifies the grandeur of the Lord is the life-giving drops of rain, not those mist-like particles of water.

Furthermore, whenever the Quran raises the subject of clouds and the descent of blessings from the sky, it refers to the phenomenon of rain. Indeed, it is rain that revives dead lands, clothes trees and plants in the garment of life, and quenches the thirst of humans and animals.

Then, referring to another amazing phenomenon of the sky and clouds, He says: "And He sends down from the sky, from mountains within it, hail" (وَيُنَزِّلُ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مِنْ جِبَالٍ فِيهَا مِنْ بَرَدٍ).

"Hailstones by which He strikes whom He wills"—the blossoms of trees, fruits, crops, and even sometimes animals and humans are not safe from its damage (فَيُصِيبُ بِهِ مَنْ يَشَاءُ).
