"And He averts it from whom He wills" (وَيَصْرِفُهُ عَنْ مَنْ يَشَاءُ).

Indeed, it is He who sometimes sends down life-giving rain from a cloud, and at other times, with a slight change, transforms it into damaging or even lethal hail. This demonstrates the ultimate extent of His power and majesty—that He has placed human benefit and harm, life and death, side by side, or rather, one within the heart of the other!

At the end of the verse, He points to another celestial phenomenon which is among the signs of Monotheism (Tawhid), saying: "The flash of its lightning almost takes away the sight" (يَكَادُ سَنَا بَرْقِهِ يَذْهَبُ بِالْأَبْصَارِ).

Clouds, which are in reality composed of particles of "water," when they carry an electrical charge, leap forth with such "fire" from within that their flash dazzles the eyes and their thunder fills the ears with its sound, sometimes shaking everything. This immense power within this delicate vapor is truly wondrous!

Answer to a Question [Which mountain is in the sky from which hail falls?]

The only question remaining here is: what is this mountain in the sky from which hail falls? Commentators have provided various explanations:

  1. Some have said that "jibal" (mountains) here is metaphorical, just as we say "a mountain of food" or "a mountain of knowledge." Thus, the meaning of the verse is that a massive heap or volume of hail is created by the clouds in the heart of the sky, part of which falls on the city and part on the desert, even striking certain individuals.

  2. Others have said that "mountains" refers to the massive masses of clouds that resemble mountains in their grandeur and size.

  3. The author of the commentary Fi Zilal offers another explanation that seems more appropriate: that cloud masses in the middle of the sky truly resemble mountains, even though they look flat when we view them from below. However, those who have traveled by airplane above the clouds have often seen this sight with their own eyes—that from that side, clouds look like mountains, valleys, peaks, and depressions similar to those on earth.

In other words, the upper surface of clouds is never flat; like the surface of the earth, it possesses many irregularities, and in this regard, applying the name "mountains" to them is fitting [1]. To this, a subtle scientific point can be added: according to scientists, the formation of hail in the sky occurs when raindrops separate from the cloud and encounter a cold front in the upper atmosphere, freezing them. Then, the violent storms prevailing in that region sometimes hurl these grains back up, and they enter the clouds again, where another layer of water settles on them and freezes upon separating from the cloud once more. Sometimes this process repeats several times, each time adding a new layer until the hailstone becomes large enough that the storm can no longer hurl it upward; it is then that it takes the path to the earth and descends, or the storm subsides and it moves toward the earth without hindrance [2]. Considering this, the scientific point hidden in the word "mountains" (jibal) becomes clearer, because the formation of large and heavy hailstones is only possible when cloud masses become dense, so that when the storm hurls the frozen hailstone into them, it absorbs more water. This only occurs where cloud masses stand like high mountains in the upward direction, serving as a significant source for hail formation (reflect deeply) [3].

[1]: Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 6, p. 109. [2]: Encyclopedia Farhang-Nameh, entry: "Hail" (Tagarg). [3]: In the phrase "وَيُنَزِّلُ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مِنْ جِبَالٍ فِيهَا مِنْ بَرَدٍ", the word "min" (from/of) is repeated three times. From the perspective of Arabic grammar, the first is "min ibtida'iyyah" (indicating origin), and the second is also consistent with "ibtida'iyyah." However, the third "min" varies according to the interpretations mentioned above. According to the first interpretation, it would be "min bayaniyyah" (explanatory), and the meaning of the sentence becomes: "Allah sends down from the sky, from mountains that are [made] of hail, hailstones" (thus the object of "yunazzilu" is omitted, which is the word "al-barad," understood from the context). But according to the second and third interpretations which we have chosen, "min" is either extra (as quoted in Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani from Al-Akhfash) or "tab'idhiyyah" (partitive) (reflect deeply).

Here we read another analysis from some writers, summarized as follows:

"In the verses under discussion, high clouds explicitly refer to mountains of ice, or in other words, mountains in which a type of ice exists. This is very interesting because after the invention of the airplane and the possibility of high-altitude flights—which expanded the scope of human knowledge—scientists encountered clouds composed of and covered by ice needles, to which the title 'mountains of ice' truly applies. It is also remarkable that a Soviet scientist, in describing thunderclouds, referred to them several times as 'cloud mountains' or 'mountains of snow.' Thus, it becomes clear that there truly are mountains of ice in the sky" [1].

In the next verse, referring to another sign of creation and the grandeur of the Lord—the creation of night and day and their characteristics—He says: "Allah alternates the night and the day; indeed, in that is a lesson for those of vision" (يُقَلِّبُ اللَّهُ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِأُولِي الْأَبْصارِ).

Regarding what this "alternation" (taqlib) refers to, several interpretations have been mentioned:

Some have taken it to mean the coming and going of night and day, where one arrives and effaces the other.

[1]: Wind and Rain in the Quran, pp. 140-141 (refer to the mentioned book for more details).

Some have considered it to mean the shortening of one and the lengthening of the other, which occurs gradually and is related to the emergence of the seasons.

Finally, some have interpreted it as the changes such as heat, cold, and other events that take place during the night and day [1]. However, it is evident that these interpretations are not contradictory, and all of them may be included in the concept of the word "yuqallib" (alternates).

Undoubtedly—as science has proven—both the succession of night and day and their gradual changes are vital for humans and serve as a lesson for "those of vision" (uli al-absar).

The continuous shining of the sun would raise the air temperature and burn living beings, exhausting the nerves; but when curtains of nocturnal darkness are placed between these periods of sunshine, it perfectly moderates it.

The gradual changes of day and night, which are the source of the four seasons, are a very effective factor for the fertilization of plants, the life of all living creatures, the falling of rains, and the storage of water in the earth [2].

The last verse under discussion refers to one of the most important aspects of the system of creation, which is among the clearest proofs of Monotheism: the issue of life in its diverse forms. It says: "And Allah created every living creature from water" (وَاللَّهُ خَلَقَ كُلَّ دَابَّةٍ مِنْ مَاءٍ).

[1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr Razi, Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, and Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani. [2]: We have also discussed this in Volume 8 of Tafsir Nemouneh, p. 228 (under verse 6 of Surah Yunus).

And despite the fact that the origin of all of them traces back to water, they nonetheless have very different and amazing forms of creation: "Some of them crawl on their bellies" (reptiles) (فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَمْشِي عَلَى بَطْنِهِ).

"And some of them walk on two legs" (humans and birds) (وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَمْشِي عَلَى رِجْلَيْنِ).

"And some of them walk on four" (quadrupeds) (وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَمْشِي عَلَى أَرْبَعٍ).

Furthermore, it is not limited to these; life has extraordinarily diverse forms, including creatures that live in the sea or insects that have thousands of types and thousands of forms. Therefore, at the end of the verse, He says: "Allah creates what He wills" (يَخْلُقُ اللَّهُ مَا يَشَاءُ).

"For Allah is powerful over all things" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

Key Points

1- What is meant by "water" (ma') here?

There is discussion among commentators regarding what the word "ma'" (water) refers to in the verse under discussion; in fact, three interpretations have been mentioned for it:

  1. It refers to the seminal fluid (nutfa). Many commentators have chosen this interpretation, and it has been pointed out in some narrations as well. The problem with this interpretation is that not all living creatures are created from seminal fluid, as unicellular animals and some other animals that fall under the definition of "living creature" (dabbah) come into existence through cell division, not from semen—unless it is said that the above rule is general in nature rather than universal.

  2. Another is that it refers to the origin of the first living being. According to some Islamic narrations, the first thing Allah created was water, and He created humans from that water later. Also, according to modern scientific hypotheses, the first spark of life appeared in the seas, and this phenomenon dominated the depths or shores of the seas before anywhere else. (Of course, the power that brought the living being with all its complexity into existence in the first stage and guided it in subsequent stages was a supernatural power—namely, the Will of the Lord).

  3. The final interpretation is that the creation of living beings from water means that currently, water constitutes their primary substance, and a major part of their structure is water; without water, no living being can continue its life.

Of course, these interpretations are not contradictory, but at the same time, the first and second interpretations seem more accurate [1].

2- Answer to a Question

A question arises here: animals are not limited to these three groups (reptiles, bipeds, and quadrupeds); rather, there are many living creatures that have more than four legs.

The answer to this question is hidden within the verse itself, for in the following phrase He says:

[1]: Some proponents of the evolution of species have relied on this verse to prove their hypothesis, but we have explained the lack of stability of this hypothesis in Volume 11, from page 81 onwards, under verse 26 of Surah al-Hijr. It is also noteworthy that, in principle, Quranic verses should not be adapted to hypotheses, because Quranic verses have a fixed truth while scientific hypotheses are subject to change.

"Allah creates what He wills" (يَخْلُقُ اللَّهُ مَا يَشَاءُ). Furthermore, the most important animals that humans deal with are these three groups. Beyond this, some believe that even animals with more than four legs have their primary support on only four legs, and the rest are considered auxiliary limbs [1] and [2].

3- Diverse Forms of Life

Undoubtedly, the most amazing phenomenon of this world is the phenomenon of "life"—the very issue whose mystery has not yet been unlocked for scientists. Everyone says that living beings have emerged from the inanimate materials of this world, but no one can say exactly under what conditions such a leap took place, because the transformation of inanimate matter into living beings has not yet been observed in any laboratory, despite hundreds of thousands of scientists thinking and experimenting on this for many years.

Of course, a distant shadow in this regard is visible to scientists, but it is only a shadow. What is certain is that the secrets of life are so complex that human sciences, with all their vastness, are still unable to discover and understand them.

In the current conditions of the world, living beings only come from living beings, and no living being takes root from an inanimate object. However, certainly in the distant past, this was not the case; in other words, life on Earth has a history of origin, but how and under what conditions is a mystery that is clear to no one.

[1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi and Tafsir Fakhr Razi under the verse in question. [2]: It is also necessary to note from a linguistic perspective that the pronoun "minhum" (among them), although usually for a group of rational beings, refers back to non-rational beings in the above verse, as does the word "man" (who/those who); this is because these words are sometimes used for non-rational beings as well.

Even more amazing is the diversity of life in all these completely different forms—from unicellular living beings that are only found under a microscope to mountain-like sea whales whose length exceeds thirty meters and are mountains of floating flesh! From insects, of which hundreds of thousands of types have been observed, to birds that appear in hundreds of thousands of forms, each having its own world and a universe full of secrets.

Books on zoology, which today constitute a massive portion of the world's great libraries, recount only a corner of these secrets for us—especially marine animals, as the sea has always been a land of wonders, and despite all the information we have recently gained, our knowledge in this field is very meager.

Truly, how Great is the God who created all these living beings with this vast diversity and granted each what they needed? And how Grand is His power and knowledge, which provided for each what was necessary according to its conditions and needs? And the wonder is that all of them were created in the beginning from one [type of] water and a few simple materials of the earth.

Ayahs

لَّقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ ءَايَـٰتٍ مُّبَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٤٦)
Certainly We have sent down illuminating signs, and Allah guides whomever He wishes to a straight path. (46)
وَيَقُولُونَ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَبِٱلرَّسُولِ وَأَطَعْنَا ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّىٰ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ ۚ وَمَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٤٧)
They say, ‘We have faith in Allah and His Apostle, and we obey.’ Then, after that, a part of them refuse to comply, and they do not have faith. (47)
وَإِذَا دُعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٤٨)
When they are summoned to Allah and His Apostle that He may judge between them, behold, a part of them turn aside. (48)
وَإِن يَكُن لَّهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ يَأْتُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ مُذْعِنِينَ (٤٩)
But if justice be on their side, they come compliantly to him. (49)
أَفِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ أَمِ ٱرْتَابُوٓا۟ أَمْ يَخَافُونَ أَن يَحِيفَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَرَسُولُهُۥ ۚ بَلْ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٥٠)
Is there a sickness in their hearts? Do they have doubts, or fear that Allah and His Apostle will be unjust to them? Indeed, it is they who are the wrongdoers. (50)

Occasion of Revelation

Commentators have mentioned two occasions of revelation for a portion of these verses: First:

One of the hypocrites had a dispute with a Jewish man. The Jewish man invited the hypocrite—who was a Muslim in outward appearance—to the arbitration of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, but the hypocrite refused to submit and instead invited him to the arbitration of "Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf the Jew"! (According to some narrations, he even explicitly said: "It is possible that Muhammad may not observe justice regarding us!") These verses were revealed and severely rebuked and condemned such individuals.

Second: A dispute arose between the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, and Uthman (or according to one narration, between him and Mughirah ibn Wa'il) over a piece of land he had purchased from Ali عليه السلام. Stones were found in the land, and the buyer wanted to return it on the grounds of it being defective. Ali عليه السلام said: "Let the Messenger of Allah judge between me and you." However, "Hakam ibn Abi al-Aas," who was among the hypocrites, said to the buyer: "Do not do this, for if you go to his cousin—meaning the Prophet—He will certainly rule in his favor!" This verse was revealed and severely censured him. [1]

[1] Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Tafsir al-Tibyan, Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Tafsir al-Safi, and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn (under the verse in question with slight variations).

Commentary

Faith and Acceptance of Allah's Judgment

Since the previous verses spoke of faith in Allah, the proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid), and His signs in the world of creation, the verses under discussion speak of the effects of faith and the reflections of Monotheism in human life, and one's submission before truth and reality.

First, He says: "We have indeed sent down clear and clarifying verses" (لَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا آياتٍ مُبَيِّناتٍ).

Verses that illuminate hearts with the light of faith and Monotheism, grant light and purity to human thoughts, and transform the dark environment of their lives.

Of course, the existence of these "clarifying verses" prepares the ground for faith, but the primary role belongs to Divine guidance, for "Allah guides whom He wills to a straight path" (وَ اللَّهُ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ).

And we know that Allah's will and His providence are not without reason; He casts the light of guidance into hearts that are ready to receive it—those who have begun the struggle (mujahadah) and have taken steps toward Him.

Then, as a reproach to the group of hypocrites who claim faith while faith does not shine in their hearts, He says: "They say: 'We believe in Allah and in the Messenger, and we obey,' but after this claim, a party of them turns away; they are not truly believers" (وَ يَقُولُونَ آمَنَّا بِاللَّهِ وَ بِالرَّسُولِ وَ أَطَعْنا ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّى فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ وَ ما أُولئِكَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ). What kind of faith is this that does not go beyond their tongues? And whose light does not manifest in their actions?

Next, as a clear proof of their lack of faith, He says: "And when they are called to Allah and His Messenger, that He may judge between them, behold, a party of them turns away" (وَ إِذا دُعُوا إِلَى اللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ إِذا فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ مُعْرِضُونَ).

To emphasize further and clarify their polytheism and worldliness, He adds: "But if the right is on their side, they come to him in prompt obedience" (وَ إِنْ يَكُنْ لَهُمُ الْحَقُّ يَأْتُوا إِلَيْهِ مُذْعِنِينَ).

It is noteworthy that in one phrase, the call is toward Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but the subsequent phrase, "that He may judge" (li-yahkuma), is in the singular form, referring only to the judgment of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. This is because the Prophet's judgment is not separate from Allah's judgment, and both in reality return to a single matter.

Additionally, it should be noted that the pronoun "to him" (ilayhi) refers to the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or his judgment.

Another point to consider is that in the above verses, this violation and turning away from the Prophet's judgment is attributed only to a "party" of the hypocrites. This is perhaps because the other group was not as shameless and audacious, for hypocrisy, like faith, has different levels.

In the last verse under discussion, He states the root causes and motives for the lack of submission to the Prophet's judgment in three phrases, saying: "Is there a disease in their hearts?" (the disease of hypocrisy) (أَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ). This is one of the characteristics of hypocrites: they profess faith, but because of the deviation in their hearts from the principle of Monotheism, they never submit to the judgment of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Or, if the disease of hypocrisy has not overcome their hearts, "are they in doubt?" (أَمِ ارْتابُوا). Naturally, a person who is hesitant in accepting a religion will not submit to its requirements.

Or, if it is neither this nor that and they are among the believers, "do they fear that Allah and His Messenger will deal unjustly with them?" (أَمْ يَخافُونَ أَنْ يَحِيفَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ رَسُولُهُ).

While this is a blatant contradiction; one who considers the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله to be the Messenger of Allah and the conveyor of His message, and regards his ruling as Allah's ruling, cannot possibly entertain the possibility of injustice regarding him. Is it possible for Allah to wrong anyone? Is not injustice born of ignorance, need, or selfishness? Allah's holy essence is pure from all of these.

"Nay, it is they who are the wrongdoers" (بَلْ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ).

They do not want to be content with their own rights, and since they know the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله will not give them anything from the rights of others, they do not submit to his judgment.

According to the author of the commentary Fi Zilal: "These three expressions are each presented in a specific form: the first for affirmation, the second for wonder, and the third for denial."

In the first phrase, He wants to clarify the true cause, which is the disease of hypocrisy. In the second phrase, the goal is to express wonder at their doubt regarding the Prophet's justice and the correctness of his judgment despite claiming faith. The third phrase refers to the clear contradiction seen between their claim of faith and their actions. [1]

[1] Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 6, p. 115.

The only criticism directed at this commentator's statement is that he interpreted the phrase "am-irtabu" (or are they in doubt) as doubt regarding the justice and correctness of the Prophet's judgment, whereas it appears to mean doubt regarding the essence of prophethood itself, as many commentators have accepted.

Key Points

1- The Disease of Hypocrisy

This is not the first time in the Holy Quran that we encounter the term "disease" (marad) regarding hypocrisy. Previously, at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, while describing the traits of the hypocrites, it was stated: "In their hearts is a disease, so Allah has increased their disease" (فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ فَزادَهُمُ اللَّهُ مَرَضاً). As we mentioned in the first volume under the said verse, hypocrisy is in reality a disease and a deviation. A healthy person has only one face; his soul and body are in harmony. If he is a believer, his entire being cries out faith, and if he is deviated, his outward and inward both express deviation. But for his outward to speak of faith while his inward reeks of disbelief—this is a type of disease.

And since such individuals, due to their stubbornness and persistence in their ways, do not deserve Divine grace and guidance, Allah leaves them to themselves so that this disease of theirs increases.

Truly, the most dangerous individuals in a society are these hypocrites, because one's duty toward them is not clear; they are neither truly friends nor openly enemies! They utilize the resources of the believers and are outwardly safe from the punishment of the disbelievers, yet their actions are worse than those of the disbelievers.

And as we know, because this disharmony between the outward and inward cannot continue forever, eventually the veils are lifted and their polluted inner selves are revealed. As we observed in the verses under discussion and their occasion of revelation, the occurrence of a scene of judgment exposed them and revealed their inner malice. [1]

2- The Government of Justice is Only Allah's Government

Undoubtedly, no matter how much a human tries to empty himself of love, hatred, selfishness, and self-love, he may still unconsciously fall prey to these things, unless he is infallible (Ma'sum) and protected by the Lord.

For this reason, we say: the true Legislator is only Allah. For in addition to knowing all human needs through His infinite knowledge and knowing the best way to meet those needs, He never falls into deviation due to needs, or love and hatred.

In the position of judgment and arbitration as well, the most just of judgments are those of Allah, the Prophet, and the Infallible Imam, and after that, the judgments of those who follow their path and bear a resemblance to them.

But this selfish human does not submit to these just judgments and does not like these justice-spreading laws. He seeks law, government, and judgment that better satisfy his greed, avarice, and lust. What a profound expression is used regarding this group in the above verses: "It is they who are the real wrongdoers" (أُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ)! Furthermore, being placed in such situations is a touchstone for measuring the standard of faith of every human, "so that everyone who has dross within may be disgraced!" Interestingly, the Quran says elsewhere: True believers not only outwardly

[1] For more explanation regarding the characteristics of hypocrites, refer to Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, under verse 10 onwards.

submit to your judgment, but in the depths of their hearts, they feel no heaviness or discomfort from your judgments, even if they are outwardly to their disadvantage: "But no, by your Lord, they will not believe until they make you judge in what they disagree about, and then find within themselves no discomfort from what you have judged and submit in full submission" (فَلا وَ رَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيما شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَرَجاً مِمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَ يُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيماً). [1] But those who accept the ruling of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله only where it protects their interests are in reality polytheists who are slaves and servants to their own interests, even if they claim faith and stand in the ranks of the believers!

[1] Surah An-Nisa, Verse 65.

Ayahs

إِنَّمَا كَانَ قَوْلَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذَا دُعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَن يَقُولُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٥١)
All the response of the faithful, when they are summoned to Allah and His Apostle that He may judge between them, is to say, ‘We hear and obey.’ It is they who are the felicitous. (51)
وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَيَخْشَ ٱللَّهَ وَيَتَّقْهِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ (٥٢)
Whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle, and fears Allah and is wary of Him—it is they who will be the triumphant. (52)
۞ وَأَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ لَئِنْ أَمَرْتَهُمْ لَيَخْرُجُنَّ ۖ قُل لَّا تُقْسِمُوا۟ ۖ طَاعَةٌ مَّعْرُوفَةٌ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (٥٣)
They swear by Allah with solemn oaths that if you order them they will surely march out. Say, ‘Do not swear! Honourable obedience [is all that is expected of you]. Allah is indeed well aware of what you do.’ (53)
قُلْ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ ۖ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَيْهِ مَا حُمِّلَ وَعَلَيْكُم مَّا حُمِّلْتُمْ ۖ وَإِن تُطِيعُوهُ تَهْتَدُوا۟ ۚ وَمَا عَلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٥٤)
Say, ‘Obey Allah, and obey the Apostle.’ But if you turn your backs, [you should know that] he is only responsible for his burden and you are responsible for your own burden, and if you obey him, you will be guided, and the Apostle’s duty is only to communicate in clear terms. (54)

Commentary

Faith and Absolute Submission to the Truth

In the preceding verses, we witnessed the reaction of the dark-hearted hypocrites—who were enveloped in layered darkness, "one above another"—towards the judgment of Allah and the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family). We saw how they would evade the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) just arbitration, as if they feared that Allah and His Messenger (peace be upon him and his family) would infringe upon their rights!

The verses under discussion describe the polar opposite: the conduct of the believers regarding this divine judgment. It states: "The only statement of the believers when they are called to Allah and His Messenger to judge between them is that they say, 'We hear and we obey'" (إِنَّما كانَ قَوْلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذا دُعُوا إِلَى اللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَنْ يَقُولُوا سَمِعْنا وَ أَطَعْنا).

What a beautiful expression: "We hear and we obey" (سَمِعْنا وَ أَطَعْنا)—brief yet profound.

Notably, the word "Innama" (إِنَّما), which denotes restriction (hasr), indicates that they have no words other than this; their entire being is encapsulated in this single statement. Indeed, the essence of true faith is nothing but this: "We hear and we obey."

How could someone who recognizes Allah as All-Knowing, independent of all, and Merciful and Compassionate toward all His servants, prefer anyone else's judgment over His? How could they show any reaction other than listening and obeying His commands and judgments? What a magnificent means for the victory of true believers, and what a great test! Thus, at the end of the verse, He says: "And those are the successful ones" (وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ).

One who entrusts their reins to Allah and accepts Him as the Ruler and Judge will undoubtedly be victorious in everything, whether in material or spiritual life.

The second verse pursues this same truth in a more general form, stating: "And whoever obeys Allah and His Messenger and fears Allah and is conscious of Him—it is those who are the attainers" (وَ مَنْ يُطِعِ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ وَ يَخْشَ اللَّهَ وَ يَتَّقْهِ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفائِزُونَ) [1]. In this verse, the obedient and the God-fearing are described as "Fa'iz" (attainers/victors), while in the previous verse, those who submit to the judgment of Allah and the Prophet were described as "Ahl al-Falah" (the successful). As derived from linguistic sources, "Fawz" and "Falah" carry nearly the same meaning. Raghib in Al-Mufradat states: "Fawz means victory and attaining goodness accompanied by safety," and regarding Falah, he says: "Falah is triumph and reaching the goal." (Of course, it originally means "to cleave" or "to split," and since victorious individuals remove obstacles and cleave their path to reach the destination, Falah is used to mean victory). Since the latter verse speaks of obedience in absolute terms and the former speaks of submission to Allah's judgment—one being general and the other specific—the result of both must also be the same.

It is noteworthy that in the latter verse, three attributes are mentioned for the "Fa'izun": obedience to Allah and the Prophet, awe (khashyah), and Taqwa (God-consciousness). Some commentators have said that obedience is a general concept, awe is its internal branch, and Taqwa is its external branch. Thus, it first speaks of obedience generally, then of its internal and external dimensions.

It is also necessary to mention that in a narration regarding the interpretation of the phrase "And those are the successful ones" (وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ), it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام: "The meaning of the verse refers to the Commander of the Faithful (Ali) عليه السلام" [2]. Undoubtedly, Ali عليه السلام is the most prominent manifestation of the verse, and this is the intent of the narration; it never negates the generality of the concept.

The tone of the next verse—as well as the circumstances of revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) mentioned in some commentaries—indicates that a group of hypocrites, after the revelation of the previous verses and their severe reproaches, became deeply distressed by their situation. They came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and swore solemn oaths that they were submissive to his command. The Quran responded decisively, saying: "And they swear by Allah their strongest oaths that if you ordered them, they would surely go forth. Say, 'Do not swear. [Your] obedience is [already] known. Indeed, Allah is Acquainted with what you do'" (وَ أَقْسَمُوا بِاللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ لَئِنْ أَمَرْتَهُمْ لَيَخْرُجُنَّ قُلْ لا تُقْسِمُوا طاعَةٌ مَعْرُوفَةٌ إِنَّ اللَّهَ خَبِيرٌ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ).

Many commentators have interpreted "going forth" (khuruj) in the phrase "layakhrujun" as going out for Jihad, while others have interpreted it as leaving behind their wealth and lives, or accompanying the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله wherever he goes and being at his service.

Certainly, the word "khuruj" or its derivatives in the Holy Quran appears both in the sense of going out to the battlefield of Jihad and in the sense of abandoning home, life, and homeland. Given the context of the previous verses, which spoke of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله judgment in disputed matters, the second interpretation is more fitting—meaning they came to express submission to the Prophet's judgments and swore that leaving a portion of their wealth is easy, and if he commanded, they would abandon their entire lives. Nevertheless, there is no obstacle to combining both meanings: that they are ready to abandon wealth and life for his command, and ready to rush to the battlefield with their lives in their hands.

However, since hypocrites sometimes change their appearance when facing unfavorable social atmospheres and resort to heavy and solemn oaths—where sometimes their very oath is evidence of their lie—the Quran explicitly answered them that oaths are unnecessary; show it through action. Allah is aware of the depths of your hearts; He knows whether you lie in this oath or if you are truly changing direction.

Therefore, in the next verse, which is the final verse under discussion, it emphasizes this meaning again and says: "Say, 'Obey Allah and obey the Messenger'" (قُلْ أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ).

Then it adds that regarding this command, there are only two possibilities: "But if you turn away, then upon him is only that with which he has been charged, and upon you is that with which you have been charged" (فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَإِنَّما عَلَيْهِ ما حُمِّلَ وَ عَلَيْكُمْ ما حُمِّلْتُمْ). (He has performed his duty, and your duty is sincere obedience).

"And if you obey him, you will be [rightly] guided" (وَ إِنْ تُطِيعُوهُ تَهْتَدُوا), for he is a leader who invites to nothing but the way of Allah, truth, and correctness.

"And there is not upon the Messenger except clear notification" (وَ ما عَلَى الرَّسُولِ إِلَّا الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ).

He is duty-bound to convey Allah's command clearly to everyone, whether they accept it or not. The benefit or harm of accepting or rejecting this call will return to them, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is never responsible for forcing people into guidance or acceptance.

It is interesting that responsibilities in the above verse are referred to as a "burden" (haml). Indeed, both the duty of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mission and the sincere obedience to his call are burdens upon the shoulders that must be carried to the destination, and none but the sincere have the strength to carry them. Thus, in a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام describing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, he quotes the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as saying:

"O assembly of Quran reciters! Fear Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, regarding what He has burdened you with of His Book, for I am responsible and you are responsible: I am responsible for conveying the message, and as for you, you will be asked about what you were burdened with of the Book of Allah and my Sunnah."

[1]: The word "yattaqhi," which is read with a silent 'qaf' and a broken 'ha', was originally "yattaqihi." Because it is part of a conditional clause, the 'ya' was dropped due to the jussive state, and because two consecutive 'kasras' were heavy, one was removed, resulting in the aforementioned form. [2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 616.

Ayah

وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ كَمَا ٱسْتَخْلَفَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ وَلَيُمَكِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ دِينَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى ٱرْتَضَىٰ لَهُمْ وَلَيُبَدِّلَنَّهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ خَوْفِهِمْ أَمْنًا ۚ يَعْبُدُونَنِى لَا يُشْرِكُونَ بِى شَيْـًٔا ۚ وَمَن كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٥٥)
Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth, just as He made those who were before them successors, and He will surely establish for them their religion which He has approved for them, and that He will surely change their state to security after their fear, while they worship Me, not ascribing any partners to Me. Whoever is ungrateful after that—it is they who are the transgressors. (55)

Occasion of Revelation

Many commentators, including "al-Suyuti" in Asbab al-Nuzul, "Tabarsi" in Majma' al-Bayan, Sayyid Qutb in Fi Zilal, and "al-Qurtubi" in his Tafsir (with slight variations), have narrated the following regarding the occasion of the revelation of this verse: "When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims migrated to Medina and the Ansar welcomed them with open arms, all the Arabs rose up against them. It was such that they were forced to never lay down their weapons; they would sleep with their arms at night and rise with them in the morning (remaining in a state of constant readiness). This situation became difficult for the Muslims. Some spoke of this openly, asking: 'How long will this state continue? Will a time ever come when we can rest at night with a mind at ease, and security and tranquility prevail over us, fearing none but Allah?' Then the above verse was revealed, giving them the glad tidings that indeed, such a time shall come." [^1]

Commentary

The Global Governance of the Oppressed

Since the previous verses spoke of obedience and submission to the command of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the verse under discussion continues this theme and describes the result of this obedience, which is global governance. It states with emphasis:

"Allah has promised those who have believed among you and done righteous deeds that He will surely grant them succession [to authority] upon the earth just as He granted it to those before them" (وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ كَمَا اسْتَخْلَفَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ).

"And that He will surely establish for them [therein] their religion which He has preferred for them" (وَ لَيُمَكِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ دِينَهُمُ الَّذِي ارْتَضى‌ لَهُمْ).

"And that He will surely substitute for them, after their fear, security" (وَ لَيُبَدِّلَنَّهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ خَوْفِهِمْ أَمْناً).

"And it shall be such that they worship Me alone and do not associate anything with Me" (يَعْبُدُونَنِي لا يُشْرِكُونَ بِي شَيْئاً).

It is certain that after this dominance of the government of Monotheism (Tawhid), the establishment of the Divine religion, and the disappearance of all anxiety, insecurity, and polytheism, "whoever disbelieves after that—then those are the truly defiant/disobedient" (وَ مَنْ كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ).

In any case, it appears from the entirety of the verse that Allah has given three promises to the group of Muslims who possess these two attributes—"Faith" and "Righteous Deeds":

1- Succession and governance on the earth. 2- The fundamental and deep-rooted spread of the true religion everywhere (derived from the word "Tamkin" - establishment). 3- The disappearance of all causes of fear, terror, and insecurity.

The result of these matters will be that they worship Allah with ultimate freedom, submit to His commands, and attribute no partner or likeness to Him, spreading pure Monotheism everywhere.

Of course, in the key points mentioned below, it will become clear when this Divine promise was fulfilled or when it will be fulfilled.

Key Points

1- Commentary on the phrase "كَمَا اسْتَخْلَفَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ" (Just as He granted it to those before them).

There is discussion among commentators as to which individuals this phrase refers to—those who held succession on earth before the Muslims:

Some consider it a reference to Adam, David (Dawud), and Solomon (Sulayman), because the Quran says regarding "Adam" in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 30: إِنِّي جاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً: "Indeed, I will make upon the earth a successive authority."

And in Surah Sad, verse 26, regarding "David," He says: يا داوُدُ إِنَّا جَعَلْناكَ خَلِيفَةً فِي الْأَرْضِ: "O David, indeed We have made you a successor upon the earth."

And since "Solomon," according to verse 16 of Surah Al-Naml, was the heir to David's government, he became a successor on the earth.

However, others, such as the eminent commentator "Allameh Tabataba'i" in Al-Mizan, consider this meaning unlikely, as he deems the expression الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ (those before them) unsuitable for Prophets, since this phrasing is not used for Prophets in the Quran. Therefore, he considers it a reference to previous nations who possessed faith and righteous deeds and attained governance on the earth.

Yet another group believes this verse refers to the "Children of Israel" (Bani Israel), because with the appearance of Moses عليه السلام and the breaking of the power of Pharaoh and his people, they became the masters of governance on the earth, as the Quran states in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 127: وَ أَوْرَثْنَا الْقَوْمَ الَّذِينَ كانُوا يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشارِقَ الْأَرْضِ وَ مَغارِبهَا الَّتِي بارَكْنا فِيها: "And We caused the people who had been oppressed to inherit the eastern regions of the land and the western ones, which We had blessed."

And He also says regarding them: وَ نُمَكِّنَ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ: "And [We intended] to establish them in the land" (and make them influential and dominant).

It is true that among the Children of Israel, even in the era of Moses عليه السلام, there were unworthy, defiant, and even occasionally disbelieving individuals, but regardless, the government was in the hands of the righteous believers (thus, the objection raised by some commentators to this interpretation is dismissed by this explanation). The third interpretation seems closer to the truth.

2- To whom does this Divine promise belong?

We read in the verse that Allah has promised the governance of the earth, the establishment of the religion, and complete security to a group that has faith and whose deeds are righteous. But as for who this group refers to in terms of specific instances, there is again discussion among commentators:

Some have restricted it to the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله who, with the victory of Islam in the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, became the masters of governance on the earth (of course, "earth" here does not necessarily mean the entire globe, but a concept that applies to both the part and the whole).

Others refer to the government of the first four Caliphs.

Some have considered its meaning so broad that this promise includes all Muslims who possess these attributes.

And a group considers it a reference to the government of al-Mahdi عليه السلام, under whose banner the East and West of the world will fall, the true religion will penetrate everywhere, insecurity, fear, and war will be removed from the face of the earth, and worship free from polytheism will be realized for all inhabitants of the world.

Undoubtedly, the verse includes the early Muslims, and undoubtedly the government of al-Mahdi عليه السلام—which, according to the belief of all Muslims, both Shia and Sunni, will fill the entire earth with justice and equity after it has been filled with oppression and tyranny—is the most perfect manifestation of this verse. However, this does not prevent the generality and breadth of the verse's meaning.

The conclusion is that in any era and time when the foundations of faith and righteous deeds are strengthened among Muslims, they will become the masters of a deep-rooted and influential government.

As for those who say the word "earth" (al-ard) is absolute and includes the entire globe, and thus exclusively pertains to the government of al-Mahdi (may our souls be sacrificed for him), this is not consistent with the phrase "كَمَا اسْتَخْلَفَ..." (just as He granted it to those before them), because the succession and governance of those before certainly did not encompass the entire expanse of the earth.

Furthermore, the occasion of the verse's revelation also shows that at least a sample of this government was achieved for the Muslims in the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (albeit toward the end of his life).

But we repeat again that the fruit of all the labors of the Prophets, their continuous and persistent preaching, and the most complete example of the sovereignty of Monotheism, complete security, and worship free from polytheism will be realized when al-Mahdi, that progeny of the Prophets and son of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, appears—the one about whom all Muslims have narrated this Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"If there were to remain of time but a single day, Allah would prolong that day until He sends a man from my progeny, whose name is my name, who will fill the earth with justice and equity as it had been filled with oppression and tyranny." [^1]

Interestingly, the late Tabarsi says under this verse: This Hadith has been narrated from the Ahl al-Bayt of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"It is regarding al-Mahdi from the family of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله." [^2]

In the Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani and many Shia commentaries, it is narrated from Imam al-Sajjad عليه السلام that in interpreting the verse he said:

"By Allah, they are our followers (Shia), the Ahl al-Bayt. Allah will do that for them by the hand of a man from us, and he is the Mahdi of this Ummah, who will fill the earth with justice and equity as it had been filled with oppression and tyranny, and he is the one about whom the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'If there were to remain of time but a single day...'"

As we have said, these interpretations do not mean the verse's meaning is restricted, but rather they describe the most perfect instance. However, since some commentators like "al-Alusi" in Ruh al-Ma'ani did not pay attention to this point, they rejected these Hadiths.

"Al-Qurtubi," the famous Sunni commentator, narrates from "Miqdad ibn al-Aswad" that he heard the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله say:

"There shall not remain on the face of the earth a house of stone or mud but that Allah will cause the word of Islam to enter it" (and faith and Monotheism will penetrate throughout the entire earth). [^1]

For further explanation regarding the government of al-Mahdi عليه السلام and its detailed and reasoned documentation in the books of Sunni and Shia scholars, refer to Volume 7 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 372 to 389, under verse 33 of Surah At-Tawbah.

3- The Ultimate Goal is Worship Free from Polytheism

The phrase "يَعْبُدُونَنِي لا يُشْرِكُونَ بِي شَيْئاً" (they worship Me, not associating anything with Me), whether it is grammatically a "circumstantial clause" (hal) or a "finality" (ghayah), [^2] its meaning is that the ultimate goal of the establishment of a just government, the rooting of the true religion, and the spread of peace and tranquility is the strengthening of the foundations of servitude and Monotheism, which is mentioned in another verse of the Quran as the purpose of creation:

وَ ما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَ الْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ: "And I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me" (Az-Zariyat: 56). A worship that is a sublime school for the education of humans and the nurturer of their souls and spirits; a worship of which Allah is independent, but which servants desperately need in order to traverse the path of perfection and progress.

Therefore, in the Islamic vision—unlike materialist visions whose ultimate goal is welfare and the enjoyment of a high-level material life—such a thing is never the primary goal. Rather, even material life is valuable only if it becomes a means to attain that spiritual goal.

However, it is necessary to note that worship free from any polytheism and the negation of any law other than Allah's and the sovereignty of whims is not possible except through the establishment of a government of the righteous.

It may be possible to draw a group toward the truth through education and continuous preaching, but the generalization of this matter in human society is not possible except through the establishment of the government of the faithful righteous. For this reason, the great Prophets directed their efforts toward forming such a government, especially the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, who at the first possible opportunity—namely upon the migration to Medina—took action to form a model of this government.

From this, it can also be concluded that such a government directs all its efforts—from war and peace to educational, cultural, economic, and military programs—toward the path of servitude to Allah, a servitude free from any form of polytheism.

It is also necessary to mention that the meaning of the government of the righteous, the establishment of the true religion, and worship free from polytheism does not mean that in such a society no sinner or deviant person will exist. Rather, the concept is that the system of government is in the hands of the righteous believers, and the general face of society is free from polytheism; otherwise, as long as man possesses free will, it is possible for deviant individuals to occasionally exist even in the best of Divine and human societies (take note).

[^1]: In the book Muntakhab al-Athar, one hundred and twenty-three Hadiths with this theme are narrated from various Islamic sources, especially from Sunni sources. Refer to page 247 onwards of that book. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed. [^1]: Al-Qurtubi, Volume 7, page 4292. [^2]: In the first case, the sentence is a circumstantial clause for the pronoun "them" mentioned in the previous verses. In the second case, the "lam" is implied, and it was originally "li-ya'budunani" (some have also suggested it is an introductory sentence, but this is weak).

Ayahs

وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (٥٦)
Maintain the prayer and give the zakat, and obey the Apostle so that you may receive [Allah’s] mercy. (56)
لَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ وَلَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٥٧)
Do not suppose that those who are faithless can frustrate [Allah] on the earth. Their refuge shall be the Fire, and it is surely an evil destination. (57)

Commentary

Escape from the Grip of His Punishment is Impossible!

In the previous verse, the promise of vicegerency on earth was given to the righteous believers. These two verses mobilize the people to prepare the prerequisites for this government, while simultaneously guaranteeing the removal of major obstacles. In reality, one of these two verses seeks to explain the necessary requirements, while the second verse addresses the negation of obstacles.

First, it says: "And establish prayer" (وَ أَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ).

The very prayer that is the secret of the bond between the creation and the Creator, ensuring their continuous connection with Allah, and acting as a barrier between them and indecency and evil.

"And give zakat" (وَ آتُوا الزَّكاةَ).

The very zakat which is a sign of the bond with "Allah's creation," an effective means for reducing social gaps, and a cause for strengthening emotional ties.

And generally, "obey the Messenger" in all things (وَ أَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ).

An obedience that places you in the ranks of the righteous believers who are worthy of governing the earth.

"So that you may receive mercy" in the light of fulfilling these commands (لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ).

And become worthy of carrying the banner of the government of truth and justice.

And if you think that powerful and stubborn enemies might cast stones in this path and prevent the fulfillment of the divine promise, such a thing is not possible, for their power is insignificant compared to the power of Allah. Therefore, "Do not think that those who disbelieve can escape [Allah's punishment] on earth" (لا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ).

Not only are they not immune from Allah's punishment in this world, "but their abode is the Fire—and wretched indeed is the destination" (وَ مَأْواهُمُ النَّارُ وَ لَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ).

"Mu'jizin" (معجزين) is the plural of "Mu'jiz" (معجز) from the root "I'jaz" (اعجاز), meaning to render someone powerless or unable. Since sometimes a person pursues someone who escapes from his hand, and no matter how much he tries, he cannot reach him and the person exits the realm of his power—thereby rendering the pursuer powerless—the word "Mu'jiz" is sometimes used in this sense. The above verse also refers to this meaning, and its concept is that you cannot exit the realm of Allah's power.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لِيَسْتَـْٔذِنكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ وَٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَبْلُغُوا۟ ٱلْحُلُمَ مِنكُمْ ثَلَـٰثَ مَرَّٰتٍ ۚ مِّن قَبْلِ صَلَوٰةِ ٱلْفَجْرِ وَحِينَ تَضَعُونَ ثِيَابَكُم مِّنَ ٱلظَّهِيرَةِ وَمِنۢ بَعْدِ صَلَوٰةِ ٱلْعِشَآءِ ۚ ثَلَـٰثُ عَوْرَٰتٍ لَّكُمْ ۚ لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَا عَلَيْهِمْ جُنَاحٌۢ بَعْدَهُنَّ ۚ طَوَّٰفُونَ عَلَيْكُم بَعْضُكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٥٨)
O you who have faith! Your slaves and any of you who have not yet reached puberty should seek your permission three times: before the dawn prayer, and when you put off your garments at noon, and after the night prayer. These are three times of privacy for you. Apart from these, it is not sinful of you or them to frequent one another [freely]. Thus does Allah clarify the signs for you, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (58)
وَإِذَا بَلَغَ ٱلْأَطْفَـٰلُ مِنكُمُ ٱلْحُلُمَ فَلْيَسْتَـْٔذِنُوا۟ كَمَا ٱسْتَـْٔذَنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٥٩)
When your children reach puberty, let them ask permission [at all times] just as those [who grew up] before them asked permission. Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (59)
وَٱلْقَوَٰعِدُ مِنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ ٱلَّـٰتِى لَا يَرْجُونَ نِكَاحًا فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْهِنَّ جُنَاحٌ أَن يَضَعْنَ ثِيَابَهُنَّ غَيْرَ مُتَبَرِّجَـٰتٍۭ بِزِينَةٍ ۖ وَأَن يَسْتَعْفِفْنَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُنَّ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٦٠)
As for women advanced in years who do not expect to marry, there will be no sin upon them if they put off their cloaks, without displaying their adornment. But it is better for them to be modest, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (60)

Commentary

Etiquette for Entering the Private Quarters of Parents

As we have previously stated, the most important issue pursued in this Surah is the matter of public chastity and the struggle against all forms of sexual impurity, which has been examined from various dimensions. The verses under discussion address one of the matters related to this issue and describe its specifics: the requirement for children—both those who have reached puberty and those who have not—to obtain permission when entering rooms where men and their spouses might be in private.

First, it says: "O you who have believed, let those whom your right hands possess and those who have not [yet] reached puberty among you ask permission of you at three times" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لِيَسْتَأْذِنْكُمُ الَّذِينَ مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَانُكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ لَمْ يَبْلُغُوا الْحُلُمَ مِنْكُمْ ثَلَاثَ مَرَّاتٍ).

"Before the dawn prayer, and when you put aside your [outer] garments at noon, and after the Isha prayer" (مِنْ قَبْلِ صَلَاةِ الْفَجْرِ وَحِينَ تَضَعُونَ ثِيَابَكُمْ مِنَ الظَّهِيرَةِ وَمِنْ بَعْدِ صَلَاةِ الْعِشَاءِ).

"Zahira" (ظهيرة), as Raghib states in Al-Mufradat and Firuzabadi in Al-Qamus, means midday or around noon, a time when people usually take off their outer garments and sometimes a husband and wife retire for privacy.

"These three times are three times of privacy for you" (ثَلَاثُ عَوْرَاتٍ لَكُمْ).

The word "Awrah" (عورة) originally stems from the root "Ar" (عار), meaning "shame" or "defect." Since the exposure of private parts is a cause for shame and defect, it is referred to as Awrah in the Arabic language.

The word "Awrah" is sometimes used to mean a breach in a wall or a tear in a garment and the like, and sometimes it means a defect in general. In any case, the application of the word Awrah to these three times is because people during these periods do not strictly commit themselves to covering up as they do at other times and maintain a private state.

Naturally, this command is directed at the guardians of children to compel them to follow this program, as they have not yet reached the age of puberty to be subject to Divine obligations; for this reason, the addressees here are the guardians.

Furthermore, the general application of the verse includes both male and female children. The word "Al-ladhina" (الذين), which is for the masculine plural, does not prevent the universality of the verse's concept, as in many cases, this expression is used to encompass the whole by way of predominance (taghlib), just as in the verse regarding the obligation of fasting, the term "Al-ladhina" is used while referring to all Muslims (Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 183).

It is also necessary to mention that the verse speaks of children who have reached the age of discernment (tamyiz) and can distinguish sexual matters, the Awrah, and other such things. The command to seek permission is itself evidence that they understand enough to know what seeking permission means, and the expression "three times of privacy" (thallathu awrat) is another witness to this meaning.

As for whether this ruling regarding slaves is specific to male slaves or includes female slaves as well, various narrations have been recorded, although the literal meaning is general and includes both groups. For this reason, the narration consistent with the literal meaning can be preferred.

At the end of the verse, He says: "There is no blame upon you nor upon them beyond these [times], for they move about among you, some of you [serving] others" (لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَا عَلَيْهِمْ جُنَاحٌ بَعْدَهُنَّ طَوَّافُونَ عَلَيْكُمْ بَعْضُكُمْ عَلَى بَعْضٍ).

Indeed, "Thus does Allah make clear to you the verses; and Allah is Knowing and Wise" (كَذَلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمُ الْآيَاتِ وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ).

The word "Tawwafun" (طوافون) is originally from the root "Tawaf," meaning to go around something. Since it appears in the intensive form (mubalaghah), it signifies frequency in this action. Considering that it is followed by "some of you [serving] others," the meaning of the sentence becomes: outside of these three times, you are permitted to move around one another, come and go, and serve each other.

According to "Fadil Miqdad" in Kanz al-Irfan, this expression is effectively a statement of the reason for not requiring permission at other times, because if they were to come and go constantly and were required to seek permission every time, it would become difficult [1].

In the next verse, He states the ruling for those who have reached puberty, saying: "And when the children among you reach puberty, let them ask permission [at all times] as those before them have done" (وَإِذا بَلَغَ الْأَطْفالُ مِنْكُمُ الْحُلُمَ فَلْيَسْتَأْذِنُوا كَمَا اسْتَأْذَنَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ).

The word "Hulum" (حلم) has been interpreted as "intellect" and is a metaphor for puberty, which is usually accompanied by a leap in intellectual and mental maturity. Sometimes it is said that "Hulum" means "dreaming," and since young people, near puberty, see scenes in their dreams that cause nocturnal emission (ihtilam), this word is used metaphorically to mean puberty.

In any case, it is understood from the above verse that the ruling for those who have reached puberty differs from that of prepubescent children. Prepubescent children, according to the previous verse, are only obliged to seek permission at three times, because their lives are so intertwined with the lives of their parents that it would be difficult if they had to seek permission at all times. Furthermore, their sexual feelings have not yet fully awakened. However, mature youths, according to this verse which makes seeking permission mandatory for them in an absolute sense, are obliged to seek permission at all times when entering upon their parents.

This ruling is specific to the place where parents are resting; otherwise, entering a common room (if they have one), especially when others are present and there is no barrier or hindrance, does not require seeking permission.

[1]: Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 2, p. 225.

It is also necessary to note that the phrase "as those before them have done" (كَمَا اسْتَأْذَنَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ) refers to adults who were obliged to seek permission from parents at all times when entering a room. In this verse, individuals who have just reached puberty are placed in the same category as adults who were required to seek permission.

At the end of the verse, for further emphasis and attention, He says: "Thus does Allah make clear to you His verses; and Allah is Knowing and Wise" (كَذَلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ آيَاتِهِ وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ).

This is the same expression found at the end of the previous verse without any change, except that in that verse it was "the verses" (al-ayat) and here it is "His verses" (ayatihi), which does not differ much in meaning.

Of course, we will discuss the specifics of this ruling as well as its philosophy in the "Key Points" section.

In the last verse under discussion, He states an exception to the ruling on the hijab for women, exempting elderly and aged women from this ruling, saying: "And women past child-bearing who do not expect wedlock—there is no blame upon them for putting aside their [outer] garments, provided they do not make a display of their adornment" (وَالْقَوَاعِدُ مِنَ النِّسَاءِ اللَّاتِي لَا يَرْجُونَ نِكَاحًا فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْهِنَّ جُنَاحٌ أَنْ يَضَعْنَ ثِيَابَهُنَّ غَيْرَ مُتَبَرِّجَاتٍ بِزِينَةٍ).

In fact, there are two conditions for this exception:

First, they must reach an age where they usually have no hope of marriage, or in other words, they have completely lost sexual attraction.

Second, while removing the hijab, they must not adorn themselves.

It is clear that with these two conditions, the harms of uncovering the hijab will not exist in their case, and for this reason, Islam has lifted this ruling from them.

It is also clear that the intention is not to become naked or remove all clothes, but only to set aside outer garments, which some narrations have interpreted as the chador and headscarf (al-jilbab and al-khimar).

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding this verse, we read that he said: "It refers to the headscarf and the chador." The narrator says: I asked the Imam: "In front of whomever it may be?" He replied: "In front of whomever it may be, provided she does not display herself or her adornment" [1]. Other narrations with the same or similar meaning have been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام [2].

At the end of the verse, it adds that in all circumstances, "but to modestly refrain [from that] is better for them" (وَأَنْ يَسْتَعْفِفْنَ خَيْرٌ لَهُنَّ).

This is because, from the perspective of Islam, the more a woman observes the side of modesty and hijab, the more commendable it is and the closer it is to piety and purity.

And since some elderly women might abuse this calculated and legitimate freedom, and perhaps engage in inappropriate conversations with men, or both parties might harbor impure thoughts in their hearts, at the end of the verse, as a warning, He says: "And Allah is Hearing and Knowing" (وَاللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ).

He hears what you say and knows what you hold in your hearts or what you nurture in your minds.

[1]: Wasail al-Shia, Vol. 14, Book of Marriage, p. 147, Chapter 110. [2]: For further explanation, refer to the previous source.

Key Points

1- The Philosophy of Seeking Permission and the Harms of Neglecting It

To uproot a social vice such as acts contrary to chastity, merely resorting to the execution of legal punishments (hudud) and flogging the deviants is not enough. In no social issue will such an approach yield the desired result; rather, one must arrange a collection of intellectual and cultural education mixed with moral and emotional etiquette, as well as correct Islamic teachings, and the creation of a healthy social environment. Then, punishment should be considered as one factor alongside these factors.

For this reason, in this Surah al-Nur, which is in fact the Surah of Chastity, it begins with the punishment of flogging for adulterous men and women, and extends to other issues such as providing means for healthy marriage, observing Islamic hijab, forbidding lustful glances, prohibiting the accusation of individuals with moral impurity, and finally, children seeking permission when entering the private quarters of parents.

This shows that Islam has not neglected any of the subtleties related to this issue.

Servants are obliged to seek permission when entering a room where a husband and wife are present.

Mature children are also obliged not to enter at any time without permission. Even prepubescent children who are constantly with their parents should be taught not to enter without permission at least at three times (before the morning prayer, after the Isha prayer, and at noon when parents retire for rest).

This is a type of Islamic etiquette, although unfortunately, it is less observed today. Despite the Quran explicitly stating it in the above verses, it is rarely seen in writings, speeches, or the explanation of rulings that this Islamic ruling and its philosophy are discussed. It is not clear why this definitive ruling of the Quran has been neglected or ignored?! Although the literal appearance of the verse indicates the obligation of observing this ruling, even if we were to assume it is recommended (mustahabb), it should still be spoken of and its details discussed.

Contrary to what some simple-minded people think—that children do not understand these matters and servants do not pay attention to these subtleties—it has been proven that children (let alone adults) are extremely sensitive to this issue. Sometimes the negligence of parents and children encountering scenes they should not have seen becomes the source of moral deviations and sometimes psychological illnesses.

We ourselves have encountered individuals who, by their own admission, due to their parents' neglect of this matter and witnessing them in the state of sexual intercourse or its preliminaries, had reached a stage of sexual stimulation and psychological complexes such that intense hostility toward the parents—to the point of murder!—had developed in their hearts, and they themselves had perhaps gone to the brink of suicide! This is where the value and greatness of this Islamic ruling become apparent, as it anticipated fourteen centuries ago matters that today's scientists have only recently reached.

We also find it necessary here to advise parents to take these matters seriously and habituate their children to seeking permission to enter. They should also avoid other actions that cause stimulation in children, including a man and woman sleeping in a room where discerning children sleep, as much as possible, and know that these matters are extraordinarily influential in their destiny from an educational perspective.

Interestingly, in a hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said: "Beware of a man having intercourse with his wife while a child in the cradle is looking at them!" [1]

2- The Ruling of Hijab for Elderly Women

The principle of exempting this group from the ruling of hijab is not a matter of debate among Islamic scholars, as the Quran is vocal about it. However, there are discussions regarding its specifics, including:

There is discussion regarding the age of these women and at what point they fall under the ruling of "Qawa'id" (women past child-bearing). In some Islamic narrations, the term "Musinnah" (aged women) is used [2], while in others, the expression "sitting back from marriage" (qu'ud an al-nikah) is used [3]. However, a group of jurists and commentators have interpreted it to mean "the end of the menstrual cycle, reaching the stage of infertility, and the lack of desire from anyone to marry them" [4]. But it appears that all these expressions point to one reality:

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 103, p. 295. [2]: Wasail al-Shia, Vol. 14, Book of Marriage, Chapter 110, Hadith 4. [3]: Ibid, Hadith 5. [4]: Jawahir, Vol. 29, p. 85 and Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 2, p. 226.

That they reach an age where normally no one marries at that age, although it is possible for such women to marry in rare cases.

Also, regarding the amount of the body they are permitted to reveal, various expressions have appeared in Islamic hadiths, while the Quran has stated it in a general way, saying there is no harm if they put aside their garments, which clearly refers to outer garments.

In some narrations, in response to the question of which of their garments they can put aside, Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: "The Jilbab" (chador) [1], while in another narration, the expression "Jilbab and Khimar" is used (Khimar meaning headscarf) [2]. But it appears that these types of hadiths are not contradictory; the meaning is that there is no harm if they uncover their heads and do not cover their hair, neck, and face. Even in some hadiths and the words of jurists, the wrists have also been exempted, but beyond this amount, we have no evidence for its exemption.

In any case, all of this is provided they do not adorn themselves (غَيْرَ مُتَبَرِّجَاتٍ بِزِينَةٍ) and they must keep hidden their secret ornaments which are obligatory for others to cover as well. Also, they should not wear decorative clothing that attracts attention. In other words, they are permitted to go out without a chador and headscarf in simple clothing and without makeup.

However, despite all this, this is not an obligatory ruling; rather, if they observe the covering like other women, it is preferred, as is explicitly stated at the end of the above verse, because in any case, the possibility of a slip—even if rare—exists for such individuals as well.

[1]: Wasail al-Shia, Book of Marriage, Chapter 110, Hadith 1. [2]: Ibid, Hadith 2 and 4.

Ayah

لَّيْسَ عَلَى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْأَعْرَجِ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْمَرِيضِ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَن تَأْكُلُوا۟ مِنۢ بُيُوتِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ ءَابَآئِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ إِخْوَٰنِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ أَخَوَٰتِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ أَعْمَـٰمِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ عَمَّـٰتِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ أَخْوَٰلِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ خَـٰلَـٰتِكُمْ أَوْ مَا مَلَكْتُم مَّفَاتِحَهُۥٓ أَوْ صَدِيقِكُمْ ۚ لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَأْكُلُوا۟ جَمِيعًا أَوْ أَشْتَاتًا ۚ فَإِذَا دَخَلْتُم بُيُوتًا فَسَلِّمُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ تَحِيَّةً مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ مُبَـٰرَكَةً طَيِّبَةً ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٦١)
There is no blame upon the blind, nor any blame upon the lame, nor any blame upon the sick, nor upon yourselves if you eat from your own houses, or your fathers’ houses, or your mothers’ houses, or your brothers’ houses, or your sisters’ houses, or the houses of your paternal uncles, or the houses of your paternal aunts, or the houses of your maternal uncles, or the houses of your maternal aunts, or those whose keys are in your possession, or those of your friends. There will be no blame on you whether you eat together or separately. So when you enter houses, greet yourselves with a salutation from Allah, blessed and good. Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may exercise your reason. (61)

Commentary

Houses Where Eating is Permitted

Since the previous verses spoke of seeking permission to enter at specific times or generally when entering the private quarters of parents, the verse under discussion is actually an exception to this rule. It identifies a group who, under certain conditions, may enter the homes of relatives and the like without permission and even eat there without asking.

First, It says: "There is no blame upon the blind, nor any blame upon the lame, nor any blame upon the ill [if they eat with you]" (لَيْسَ عَلَى الْأَعْمى‌ حَرَجٌ وَ لا عَلَى الْأَعْرَجِ حَرَجٌ وَ لا عَلَى الْمَرِيضِ حَرَجٌ‌).

This is because, according to explicit narrations, the people of Medina, before accepting Islam, used to prevent the blind, the lame, and the sick from attending the dining spread and would not eat with them, as they felt a sense of aversion toward this.

Conversely, after the emergence of Islam, some people would provide food for such individuals separately—not because they disliked eating with them, but because they feared that a blind person might not see the good portions of food while they themselves could see and eat them, which they considered contrary to ethical conduct. Similarly, for the lame and the sick, they worried these individuals might lag behind in eating while healthy people finished quickly.

For whatever reason, they avoided eating with them, and consequently, the blind, the lame, and the sick also withdrew themselves, fearing they might cause discomfort to others, and they considered this act a sin for themselves.

When the Prophet of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله was questioned about this matter, the above verse was revealed and stated: There is no obstacle to them eating with you [^1]. Of course, commentators have mentioned other interpretations for this sentence, including that the verse refers to the exemption of these three groups from the duty of Jihad.

Or that it means you are permitted to take such disabled individuals with you to the eleven types of houses mentioned in the following part of the verse, and they too may eat from their food.

However, these two interpretations seem very unlikely and are not consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse (reflect on this).

Then the Holy Quran adds: "Nor is there any blame upon yourselves if you eat from these houses [without seeking permission]: your own houses" (referring to the houses of children or spouses, which are expressed as one's own house) (وَ لا عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَنْ تَأْكُلُوا مِنْ بُيُوتِكُمْ‌).

"Or the houses of your fathers" (أَوْ بُيُوتِ آبائِكُمْ‌).

"Or the houses of your mothers" (أَوْ بُيُوتِ أُمَّهاتِكُمْ‌).

"Or the houses of your brothers" (أَوْ بُيُوتِ إِخْوانِكُمْ‌).

"Or the houses of your sisters" (أَوْ بُيُوتِ أَخَواتِكُمْ‌).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur and also Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn under the verse discussed. Other commentators have also cited this narration, such as Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, the late Fayd in Tafsir al-Safi, Fakhr Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir, and Sheikh Tusi in al-Tibyan.

"Or the houses of your paternal uncles" (أَوْ بُيُوتِ أَعْمامِكُمْ‌).

"Or the houses of your paternal aunts" (أَوْ بُيُوتِ عَمَّاتِكُمْ‌).

"Or the houses of your maternal uncles" (أَوْ بُيُوتِ أَخْوالِكُمْ‌).

"Or the houses of your maternal aunts" (أَوْ بُيُوتِ خالاتِكُمْ‌).

"Or those whose keys you possess" (أَوْ ما مَلَكْتُمْ مَفاتِحَهُ‌).

"Or the houses of your friends" (أَوْ صَدِيقِكُمْ‌).

Of course, this ruling has conditions and explanations that will follow after the completion of the verse's commentary.

Then It continues: "There is no blame upon you whether you eat together or separately" (لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ أَنْ تَأْكُلُوا جَمِيعاً أَوْ أَشْتاتاً).

It seems a group of Muslims at the beginning of Islam refrained from eating alone, and if they could not find someone to eat with, they would remain hungry for a while. The Quran teaches them that eating both collectively and individually is permitted [^1]. Some have also said that a group of Arabs felt obligated to take a guest's food separately as a sign of respect and would not eat with him (lest he feel shy or restricted); the verse removed these constraints and taught that this is not a praiseworthy custom ^2. Others have said that some were committed to the idea that the wealthy should not eat with the poor, maintaining class distinctions even at the table; the Quran negated this false and oppressive custom with the above phrase [^3]. There is no obstacle to the verse referring to all these matters.

Then It points to another ethical instruction, saying:

"But when you enter houses, greet yourselves [one another] with a greeting from Allah, blessed and good" (فَإِذا دَخَلْتُمْ بُيُوتاً فَسَلِّمُوا عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِكُمْ تَحِيَّةً مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ مُبارَكَةً طَيِّبَةً).

And finally, It concludes the verse with this sentence: "Thus does Allah make clear to you the verses [of revelation] that you may perhaps use reason" (كَذلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمُ الْآياتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

Regarding what is meant by these "houses" (buyut), some commentators consider it a reference to the eleven types of houses mentioned above.

Others have considered it specific to mosques.

However, it is evident that the verse is general and includes all houses, whether the eleven types that a person enters to consume food, or others such as the houses of friends, relatives, or strangers, as there is no evidence to restrict the broad concept of the verse.

As for what is meant by "greeting yourselves," several interpretations are found here:

Some have taken it to mean greeting one another, just as we read in the story of the Children of Israel (Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 54): "So kill yourselves" (فَاقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسِكُمْ‌), meaning "some of you must kill others as a punishment."

Some commentators have interpreted it as greeting one's spouse, children, and family, as they are like one's own self, hence the expression "yourselves" (anfusikum). This expression is also seen in the Verse of Mubahala (Surah Al-Imran, Verse 61), indicating that sometimes the extreme closeness of one person to another causes them to be referred to as "self" (nafs), just as the closeness of Ali عليه السلام to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله led to this expression.

[^1], ^2, [^3]: Tafsir al-Tibyan under the verse discussed.

Some commentators also consider the above verse to refer to houses where no one resides; upon entering, a person greets himself with the phrase: "Peace be upon us from our Lord" (Al-salamu 'alayna min qibali rabbina) or "Peace be upon us and upon the righteous servants of Allah" (Al-salamu 'alayna wa 'ala 'ibadillah al-salihin).

We believe there is no contradiction between these interpretations. Upon entering any house, one should offer a greeting: believers to one another, family members to one another, and if no one is present, greeting oneself, as all of these ultimately return to greeting oneself.

Thus, in a narration from Imam Baqir عليه السلام, we read that when he was asked about the interpretation of this verse, he replied: "It is a man's greeting to the people of the house when he enters, and then they return it to him; thus it is your greeting upon yourselves" [^1]. And we also read from the same Imam عليه السلام that he said: "When one of you enters his house, if there is someone in it, let him greet them; and if there is no one in it, let him say: 'Peace be upon us from our Lord,' as Allah Almighty says: 'A greeting from Allah, blessed and good'" ^2.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 627.

Key Points

1. Is eating the food of others not conditional upon their permission?
