As we saw in the above verse, Allah has permitted a person to eat from the houses of close relatives and certain friends and the like—totaling eleven cases—and the verse did not make seeking permission a condition. Certainly, it is not conditional upon explicit permission, because with permission, one can eat anyone's food, and these eleven groups would have no special distinction.

But is it a condition to ascertain internal satisfaction (so-called through circumstantial evidence) due to the special relationship and closeness between the parties?

The apparent generality of the verse negates this condition as well; it considers the mere probability of their satisfaction (which is usually present) to be sufficient.

However, if the situation between the parties has become such that there is certainty of a lack of satisfaction, although the verse is general in this regard, it is not unlikely that the verse excludes such a case. Especially since such individuals are rare, and general rulings usually do not encompass such rare instances.

Therefore, the above verse, within a specific scope, qualifies (takhsis) the verses and narrations that make disposing of others' property conditional upon ascertaining their satisfaction. But we repeat, this qualification is within a defined limit—that is, eating to the extent of need, free from extravagance and waste.

What is mentioned above is well-known among our jurists, and part of it is explicitly stated in narrations.

In a reliable narration from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that when he was asked about this verse—the phrase "or your friend"—he said: "By Allah, it means a man enters the house of his friend and eats without his permission" [^1]. Several other narrations have been narrated with the same meaning, stating that seeking permission in these cases is not a condition (of course, there is no disagreement among jurists that it is not permissible with an explicit prohibition or knowledge of dislike, and the verse is diverted from such cases).

Regarding "not causing damage" (and not being extravagant), it has also been explicitly mentioned in some narrations ^2. The only thing remaining here is that we read in a narration related to this chapter that only specific food items can be used, not just any food. However, since this narration is disregarded by the jurists, its chain of transmission is not considered authoritative.

Some other jurists have exempted precious and high-quality foods that the homeowner may have stored for himself, an honored guest, or special occasions; this exemption does not seem unlikely based on the verse's diversion from such a scenario [^3].

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 16, p. 434, Book of Foods and Drinks, Chapters on Table Etiquette, Chapter 24, Hadith 1. ^2: Ibid, Hadith 4. [^3]: For further explanation, refer to Jawahir al-Kalam, Vol. 36, p. 406 (Book of Foods and Drinks).

2. The Philosophy of this Islamic Ruling

This Islamic ruling might raise questions when compared to the strict and firm laws regarding the prohibition of usurpation (ghasb) in Islamic programs: how could Islam, with all that precision and rigor regarding the disposal of others' property, permit such a thing?!

We believe this question is suited to 100% materialistic environments, such as the social environment of Westerners, who even turn their own children out of the house once they grow up a little! And they dismiss their parents when they are old and disabled! They are never ready to show gratitude and affection toward them, because all matters there revolve around material and economic relations, and there is no sign of human emotions!

But this issue is not at all surprising when considering Islamic culture and deep-rooted human emotions, especially regarding close relatives and special friends, which govern this culture.

In truth, Islam has considered close ties of kinship and friendship to be above these matters. This actually reflects the ultimate purity and sincerity that should prevail in Islamic society, where narrow-mindedness, exclusivism, and selfishness are cast aside.

Undoubtedly, the laws of usurpation apply outside this scope, but within this specific range, Islam has prioritized emotional matters and human bonds, and it is, in fact, a model for other relationships among relatives and friends.

3. Who is meant by "Friend"?

Undoubtedly, sincerity and friendship have a broad meaning, and what is meant here are certainly special and close friends who visit one another, and the relationship between them necessitates going to each other's homes and eating each other's food. Of course, as we noted in the core of the issue, in such cases, ascertaining satisfaction is not a condition; it is enough that there is no certainty of dissatisfaction.

Therefore, some commentators have said regarding this phrase: it means a friend who acts sincerely with you in his friendship. Others have said: it is a friend whose outward and inward are the same toward you. Apparently, all refer to the same point.

Furthermore, it becomes broadly clear from this expression that those who do not have such forbearance toward their friends are not truly friends! Here it is appropriate to hear the expansion of the concept of friendship and its comprehensive conditions as narrated in a hadith from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام: The Imam عليه السلام said:

"Friendship does not exist except with its boundaries. Whoever possesses these boundaries or some of them, attribute him to friendship; and whoever possesses none of them, do not attribute him to any degree of friendship: First: that his secret and his public [attitude] toward you be one. Second: that he sees your beauty as his beauty and your shame as his shame. Third: that neither position nor wealth changes his attitude toward you. Fourth: that he does not withhold from you anything within his capacity. Fifth—which encompasses all these traits: that he does not abandon you during calamities!" [^1]

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 467.

4. Interpretation of "Those whose keys you possess"

In some accounts of the occasions of revelation, it is mentioned that in the early days of Islam, when Muslims went to Jihad, they would sometimes give the keys of their houses to disabled individuals who were unable to join the Jihad, and even permitted them to eat from the food available in the house. However, those individuals would sometimes refrain from eating out of fear that it might be a sin.

According to this narration, this is what is meant by "those whose keys you possess" (houses whose keys you have become the owner of) ^2. It is also narrated from Ibn Abbas that it refers to a person's agent and representative regarding water, property, agriculture, and livestock, who is permitted to eat from the fruit of the orchard as needed and drink from the milk of the animals. Some have also interpreted it as the "storekeeper" who has the right to consume a little of the food supplies.

However, considering the other groups named in this verse, it appears that this phrase refers to those who entrust the keys of their house to another due to a close relationship and trust. The close bond between the two has placed them in the same rank as close relatives and friends, whether he was officially an agent or not.

And if we see in some narrations that this phrase is interpreted as an agent in charge of supervising someone's property, it is actually by way of mentioning an example and is not limited to it.

^2: Tafsir al-Qurtubi under the verse discussed (in Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 16, p. 436, Chapter 24 of the Chapters on the Table, a hadith with this meaning is also found).

5. Greeting and Salutation

"Salutation" (tahiyyah), as we have said before, originally comes from the root "life" (hayat), and it means praying for the health and life of another, whether this prayer is in the form of "Peace be upon you" (Salamun 'alaykum), "Peace be upon us" (Al-salamu 'alayna), or for example, "May Allah give you life" (Hayyak Allah). However, usually, any type of expression of affection that individuals show toward one another at the beginning of a meeting is called "tahiyyah."

The meaning of "a greeting from Allah, blessed and good" is to link the greeting in some way to God. That is, the intention behind "Peace be upon you" should be "May the peace of Allah be upon you" or "I ask Allah for your safety." For from the perspective of a monotheist, any kind of prayer ultimately returns to Allah and is requested from Him. Naturally, a prayer that is such is both full of blessing (mubarak) and pure (tayyibah).

(Regarding the greeting (Salam), its importance, and the obligation to respond to any form of salutation, we have discussed this in the fourth volume of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 41, under Verse 86 of Surah An-Nisa).

Ayahs

إِنَّمَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَإِذَا كَانُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرٍ جَامِعٍ لَّمْ يَذْهَبُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَسْتَـْٔذِنُوهُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَـْٔذِنُونَكَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۚ فَإِذَا ٱسْتَـْٔذَنُوكَ لِبَعْضِ شَأْنِهِمْ فَأْذَن لِّمَن شِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمُ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٦٢)
Indeed the faithful are those who have faith in Allah and His Apostle, and when they are with him in a collective affair, they do not leave until they have sought his permission. Indeed those who seek your permission are those who have faith in Allah and His Apostle. So when they seek your permission for some of theirs [private] work, give permission to whomever of them you wish and plead with Allah to forgive them. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (62)
لَّا تَجْعَلُوا۟ دُعَآءَ ٱلرَّسُولِ بَيْنَكُمْ كَدُعَآءِ بَعْضِكُم بَعْضًا ۚ قَدْ يَعْلَمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَسَلَّلُونَ مِنكُمْ لِوَاذًا ۚ فَلْيَحْذَرِ ٱلَّذِينَ يُخَالِفُونَ عَنْ أَمْرِهِۦٓ أَن تُصِيبَهُمْ فِتْنَةٌ أَوْ يُصِيبَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٦٣)
Do not consider the Apostle’s summons amongst you to be like your summoning one another. Allah certainly knows those of you who slip away shielding one another. Those who disobey his orders should beware lest an affliction should visit them or a painful punishment should befall them. (63)
أَلَآ إِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ قَدْ يَعْلَمُ مَآ أَنتُمْ عَلَيْهِ وَيَوْمَ يُرْجَعُونَ إِلَيْهِ فَيُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌۢ (٦٤)
Behold! To Allah indeed belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. He certainly knows your present state. The day they are brought back to Him He will inform them about what they have done, and Allah has knowledge of all things. (64)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the first verse under discussion, various occasions of revelation have been narrated:

In some narrations, we read that this verse was revealed concerning "Hanzalah ibn Abi 'Ayyash," who wished to get married on the very night before the Battle of Uhud broke out. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was busy consulting with his companions and allies about the war. Hanzalah came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and submitted that if the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would permit him, he would stay with his wife that night. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله granted him permission.

In the morning, he was in such a rush to participate in the program of Jihad that he did not manage to perform the ritual bath (ghusl). In that state, he entered the battlefield and ultimately drank the draught of martyrdom.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said regarding him: "I saw the angels washing Hanzalah between the heavens and the earth!" Therefore, after that, Hanzalah became known as "Ghasil al-Mala'ikah" (the one washed by the angels). [^1]

In another occasion of revelation, we read that the verse was revealed during the story of the Battle of the Trench (Khandaq). At that time, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the mass of Muslims were busily and rapidly digging the trench around Medina. A group of hypocrites were outwardly in their ranks but performed very little work; whenever they saw the Muslims were distracted, they would quietly return to their homes without seeking permission from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. However, when the true Muslims encountered a problem, they would come to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to ask for permission, and as soon as they finished their task, they would return and continue digging the trench so as not to fall behind in this virtuous and important work. The above verse censures the first group and praises the second group. [^2]

Commentary

Do Not Leave the Prophet Alone!

Regarding the connection of these verses with the preceding ones, some commentators, including the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan and the author of Tafsir Fi Zilal, have stated that since the previous verses discussed aspects of how individuals should socialize with friends and relatives, the verses under discussion address the manner in which Muslims should interact with their leader, the Prophet, and emphasize the necessity of discipline before him. This is so they may be obedient to the Prophet's command in all things and not separate from the congregation in important matters without necessity and without his permission.

It is also possible that since the necessity of obeying Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was mentioned a few verses prior, and one of the aspects of obedience is not to do anything without His permission and command, these verses speak to this matter.

In any case, the first verse states: "The true believers are only those who have believed in Allah and His Messenger, and when they are with him on a matter of common concern that requires the presence of the congregation, they do not leave until they have asked his permission" (إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ وَ إِذا كانُوا مَعَهُ عَلى‌ أَمْرٍ جامِعٍ لَمْ يَذْهَبُوا حَتَّى يَسْتَأْذِنُوهُ‌).

The term "amr jami'" (matter of common concern) refers to any important task that requires the gathering of people and necessitates their cooperation and assistance, whether it be an important matter of consultation, a topic regarding Jihad and struggle against the enemy, or Friday prayers under extraordinary circumstances, and the like. Therefore, if we see some commentators interpreting it specifically as consultation, or specifically the issue of Jihad, or specifically Friday or Eid prayers, it must be said that they have reflected only a part of the verse's meaning; the aforementioned occasions of revelation are also instances of this general rule.

In reality, this is a disciplinary instruction that no organized and cohesive group can afford to ignore, because in such moments, even the absence of one individual can be costly and damage the ultimate goal—especially if the head of the assembly is the Messenger of the Lord, the Prophet of God, and an influential spiritual leader.

It is also necessary to note that the purpose of seeking permission is not for everyone who has a task to simply take a formal permission and go about their business; rather, they must truly seek permission, meaning that if the leader determines that their absence is not harmful, He grants permission, and otherwise, they must stay and sometimes sacrifice their private affairs for the more important goal.

Hence, following this sentence, He adds: "Those who ask your permission, they are the ones who truly believe in Allah and His Messenger." Their faith is not merely with the tongue, but with their soul and heart they are obedient to your command (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَأْذِنُونَكَ أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ‌).

"In that case, whenever they ask your permission for some of their important affairs, grant permission to whomsoever of them you will (and deem appropriate)" (فَإِذَا اسْتَأْذَنُوكَ لِبَعْضِ شَأْنِهِمْ فَأْذَنْ لِمَنْ شِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ‌).

It is clear that such believers, considering they have gathered for an important matter, would never seek permission for a trivial task; the term "shanihim" (their affairs) in the verse refers to necessary and significant matters.

On the other hand, the Prophet's "will" does not mean He should grant permission without considering the aspects of the matter and the effects of the presence or absence of individuals; rather, this expression is used so that the leader's hand is open, and in any case where He feels the necessity of the individuals' presence, He may not grant them permission to leave.

The evidence for this is that in verse 43 of Surah At-Tawbah, Allah questions the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله for granting permission to some individuals, saying: عَفَا اللَّهُ عَنْكَ لِمَ أَذِنْتَ لَهُمْ حَتَّى يَتَبَيَّنَ لَكَ الَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا وَ تَعْلَمَ الْكاذِبِينَ‌: "Allah has forgiven you; why did you grant them permission before those who were truthful became clear to you and you knew the liars?!"

This verse shows that even the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله must be careful in granting permission to individuals, consider all aspects of the work, and He bears a Divine responsibility in this matter.

At the end of the verse, He says: when you grant them permission, "ask forgiveness for them from Allah, for Allah is Forgiving and Merciful" (وَ اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمُ اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

Here, the question arises: what is this seeking of forgiveness for? Are they still sinners despite seeking permission from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that they need forgiveness?!

This question can be answered in two ways: First, although they are authorized and permitted, they have ultimately prioritized their personal work over the collective work of the Muslims, and this is not devoid of a kind of "leaving the better option" (tark al-awla) [^1]; hence they need to seek forgiveness (similar to seeking forgiveness for a disliked/makruh act).

Furthermore, this expression shows that they should refrain from seeking permission as much as possible and practice self-sacrifice and altruism, for even after permission, their action is still a "leaving of the better," lest they use trivial events as an excuse to leave these important programs.

Secondly, because of their observance of etiquette before their leader, they are worthy of Divine grace, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله seeks forgiveness for them as a way of thanking them for this act. [^2] At the same time, these two answers are not contradictory, and both may be intended.

Of course, this important Islamic disciplinary instruction was not exclusive to the Prophet and his companions; rather, it is necessary to observe it before all Divine leaders and precursors, whether they be the Prophet, the Imam, or the scholars who are their successors, because the fate of the Muslims and the system of Islamic society is at stake. In fact, in addition to the command of the Holy Quran, intellect and logic also dictate it, for fundamentally no organization can remain standing without observing this principle, and correct management is impossible without it.

It is strange that some famous Sunni commentators have considered this verse as evidence for the permissibility of Ijtihad and delegating the ruling to the opinion of the Mujtahid. However, it goes without saying that the Ijtihad discussed in the fields of Principles (Usul) and Jurisprudence (Fiqh) pertains to the rulings of Sharia, not to external subjects. Ijtihad in subjects cannot be denied; every army commander, every head of an office, and every supervisor of a group has a respected opinion when making decisions on executive matters and external subjects. This is not evidence that one can perform Ijtihad in the general rulings of Sharia and, based on perceived expediency, legislate a ruling or negate one.

Then, He states another instruction in connection with the commands of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "Do not treat the calling of the Messenger among yourselves like the calling of one of you to another" (لا تَجْعَلُوا دُعاءَ الرَّسُولِ بَيْنَكُمْ كَدُعاءِ بَعْضِكُمْ بَعْضاً). When He calls you for a matter, it is certainly an important Divine and religious subject; you must treat it with importance and stand by it seriously. Do not take His calls lightly, for His command is the command of Allah and His call is the call of the Lord.

Then He continues: "Allah knows those of you who slip away from the Prophet's important programs by hiding behind others and fleeing one after another" (قَدْ يَعْلَمُ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ يَتَسَلَّلُونَ مِنْكُمْ لِواذاً).

"But let those who oppose His command beware lest a trial (fitnah) befall them or a painful punishment reach them" (فَلْيَحْذَرِ الَّذِينَ يُخالِفُونَ عَنْ أَمْرِهِ أَنْ تُصِيبَهُمْ فِتْنَةٌ أَوْ يُصِيبَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ‌).

"Yatasallalun" is from the root "tasallul," which originally means to pull something out (for example, it is said "salla al-sayfa min al-ghimd," meaning he drew the sword from the scabbard). Usually, those who flee from a place secretly or by smuggling themselves out are called "mutasallilun."

"Liwadha" is from "mulawadhah," meaning concealment, and here it refers to the action of those who hide themselves behind someone else or stand behind a wall and, so to speak, catch people off guard and flee. This was an act the hypocrites would perform when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله called the people for Jihad or another important matter.

The Holy Quran says: Even if this ugly hypocritical act of yours remains hidden from the people, it will never be hidden from Allah, and these oppositions of yours to the Prophet's command carry a painful punishment in this world and the Hereafter.

As for what is meant by "fitnah" here? Some commentators have interpreted it as killing, some as misguidance, some as the dominance of a tyrannical ruler, and finally, some as the affliction of hypocrisy that appears in the human heart.

It is also possible that fitnah refers to social turmoils, disorders, chaos, defeat, and other calamities that befall a society as a result of defying the leader's command.

In any case, fitnah has a broad meaning that includes all these matters and more, just as "painful punishment" may encompass the punishment of this world, the Hereafter, or both.

It is noteworthy that in the interpretation of the above verse, two other possibilities have been mentioned besides what we have said:

First: The meaning of "Do not treat the calling of the Messenger among yourselves like the calling of one of you to another" is that when you call the Prophet, call Him with the etiquette and respect worthy of His station, not like calling one another. For some who were not acquainted with Islamic etiquette would come to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and, in public or private, repeatedly say: "O Muhammad! O Muhammad!..." in a manner that was not worthy of a great heavenly leader. The goal is to call Him with expressions like "O Messenger of Allah" and "O Prophet of Allah" and with a reasonable and polite tone.

This interpretation has also appeared in some narrations, but considering the previous verse and the expressions at the end of this very verse—which speak of responding to the Prophet's call and not being absent from His presence without permission—this interpretation is not consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse, unless we say both meanings are intended and combine the first and second interpretations within the concept of the verse.

A third interpretation has also been narrated for the verse, which seems very weak, and that is: "Do not take the prayers and curses of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله like your own curses regarding one another" [^1], because His prayer and curse are based on a reckoning and a Divine program and will certainly be effective.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 14, p. 568

Since this interpretation has no harmony with the beginning and end of the verse and no narration has reached us regarding it, it is not acceptable.

It is also necessary to mention that the scholars of Principles (Usul) have derived from the phrase "Let those who oppose His command beware..." that the Prophet's commands indicate obligation (wujub), but this argument has certain objections that have been noted in the field of Usul.

The last verse under discussion, which is the "last verse of Surah An-Nur," is a subtle and meaningful reference to the Origin and the Return (Ma'ad), which is the motive for performing all Divine commands and is, in fact, the guarantor for the implementation of all commands and prohibitions, including the important ones mentioned throughout this Surah. It says: "Be aware that to Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth" (أَلا إِنَّ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌).

The God whose knowledge encompasses the entire world "knows that which you are upon" (your method, your actions, your belief, and your intention are all manifest to Him) (قَدْ يَعْلَمُ ما أَنْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ‌).

[^1]: The word "du'a" (call/prayer), if followed by the preposition "lam," means a prayer for someone, and if followed by "'ala," it means a curse against someone; if neither is present, it holds the possibility of both.

And all these matters are recorded on the page of His knowledge, "and the Day they are returned to Him, He will inform them of what they did" and will give them the result, whatever it may be (وَ يَوْمَ يُرْجَعُونَ إِلَيْهِ فَيُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما عَمِلُوا).

"And Allah is Knowing of all things" (وَ اللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَلِيمٌ‌).

It is noteworthy that in this verse, the knowledge of Allah regarding human actions is emphasized three times. This is because when a person feels that someone is constantly watching him, and not an atom of his hidden or manifest affairs is concealed from Him, this belief and conviction has an extraordinary educational effect on him and is a guarantor of human control against deviations and sins.

O Allah! Illuminate the "lamp" (misbah) of our hearts with the light of knowledge and faith, and strengthen the "niche" (mishkat) of our being to preserve faith, so that we may traverse the "straight path" of Your prophets toward Your pleasure and, as an instance of "neither of the East nor of the West," remain immune to any deviation under the shadow of Your grace. O Allah! Illuminate our eyes with the light of chastity, our hearts with the light of gnosis, our souls with the light of piety, and our entire being with the light of guidance, and keep us safe from wandering, negligence, and being caught in the clutches of Satan's whispers.

O Lord! Strengthen the foundations of the Islamic government of justice for the implementation of Your limits, and protect our society from falling into the trap of indecencies. إِنَّكَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ (Indeed, You are powerful over all things).

End of Surah An-Nur. End of Volume 14 of Tafsir Nemouneh. 27 Rabi' al-Awwal 1403, corresponding to 22/10/61 [Solar Hijri].
